### 2021063042000305_Accountdeaktivierung.md
# Ticket 2021063042000305

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: Account Deactivation

## Keywords
- Account deactivation
- Account validity
- HPC network access
- Account extension
- IDM portal

## Summary
A user reported being unable to connect to the HPC network despite their account showing as valid until the end of the day in the IDM portal. The user suspected the account had been deactivated prematurely.

## Root Cause
- The IDM portal's display of the account validity date (e.g., '30.06.') does not specify whether the account is valid until the start or end of the day.
- The account was deactivated at the beginning of the last day of its validity period.

## Solution
- The HPC Admin extended the account validity to the next day.
- The user was informed that it might take several hours for the account to be reactivated across all HPC systems.

## Lessons Learned
- The IDM portal should provide clearer information on account validity periods.
- Accounts are deactivated at the beginning of the last day of their validity period.
- Extending account validity may require several hours to propagate across all HPC systems.
- Users should be advised to apply for account extensions well in advance to avoid disruptions.

## Follow-up Actions
- Improve communication regarding account validity periods in the IDM portal.
- Ensure users are aware of the account deactivation policy.
- Provide clear instructions for applying for account extensions.
```
---

### 2023061542003373_Hochleistungsrechner%20und%20Berufungszusagen%20-%20%20W2-Professur%20Ghiringhelli.md
# Ticket 2023061542003373

 # HPC Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

## Keywords
- HPC (High-Performance Computing)
- Berufungsverhandlungen (Appointment Negotiations)
- FAU (Friedrich-Alexander-Universität Erlangen-Nürnberg)
- RRZE/NHR@FAU
- Tier3 Systems
- Rechenzeit (Compute Time)
- GPU (Graphics Processing Unit)
- AI Training
- NHR Rechenzeitanträge (NHR Compute Time Applications)
- Zoom Meeting

## General Learnings
- FAU centralizes HPC resources at RRZE/NHR@FAU.
- Tier3 systems are available for general use by FAU scientists.
- Rechenzeitgarantierte (guaranteed compute time) cannot be provided due to variable demand and funding.
- Financial participation is possible for additional compute resources.
- NHR and GCS centers offer alternative compute time application options.
- Early contact with NHR@FAU is recommended for detailed discussions.
- GPU availability and AI training requirements should be discussed with the Head of the Datacenter.

## Root Cause of the Problem
- A new W2 professor requires information on HPC availability, specifically regarding GPU resources for AI training.
- There is a lack of clear communication and specific details about the professor's needs and the university's plans for financial support.

## Solution
- The professor was advised to start with Tier3 systems and later transition to NHR if needed.
- A Zoom meeting was scheduled with the Head of the Datacenter to discuss specific requirements and strategies for GPU resources.
- The professor was informed about the application process for a test project through NHR to gain access to the systems.

## Additional Notes
- The HPC Admin provided a standard response regarding HPC resources and procedures for appointment negotiations.
- The professor was proactive in scheduling a meeting to discuss specific needs and strategies.
- The Head of the Datacenter offered a meeting to discuss accessibility to the systems and GPU infrastructure.
---

### 2023080542000131_Re%3A%20Help%20-%20broken%20local%20NAS%20-%20mpt4.md
# Ticket 2023080542000131

 ```markdown
# HPC-Support Ticket: Help - broken local NAS - mpt4

## Problem Description
- User's group has been using a NAS for data storage (200 TB).
- Simultaneous loss of 4 hard drives, NAS turned off.
- Need to offload accessible data (~180 TB) before resurrecting the old NAS.
- New NAS purchased but won't be operational until September.

## Root Cause
- Hardware failure: Loss of 4 hard drives in the NAS.

## Solution
- **Option 1: rsync**
  - Increase quota on `atuin` for one of the projects (b171dc/b188dc).
  - Use existing HPC account credentials.
  - Begin data transfer using `rsync`.

- **Option 2: NFS-mount of a "bigdatashare"**
  - Less preferred due to more administrative work.

## Key Points Learned
- **Data Transfer**: Network influence is minor; disk read/write speed is the main bottleneck.
- **Quota Management**: Increase quota on `atuin` for specific projects as needed.
- **Technical Contact**: Identify and communicate with the technical contact in the user's group.
- **Hardware Handling**: Avoid transporting the NAS to prevent further damage to the hard drives.

## Actions Taken
- HPC Admins provided guidance on data transfer options.
- Quota on `atuin` increased for the specified project.
- User began data transfer using `rsync`.

## Follow-up
- Monitor data transfer progress.
- Ensure data integrity and completeness.
- Assist with any further issues that arise during the transfer process.
```
---

### 2025010942000231_Concerns%20About%20Job%20Queue%20Times.md
# Ticket 2025010942000231

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: Concerns About Job Queue Times

## Keywords
- Job queue times
- Node availability
- Energy saving
- Cluster name
- Scheduling

## Root Cause
- Longer job queue times, especially for larger jobs.
- Insufficient node availability due to nodes being turned off for energy saving.

## Solution
- Re-enable additional nodes to reduce queue times.

## Lessons Learned
- Always include the cluster name in support requests for easier troubleshooting.
- Node availability can significantly impact job queue times.
- Energy-saving measures may need to be balanced with user needs.

## Actions Taken
- HPC Admins turned on additional nodes (m[1001-1056]) to reduce queue times.
- The issue was resolved, and no jobs were left in the queue.
```
---

### 2024043042000938_Kein%20Zugriff%20auf%20WORK.md
# Ticket 2024043042000938

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: Kein Zugriff auf WORK

## Keywords
- WORK Verzeichnis
- Zugriff hängt
- Job prolog
- Service-Disruptions
- High file server load

## Problem Description
- User cannot access their WORK directory (`/home/woody/mppm/mppm001h`).
- Commands like `ls` hang when trying to access the directory.
- A job started by the user received the message "Hung on job prolog" and then hung.

## Root Cause
- High file server load causing service disruptions on the cluster.

## Solution
- HPC Admins informed the user about ongoing service disruptions due to high file server load.
- Reference link provided: [Service Disruptions on Clusters due to High File Server Load](https://hpc.fau.de/2024/04/19/service-disruptions-on-clusters-due-to-high-file-server-load/)

## General Learnings
- High file server load can cause access issues to WORK directories.
- Service disruptions can lead to jobs hanging during the prolog phase.
- Users should be informed about ongoing service disruptions and provided with relevant links for more information.
```
---

### 42108240_HPC-Kennung%20Ingo%20Faber.md
# Ticket 42108240

 ```markdown
# HPC-Support Ticket: HPC-Kennung Ingo Faber

## Keywords
- HPC-Kennung
- Präfix
- GID
- Maschinenbau
- FH Coburg
- corz

## Summary
A user created an HPC identifier (com001) for Ingo Faber from FH Coburg. The prefix `com0` was used for the Maschinenbau department, and `corz` was used as the GID since `com0` was not available. The ticket was reviewed to ensure the correctness of the GID.

## Root Cause
The user needed to create an HPC identifier for a new user and had to use a different GID (corz) because the expected GID (com0) was not available.

## Solution
The HPC Admins confirmed that `corz` is a valid GID for FH Coburg in general. The identifier was approved as is.

## What Can Be Learned
- **GID Assignment**: When creating HPC identifiers, ensure the correct GID is used. If the expected GID is not available, use an appropriate alternative.
- **Communication**: Always confirm with HPC Admins if there is any uncertainty about the correctness of the GID or other identifiers.
- **Prefix Usage**: Understand the prefixes used for different departments and ensure they are applied correctly.

## Actions Taken
- The user created the identifier `com001` with the GID `corz`.
- HPC Admins confirmed the validity of the GID.
- The ticket was closed with the identifier approved.
```
---

### 2020111042001307_hpc%20account%20antrag.md
# Ticket 2020111042001307

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

## Subject: HPC Account Application

### Keywords:
- HPC Account Application
- IdM-Kennung
- HPC-Kennung
- HPC-Cafe
- Password Setup
- New User
- HPC Services

### Summary:
A user requests an HPC account for a colleague. The HPC admin processes the request, creates a new HPC-Kennung, and provides instructions for password setup and service initialization.

### Root Cause:
- User requests an HPC account for a colleague.

### Solution:
1. **Account Creation**:
   - HPC admin creates a new HPC-Kennung (iww1012h).
   - User is instructed to set a password for the new HPC-Kennung via IdM-Kennung at [www.idm.fau.de](http://www.idm.fau.de).

2. **Service Initialization**:
   - It may take up to 2 days for initial setup of HPC services.
   - Password changes can take several hours to be recognized on HPC systems.

3. **User Support**:
   - New users are encouraged to attend the HPC-Cafe for additional support.
   - HPC-Cafe details are available at [HPC-Cafe](https://hpc.fau.de/services/hpc-cafe/).

### General Learnings:
- **Account Application Process**: Understanding the steps involved in applying for and setting up a new HPC account.
- **Password Setup**: Importance of setting up passwords via IdM-Kennung and the delay in recognition.
- **Service Initialization**: Awareness of the time required for initial service setup on HPC servers.
- **User Support**: Availability of support resources like the HPC-Cafe for new users.
```
---

### 2023090542002781_atuin%20load%20-%20k103bf.md
# Ticket 2023090542002781

 ```markdown
# HPC-Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

## Subject: atuin load - k103bf

### Keywords:
- High I/O load
- atuin
- lustre
- File quota
- High watermark deletion
- Data migration
- Tar-balls

### Summary:
The user's jobs were causing high I/O load on the atuin system. The HPC Admin suggested moving some jobs to lustre to reduce the load. The user encountered issues with file quotas and high watermark deletion on lustre. The solution involved consolidating data files into tar-balls and moving them back to atuin for long-term storage.

### Root Cause:
- High I/O load on atuin due to user jobs.
- Excessive number of files exceeding quota on lustre.

### Solution:
- Consolidate data files into tar-balls.
- Move data back to atuin for long-term storage.
- Ensure proper file management to avoid quota issues.

### Lessons Learned:
- High I/O load can be mitigated by distributing jobs across different storage systems.
- Proper file management and consolidation can help avoid quota issues.
- Understanding the limitations and features of different storage systems (e.g., high watermark deletion on lustre) is crucial for efficient data management.

### Additional Notes:
- The user had a large number of files (8.5 million) on atuin, which contributed to the high I/O load.
- The HPC Admin noted that the quota enforcement on lustre was not properly configured, leading to quota overruns.
- The user was advised to keep data on atuin for long-term storage and analysis.
```
---

### 2018121442000956_Erreichbarkeit%20von%20Meggie.md
# Ticket 2018121442000956

 # HPC Support Ticket: Accessibility Issue with Meggie

## Keywords
- Remote Access
- SSH Connection
- Meggie
- cshpc
- Home Network
- Configuration

## Summary
A user reported being unable to log into Meggie from their home network, while access to cshpc was still functional. The user provided SSH debug information indicating a connection attempt to Meggie's IPv6 address.

## Root Cause
The exact root cause was not identified in the provided conversation. However, the issue seems to be related to the SSH connection to Meggie's IPv6 address from the user's home network.

## Solution
No solution was provided in the conversation. The HPC Admin mentioned they could not reproduce the issue.

## Learning Points
- **SSH Debug Information**: Useful for diagnosing connection issues.
- **Network Configuration**: Differences in network settings between home and institutional networks can affect accessibility.
- **IPv6 vs IPv4**: Ensure both IPv6 and IPv4 configurations are checked when troubleshooting remote access issues.

## Next Steps
- **Further Diagnostics**: Ask the user to provide more detailed network and SSH configuration information.
- **Reproduce Issue**: Attempt to reproduce the issue from a similar network environment.
- **Check Meggie Configuration**: Verify if there have been any recent changes to Meggie's configuration that might affect remote access.

## Additional Resources
- SSH Troubleshooting Guide
- Network Configuration Checklist
- Meggie Access Documentation
---

### 2022071142004558_Fritz%20script.md
# Ticket 2022071142004558

 # HPC Support Ticket: Fritz Script Issue

## Keywords
- GROMACS
- SLURM
- MPI
- `srun`
- `gmx_mpi`
- `mdrun`
- `grompp`
- Job script
- Restart simulation
- `.tpr` files

## Problem
- User's script for running MD simulations using GROMACS generates 14 `.tpr` files upon restart.
- Initial attempt to fix by removing `srun` from `grompp` command resulted in job failure with MPI error.

## Root Cause
- Incorrect usage of `srun` with `grompp` command in the job script.
- MPI initialization error due to missing `srun` parameters.

## Solution
- Modify the `grompp` command to use `srun -n 1 gmx_mpi grompp ...` to ensure proper MPI initialization.

## General Learnings
- Understand the difference between `mdrun` and `grompp` commands in GROMACS.
- Proper usage of `srun` with MPI commands in SLURM job scripts.
- Importance of checking job dependencies and resource allocations in job scripts.
- Handling restart simulations and managing output files in MD simulations.

## Ticket Conversation Summary
- User reported issue with job script generating multiple `.tpr` files.
- HPC Admin suggested removing `srun` from `grompp` command.
- User encountered MPI error after implementing the suggested change.
- HPC Admin provided the correct usage of `srun` with `grompp` command, which resolved the issue.

## Relevant Commands/Tools
- `sbatch`
- `srun`
- `gmx_mpi mdrun`
- `gmx_mpi grompp`
- `sed`
- `awk`
- `module load`
---

### 2024070142005027_ssh%20access%20extension%20for%20data%20transfer%20-%20a103bc11%20%26%20NHR-Abschlu%C3%83%C2%9Fberic.md
# Ticket 2024070142005027

 # HPC Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

## Keywords
- SSH access extension
- Data transfer
- Final simulation run
- LargeScale project
- Abschlussbericht (final report)
- Project completion
- Follow-up projects
- Account reactivation
- Project advisor
- Projekt-ID/Akronym
- Antragsteller (applicant)
- Genehm. Ressourcen (approved resources)
- Zeitraum (time period)
- DFG-Projekt (DFG project)
- Projektkategorie (project category)
- Project Advisor(s)
- Abschlussbericht (final report)
- Restart-Dateien (restart files)
- Systeme (systems)
- Zugriff (access)
- Antragsportal JARDS (application portal JARDS)

## General Learnings
- Users may request extensions for SSH access to transfer data after completing simulations.
- Extensions can be granted by HPC Admins, and it may take a few hours for accounts to be reactivated.
- LargeScale projects require a final report (Abschlussbericht) upon completion.
- Follow-up projects may be planned, and users should apply for new projects through the designated portal.
- Project data and accounts are deleted after project completion unless further access is requested.
- Communication with users regarding project status, extensions, and final reports is crucial for smooth operations.

## Root Cause of the Problem
- The user needed additional time to transfer data from a completed simulation run.
- The user had not submitted the final report (Abschlussbericht) for the completed LargeScale project.

## Solution
- HPC Admins extended the user's SSH access by 2 weeks to facilitate data transfer.
- The user was reminded to submit the final report and eventually provided it.
- The user was informed about the project's official closure and the deletion of project data and accounts.
- The user was advised to apply for new projects through the Antragsportal JARDS if further access to HPC systems is needed.
---

### 2021111242001981_Early-Alex%20%22Lukas%20Folle%22%20_%20iwi5044h.md
# Ticket 2021111242001981

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

## Keywords
- Early-access
- Alex cluster
- GPU-hours
- NHR project
- HPC Usage Report
- Multi-node training
- Data transfer
- Documentation
- Performance
- System stability
- Software availability

## General Learnings
- **Early-access phase**: Users are given early access to new HPC resources for testing and feedback.
- **Feedback collection**: HPC admins collect feedback on system stability, software availability, performance, and other aspects.
- **HPC Usage Report**: Users are required to submit a usage report detailing their project, methods, results, and outreach.
- **Multi-node training**: Users may need guidance on how to handle data access and command execution across multiple nodes.
- **Data transfer**: Slow data transfer between global storage and local SSD can be a bottleneck.
- **Documentation**: There is a need for better documentation on multi-node training and data access.

## Root Cause of Problems
- **Slow data transfer**: The limited access speed to global storage is a known issue, with NVMe storage servers ordered but delayed.
- **Lack of multi-node documentation**: Users need clear documentation on how to handle data access and command execution in multi-node setups.

## Solutions
- **Data transfer**: HPC admins are aware of the issue and have ordered NVMe storage servers to address it.
- **Multi-node documentation**: HPC admins acknowledge the need for better documentation and suggest using MPI or Nvidia's NCL for communication across nodes.

## Notes for Support Employees
- **Early-access feedback**: Always collect feedback on system stability, software, performance, and other aspects during early-access phases.
- **Usage reports**: Ensure users submit usage reports with the required structure and length.
- **Multi-node training**: Provide or improve documentation on handling data access and command execution in multi-node setups.
- **Data transfer**: Be aware of potential bottlenecks in data transfer and communicate ongoing efforts to address them.
```
---

- **Unaffected Clusters:** Emmy, Cshpc, Woody.

### 2016090142002265_Verlaengerung%20der%20Dienstleistungen%20%28gwgk01%29.md
# Ticket 2016090142002265

 # HPC Support Ticket: Service Extension Request

## Keywords
- Service Extension
- DFG-Projekt
- HPC-Kennung
- Email Request

## Summary
A user received an email indicating that some services would expire on 30.09. The user inquired whether a formal written application was required for an extension or if an email would suffice.

## Root Cause
- Expiration of HPC services associated with a DFG project.

## Solution
- The HPC Admin identified the user's HPC-Kennung (gwgk01) and extended the services until 11/17, as the project was associated with a DFG project with that duration.
- No formal written application was required; an email request was sufficient.

## General Learning
- Users can request service extensions via email for projects associated with specific funding durations.
- HPC Admins can extend services based on project timelines without requiring formal written applications.

## Actions Taken

## Follow-Up
- No further action required from the user.
- HPC Admin confirmed the extension and the user acknowledged the response.

### 2023080742001654_Verbindung%20mit%20fritz%20%C3%83%C2%BCber%20den%20Account%20k103bf18.md
# Ticket 2023080742001654

 # HPC Support Ticket Analysis

## Subject
Verbindung mit fritz über den Account k103bf18

## Keywords
- SSH Connection
- Permission Denied
- Public Key
- GSSAPI
- FAQ

## Problem Description
User unable to connect to `fritz.nhr.fau.de` via SSH, receiving a "Permission denied" error related to public key and GSSAPI.

## Root Cause
The user does not have the appropriate private key on the `cshpc` machine to authenticate the SSH connection to `fritz.nhr.fau.de`.

## Solution
- Refer to the relevant FAQ entry for setting up SSH keys: [FAQ Link](https://hpc.fau.de/faqs/#innerID-13183)
- Ensure the private key used for SSH authentication is correctly configured on the `cshpc` machine.

## General Learning
- SSH connection issues often stem from misconfigured or missing private keys.
- FAQs and documentation are valuable resources for troubleshooting common issues.
- Proper key management is crucial for secure and seamless SSH access.

## Roles Involved
- **HPC Admins**: Provided guidance and referred to relevant FAQ.
- **User**: Reported the issue and followed the provided solution.

## Conclusion
Proper configuration of SSH keys is essential for accessing HPC resources. Users should refer to documentation and FAQs for common troubleshooting steps.

### 2024121842001132_Faster%20computation%3F.md
# Ticket 2024121842001132

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: Faster Computation?

## Subject
- **User Issue:** Long computation time for WRF simulations on Fritz Lustre.
- **Goal:** Optimize computational resources for faster simulations.

## User's Initial Setup
- **Batch Script:**
  ```bash
  #!/bin/bash -l
  #SBATCH --job-name=wrf_run8_noice
  #SBATCH --nodes=1
  #SBATCH --ntasks-per-node=72
  #SBATCH --time=24:00:00
  #SBATCH --cpu-freq=high-high:performance
  unset SLURM_EXPORT_ENV
  srun ./wrf.exe >& log.wrf
  ```

## HPC Admin's Recommendations
- **Benchmarking:**
  - Run three tests to determine scaling:
    1. Default run with 72 cores.
    2. `#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node=36` and `#SBATCH --cores-per-socket=36`.
    3. `#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node=36` and `#SBATCH --cores-per-socket=18`.

## User's Findings
- **Test Results:**
  - Default run (72 cores) was faster than the other two tests.
  - Tests with 36 cores took significantly longer.

## HPC Admin's Analysis
- **CPU Load Issue:**
  - High CPU load due to potential OpenMP compilation.
  - Recommended adding:
    ```bash
    #SBATCH --cpus-per-task=1
    export OMP_NUM_THREADS=1
    export SRUN_CPUS_PER_TASK=1
    ```

## Final Recommendation
- **Scaling:**
  - Application does not scale well to 72 cores but is acceptable.
  - Recommended to continue using 72 cores.
  - Multi-node scaling is not expected to provide significant speedup.

## Conclusion
- **User Action:** Continue with the default job script using 72 cores.
```
---

### 42438746_Problem%20with%20number%20of%20simultaneous%20processes%20in%20Woody.md
# Ticket 42438746

 # HPC Support Ticket: Problem with Number of Simultaneous Processes in Woody

## Keywords
- HPC
- Job Submission
- PBS Script
- Simultaneous Processes
- Array Jobs
- Woody

## Problem Description
The user is experiencing issues with running multiple processes simultaneously on the Woody HPC cluster. Despite requesting multiple nodes, only one process runs per core at a time, with new processes launching only after the previous ones finish.

## Root Cause
The PBS script is not configured to launch multiple processes simultaneously. The script uses a sequential loop to launch processes, which results in only one process running at a time per core.

## Script Provided by User
```bash
#!/bin/bash -l
#
#PBS -l nodes=1:ppn=4,walltime=00:30:00
#
# job name
#PBS -N Program_Name
#
# stdout and stderr files
#PBS -o Program_Name.out -e Program_Name.err
#
# make it an array
#PBS -t 1-4
#
# first non-empty non-comment line ends PBS options

module load gcc/4.6.1
module load openmpi/1.6.5-gcc

cd "${PBS_O_WORKDIR}"

for i in $(seq 1 ${PBS_NUM_PPN}); do
    n=$(((${PBS_ARRAYID} - 1)*${PBS_NUM_PPN} + ${i}))
    "${HOME}/bin/Program_Name" "${n}.cfg"
done
wait
```

## Solution
The user resolved the issue independently. The solution likely involved modifying the script to launch processes in parallel rather than sequentially.

## General Learning
- Ensure that job scripts are configured to launch processes in parallel if simultaneous execution is required.
- Understand the difference between sequential and parallel job execution in PBS scripts.
- Review and modify PBS script configurations to optimize resource utilization.

## Next Steps
- Document the specific changes made to the script for future reference.
- Provide guidance on parallel job submission in PBS for other users facing similar issues.
---

### 2022010442000621_Job%20schedulling%20problem.md
# Ticket 2022010442000621

 # Job Scheduling Problem on HPC Cluster

## Keywords
- Job scheduling
- GPU nodes
- Memory issues
- Pending jobs
- ExcNodeList

## Problem Description
- User is experiencing job scheduling issues on the HPC cluster.
- Jobs require specific GPU nodes with large memory (e.g., tg071-tg074, tg090-tg097).
- Jobs are stuck in pending status for an extended period (almost a week).
- Lower GPU nodes cause memory issues due to the coarseness of the model.

## Root Cause
- Incorrect usage of `ExcNodeList` in job submission scripts.
- Lack of understanding of the cluster's job scheduling documentation.

## Solution
- Refer to the official documentation: [TinyGPU Cluster Documentation](https://hpc.fau.de/systems-services/systems-documentation-instructions/clusters/tinygpu-cluster/)
- Ensure proper job submission parameters are used as per the documentation.
- Avoid using `ExcNodeList` as it is not supported in the intended manner.

## General Learnings
- Always refer to the official documentation for job submission guidelines.
- Ensure job scripts are correctly configured to request the appropriate resources.
- Misconfiguration in job scripts can lead to jobs being stuck in pending status.

## Next Steps for Support
- Verify the job submission script for correct resource requests.
- Ensure the user is aware of the correct parameters for requesting specific GPU nodes.
- Monitor job status and provide further assistance if jobs continue to remain pending.

---

This documentation aims to help support employees resolve similar job scheduling issues in the future.
---

### 2023033142001706_Multi-Node%20Jobs%20auf%20dem%20Alex.md
# Ticket 2023033142001706

 # HPC Support Ticket: Multi-Node Jobs auf dem Alex

## Keywords
- Multi-Node Jobs
- Ray Tune
- Hyperparameteroptimierung
- Tier3 User
- Alex Cluster
- NHR-Antrag
- a40multi
- a100multi

## Problem
- User versucht, eine automatische Hyperparameteroptimierung mit Ray Tune auf dem Alex Cluster durchzuführen.
- Ray Tune erfordert mehrere Nodes für die Optimierung.
- Tier3 User sind auf eine Node beschränkt.

## Lösung
- User wurde auf "Probe" für a40multi und a100multi freigeschaltet.
- Weitere Kommunikation und Unterstützung durch HPC Admin übernommen.
- NHR-Antrag soll folgen, wenn User als Doktorand anfängt.

## Zu Lernendes
- Tier3 User sind auf eine Node beschränkt.
- Für Multi-Node Jobs ist ein NHR-Antrag erforderlich.
- HPC Admin kann temporäre Freischaltungen für spezielle Nodes gewähren.
- Detaillierte Informationen zu Batch-Jobs finden sich in der Dokumentation unter [hpc.fau.de](https://hpc.fau.de/systems-services/documentation-instructions/clusters/alex-cluster/#batch).

## Weiterführende Schritte
- Bei ähnlichen Anfragen auf die Dokumentation verweisen.
- Bei Bedarf temporäre Freischaltungen durch HPC Admin prüfen.
- NHR-Antrag für Multi-Node Jobs empfehlen.
---

### 2024051242000274_Woody%20ist%20extrem%20langsam.md
# Ticket 2024051242000274

 # HPC Support Ticket: Woody ist extrem langsam

## Keywords
- Woodycap6
- Woodycap5
- File system issues
- SSH connection issues
- High file server load

## Problem Description
- User reported extreme slowness on `woodycap6`, with simple commands taking several minutes to execute.
- User suspected the cluster might be overloaded by a job.

## Root Cause
- High file server load causing disruptions on the cluster.

## Ticket Conversation Summary
- **User**: Reported slowness on `woodycap6`.
- **HPC Admin**: Informed the user about ongoing file system issues due to high file server load and provided a link to a service disruption notice.
- **User**: Reported that `woodycap5` was working better, but `woodycap6` was unreachable via SSH.
- **HPC Admin**: Acknowledged the issue and requested further updates if the problem persisted.

## Solution
- The issue was linked to ongoing file system problems due to high file server load.
- User was advised to use `woodycap5` as a temporary solution.

## Additional Notes
- The problem was intermittent and not consistently reproducible by the admin.
- Users should check the service disruption notice for updates on file system issues.

## References
- [Service Disruptions on Clusters Due to High File Server Load](https://hpc.fau.de/2024/04/19/service-disruptions-on-clusters-due-to-high-file-server-load/)
---

# HPC Support Ticket: Requesting Help

- User needs to run ~16,000 simulations.
- Each simulation takes approximately 2 minutes using 1 node with 20 processors.

## Root Cause

### 2018111442000494_Jobs%20auf%20Woody%20mit%20%3Asl%20Property%20_%20iwi5003h.md
# Ticket 2018111442000494

 # HPC Support Ticket: Jobs with :sl Property

## Keywords
- Job scheduling
- Resource allocation
- Account sharing
- HPC account management
- Software installation transfer

## Summary
The user's jobs were requesting specific nodes with increased memory (:sl property) despite not requiring significant memory, leading to inefficient resource usage and job queue delays. Additionally, account sharing was detected, resulting in account suspension.

## Root Cause
- **Resource Misallocation**: Jobs were explicitly requesting nodes with higher memory capacity (:sl) but did not require the allocated resources.
- **Account Sharing**: The user's account was shared among multiple individuals, violating HPC policies.

## Solution
- **Resource Allocation**: The user was advised to adjust job properties to match actual resource requirements, ensuring efficient use of cluster resources.
- **Account Management**: The user was informed about the personal nature of HPC accounts and the consequences of account sharing. The account was temporarily suspended but later reinstated with instructions on setting Unix permissions to share software installations without violating policies.

## Lessons Learned
- Ensure job properties accurately reflect resource requirements to optimize cluster usage.
- HPC accounts are personal and should not be shared.
- Proper Unix permissions can facilitate software sharing without violating account policies.

## Follow-Up
- Monitor job properties to ensure efficient resource allocation.
- Educate users on the importance of adhering to account policies to prevent future suspensions.
---

### 2023072642000693_HPC%20f%C3%83%C2%BCr%20Doktoranden.md
# Ticket 2023072642000693

 # HPC Support Ticket: HPC Access for PhD Student

## Keywords
- HPC Access
- PhD Student
- Application
- IdM ID
- HPC ID
- HPC Cafe
- Documentation

## Summary
A PhD student from the Chair of Pattern Recognition (Computer Science 5) at Friedrich-Alexander-Universität Erlangen-Nürnberg requested access to HPC resources. The application was processed, and an HPC ID was created for the student.

## Root Cause
- A PhD student needed access to HPC resources for their research.

## Solution
1. **Application Processing**: The HPC Admin processed the application and created an HPC ID (`iwi5150h`) for the student.
2. **IdM Registration**: The student was instructed to register with their IdM ID at [www.idm.fau.de](http://www.idm.fau.de) and set a password for the new HPC ID under Services.
3. **Initial Setup**: The student was informed that it may take up to 2 days for the services to be initially set up on the HPC servers.
4. **Support Resources**: The student was invited to participate in the HPC Cafe and was provided with links to relevant documentation and online introductions for new users.

## Follow-up
- The ticket was closed after the HPC ID was created and the student was informed about the next steps.

## Additional Notes
- The HPC Cafe is held every second Tuesday of the month at 4 p.m. in room 2.049, RRZE, Martensstr.1.
- Details about the HPC Cafe and online introductions can be found at [HPC Cafe website](https://hpc.fau.de/systems-services/support/hpc-cafe/).
- The HPC documentation is available at [HPC Documentation](https://hpc.fau.de/systems-services/systems-documentation-instructions/getting-started/).

## Roles Involved
- **HPC Admins**: Processed the application and created the HPC ID.
- **2nd Level Support Team**: Available for further assistance if needed.
- **Head of the Datacenter** and **Training and Support Group Leader**: Oversee the overall support and training processes.
- **NHR Rechenzeit Support**: Available for support related to computing time and grant applications.
- **Software and Tools Developer**: Available for assistance with software and tools.
---

### 2022051942002521_Tier3-Access-Fritz%20%22Paras%20Kumar%22%20_%20iwtm020h.md
# Ticket 2022051942002521

 # HPC Support Ticket Analysis

## Keywords
- Multi-node workload
- Parallelism
- Hardware performance counters
- LIKWID
- Deal.ii
- Trilinos
- MPI
- Shared memory parallel
- Job saturation
- System monitoring interference

## Summary
- **User Request:** Access to Fritz for multi-node workload with specific hardware requirements.
- **HPC Admin Concerns:**
  - Previous jobs were single-node with low load.
  - Use of LIKWID without measuring hardware performance counters.
- **User Response:**
  - Plans to use MPI variant of in-house code for future jobs.
  - Will ensure proper use of LIKWID.

## Root Cause of the Problem
- User's previous jobs did not demonstrate the need for multi-node workload.
- Inappropriate use of LIKWID, interfering with system monitoring.

## Solution
- User explained the plan to use MPI for future jobs, addressing the parallelism concern.
- User agreed to use LIKWID only when necessary, mitigating interference with system monitoring.

## General Learnings
- Always justify multi-node requests with evidence of job scalability.
- Be mindful of tools like LIKWID that can interfere with system operations.
- Clear communication about job requirements and tool usage is crucial.

## Follow-up Actions
- Monitor user's future jobs to ensure proper use of resources and tools.
- Provide guidance on best practices for multi-node jobs and tool usage if needed.
---

### 2023032142001691_Jobs%20on%20Alex%20only%20use%204%20of%208%20allocated%20GPUs%20%5Bbccc007h%5D.md
# Ticket 2023032142001691

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: Jobs on Alex only use 4 of 8 allocated GPUs

## Keywords
- GPU allocation
- Job script
- Lammps
- Variable export
- Resource utilization

## Problem Description
The user's jobs on Alex requested 8 GPUs but only utilized 4. The job script exported the variable `export ng=4`, which was then passed to Lammps with the flag `-pk gpu $ng`.

## Root Cause
The user mistakenly set the variable `ng` to 4 instead of 8, leading to underutilization of the allocated GPUs.

## Solution
The user acknowledged the mistake and promised to ensure that future jobs utilize all allocated GPUs.

## Lessons Learned
- Always verify that the job script correctly sets the number of GPUs to match the allocated resources.
- Double-check variable settings to avoid underutilization of resources.
- Properly configure job scripts to ensure efficient use of HPC resources.

## Actions Taken
- The HPC Admin notified the user about the issue and provided a screenshot of the job script as an example.
- The user acknowledged the error and committed to correcting it in future jobs.
- The ticket was closed as the user recognized the mistake and promised improvement.
```
---

### 2019011042001829_ncl%20auf%20Meggie%20_%20gwgi006h.md
# Ticket 2019011042001829

 # HPC Support Ticket: ncl auf Meggie / gwgi006h

## Keywords
- ncl
- Serial Program
- Job Submission
- Time Limit
- Manual Time Extension
- TinyFAT
- Woody

## Problem
- User submitted a job (ncl) that requested multiple nodes, but ncl is a serial program and can only use one core on one node.
- The job exceeded the 24-hour time limit on a single node.

## Root Cause
- Misunderstanding of the job requirements and resource allocation.
- The job was submitted with incorrect resource specifications, leading to unnecessary blocking of multiple nodes.

## Solution
- HPC Admin advised the user to submit the job with a 24-hour time limit and send the JobID for manual time extension.
- For occasional long-running jobs, the user was advised to use TinyFAT or Woody, which allow up to 48 hours of runtime.
- The user's job was manually extended to 5 days to accommodate the long runtime.

## General Learnings
- Always ensure that the job submission parameters match the job requirements.
- Serial jobs should only request one core on one node.
- For jobs that exceed the default time limit, manual time extension can be requested.
- Alternative resources like TinyFAT or Woody can be used for long-running jobs.

## Actions Taken
- HPC Admin manually extended the job's runtime to 5 days.
- User was advised on proper job submission for serial programs and alternative resources for long-running jobs.

## Follow-up
- User successfully completed the job and requested manual time extension for additional jobs.
- HPC Admin granted the requested time extensions.
---

### 2024091042000261_network%20unreachable%20error.md
# Ticket 2024091042000261

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: Network Unreachable Error

## Keywords
- Network unreachable
- SSH connection error
- Routing issue
- Network outage

## Summary
A user reported an SSH connection error while trying to connect to the HPC cluster. The error message indicated that the network was unreachable.

## Error Message
```
ssh: connect to host csnhr.nhr.fau.de port 22: Network is unreachable
kex_exchange_identification: Connection closed by remote host
Connection closed by UNKNOWN port 65535
```

## Root Cause
The issue was identified as a networking problem at the user's institution (IIS). It was suspected to be related to a network outage affecting the routing between IIS and FAU.

## Solution
The network outage was resolved by the institution's IT department. Users were advised to use their home internet connection without a VPN to Fraunhofer until the issue was fixed.

## Lessons Learned
- Networking issues can affect specific institutions or locations.
- It's important to check if the issue is widespread or localized.
- Users can temporarily use alternative internet connections to bypass local network issues.
- Communication with the institution's IT department can help identify and resolve network-related problems.
```
---

### 2016041242001674_LIMA%3A%20GLIBC%20library.md
# Ticket 2016041242001674

 ```markdown
# HPC-Support Ticket: LIMA: GLIBC Library Issue

## Keywords
- GLIBC
- Version Conflict
- Software Compatibility
- Library Dependencies

## Problem Description
- **Software**: GAMMA
- **Issue**: The `SLC_copy` command fails due to a missing GLIBC version.
- **Error Message**: `SLC_copy: /lib64/libc.so.6: version 'GLIBC_2.14' not found (required by SLC_copy)`
- **Current GLIBC Version**: GLIBC_2.12 (2011)
- **Required GLIBC Version**: GLIBC_2.14

## Root Cause
- The precompiled software package requires a newer version of the GLIBC library than what is currently installed on the system.

## Solution
- **Local Installation**: The user has locally installed GLIBC_2.14 but wants to avoid using 300MB of space.
- **Potential Fix**: Update the system's GLIBC library to the required version or use a containerized environment that includes the necessary dependencies.

## General Learning
- **Library Dependencies**: Ensure that all required libraries are compatible with the software being used.
- **Version Management**: Be aware of version conflicts and the need to update system libraries or use local installations.
- **Space Management**: Consider the impact of local installations on storage space and explore alternative solutions like containers.

## Next Steps
- **HPC Admins**: Assess the feasibility of updating the system's GLIBC library.
- **2nd Level Support**: Provide guidance on using containerized environments to manage dependencies without affecting the system-wide configuration.
```
---

### 2016112542001369_HPC-Zugang.md
# Ticket 2016112542001369

 # HPC-Support Ticket: Changing HPC Password

- HPC Access

- User is unaware of the procedure to set a separate password for HPC access.

## General Learnings
- HPC passwords can be changed separately from other university services.
- The IDM-Portal is the primary interface for managing user credentials.
- The RRZE-ServiceTheke is the point of contact for issues related to HPC access and password management.

- Nvidia A100, A40 GPUs
- Singularity/Anaconda
- Neural Scene Representation Networks
- Publications
- Partition Specification

## General Learnings
- **Prerequisites**: Access to HPC systems requires an HPC account.
- **Documentation**: Users should refer to the HPC4FAU section in the getting started guide.
- **Partition Specification**: Explicitly specifying a partition (e.g., `--partition=a40` or `partition=a100`) is mandatory.
- **Account Setup**: Newly created HPC accounts may take time to propagate across all fileservers.

## Root Cause of the Problem
- User did not have an HPC account initially.
- User needed access to the early-usage Alex system.

## Solution
- User created an HPC account.
- HPC Admin granted access to the early-usage Alex system.
- User was informed about the mandatory partition specification and the delay in fileserver propagation.

## Documentation Links
- [Getting Started with HPC4FAU](https://hpc.fau.de/systems-services/systems-documentation-instructions/getting-started/)
- [Alex Cluster Documentation](https://hpc.fau.de/systems-services/systems-documentation-instructions/clusters/alex-cluster/)

## Notes
- The user's application involved training Neural Scene Representation Networks with high memory requirements and long job runtimes.
- The expected outcome of the user's work was publications.
---

### 42127943_HPC-Kennung.md
# Ticket 42127943

 # HPC Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

## Keywords
- HPC Account Setup
- Password Configuration
- Queue Service Issue
- Job Submission Problem
- Access Request

## General Learnings
- **Account Setup Process**: Users are provided with an HPC account and need to set a password via the identity management website.
- **Language Barrier**: Users may require support in their preferred language.
- **Queue Service Troubleshooting**: Issues with job submission and queue services may require detailed investigation.
- **Access Requests**: Users may request access to specific HPC resources based on their computational needs.

## Root Cause of the Problem
- **User Issue**: The user encountered problems with job submission on the HPC server, with jobs not being processed and no error logs generated.

## Solution
- **Account Setup**: The user was instructed to log in to the identity management website and set a password for the new HPC account.
- **Queue Service Issue**: The user reported a problem with the queue service not processing jobs. This issue was noted for further investigation by the support team.
- **Access Request**: The user requested access to a specific HPC resource ("testfront.rrze.uni-erlangen") due to the nature of their calculations.

## Documentation for Support Employees
When encountering similar issues, ensure the following steps are taken:
1. **Account Setup**: Guide the user to log in to the identity management website and set a password for their new HPC account.
2. **Language Support**: Offer support in the user's preferred language if possible.
3. **Queue Service Troubleshooting**: Investigate queue service issues by checking job submission logs and system status.
4. **Access Requests**: Evaluate and process requests for access to specific HPC resources based on user needs.

By following these steps, support employees can effectively address account setup, language barriers, queue service issues, and access requests.

### 2022031942000483_Videotranscoding%20l%C3%83%C2%A4uft%20nicht%20an.md

# HPC Support Ticket: Videotranscoding lÃ¤uft nicht an

- w11xx
- w12xx
- any32g

- Two videos were submitted for transcoding:
  - `20220316-WissenWollen-Kinzelbach.mov`
  - `Weltwassertag2022.mov`
- User suspected a missed maintenance interval.

## Root Cause
- The transcoding jobs were failing because `ffmpeg` was not installed on some nodes (w12xx).

## Solution
- HPC Admin identified that `ffmpeg` was missing on w12xx nodes.
- The nodes were manually updated with `ffmpeg`.
- The problem was resolved by reinstalling the necessary software on the affected nodes.

## Lessons Learned
- Ensure that all required software is installed on all nodes that may run specific jobs.
- Regular maintenance and updates can prevent such issues.
- Proper documentation of changes and configurations can help in troubleshooting.
```

### 2023112342004294_Tier3-Access-Alex%20%22Simeon%20Allmendinger%22%20_%20btr0104h.md
# Ticket 2023112342004294

 # HPC Support Ticket Analysis: Tier3-Access-Alex

## Keywords
- Tier3 Access
- Alex (GPGPU Cluster)
- Nvidia A100, A40 GPGPUs
- GPU-hours
- NHR Proposal
- Python, PyTorch, pip
- Surgical Simulations
- Generative AI
- Diffusion Models

## Summary
A user requested access to the Alex GPGPU cluster for a project involving surgical simulations using generative AI and diffusion models. The user specified the need for Nvidia A100 and A40 GPUs, along with software requirements including Python, PyTorch, and pip. The user also mentioned the possibility of downsizing to a single GPU if a multi-GPU setting is not available.

## Root Cause of the Problem
The user's request for GPU-hours may not be fully satisfied by the free Tier3 basic service.

## Solution
The HPC Admin enabled the user's account on Alex but recommended applying for compute time via an NHR proposal to ensure the requested compute time is available.

## General Learnings
- **Account Activation**: HPC Admins can enable user accounts on specific clusters.
- **Resource Allocation**: Free Tier3 basic service may not guarantee the requested compute time.
- **NHR Proposal**: Users should apply for compute time via an NHR proposal to secure the needed resources.
- **Software Requirements**: Users should specify their software needs, such as Python, PyTorch, and pip.
- **Flexibility**: Users should be prepared to adjust their resource requests based on availability.

## Relevant Links
- [NHR Application Rules](https://hpc.fau.de/systems-services/documentation-instructions/nhr-application-rules/)

## Next Steps for Support
- Ensure the user is aware of the NHR proposal process.
- Monitor the user's resource usage and provide assistance with software installation if needed.
- Document any further issues related to GPU allocation and resource management.
---

### 2024072942002013_HPC%20Login%20Error%20for%20account%20iwal117h.md
# Ticket 2024072942002013

 # HPC Login Error: Shell Request Failure and Permission Denied

## Keywords
- HPC Login Error
- Shell Request Failure
- Permission Denied
- SSH Debug Log
- Known Hosts

## Problem Description
- User unable to login to `tinyx.nhr.fau.de` with a shell request failure error.
- User can login to dialog servers (`cshpc.rrze.fau.de` and `csnhr.nhr.fau.de`) but encounters a permission denied error when trying to login to `tinyx` from there.
- User sees a message "There is 1 zombie process" on `csnhr` login.
- User suspected passkey or authentication issue but can login to WinSCP using the same configuration file and passkey.

## Troubleshooting Steps
1. **SSH Debug Log**: HPC Admin requested the output of `ssh -vv ...` for investigation.
2. **Known Hosts Reset**: HPC Admin suggested deleting the content of the `known_hosts` file in the `.ssh` directory on both the local machine and `csnhr`.

## Solution
- Deleting the content of the `known_hosts` file resolved the login issue. The user was able to login to `tinyx` without encountering the shell request failure or permission denied errors.

## Conclusion
- The root cause of the problem was related to the `known_hosts` file. Resetting this file resolved the login issues.
- This solution can be applied to similar login errors where users encounter shell request failure or permission denied errors.
---

The user encountered an error while compiling MAGMA, specifically:
```
make: ./tools/codegen.py: Command not found
```
The error indicates that the `./tools/codegen.py` script is missing or not found in the specified directory.

## Root Cause
The root cause of the problem is that the `./tools/codegen.py` script is missing from the MAGMA source directory. This script is required for the compilation process.

## Solution
The user confirmed that the `./tools/codegen.py` script was indeed missing. The solution involves ensuring that all necessary files, including `./tools/codegen.py`, are present in the MAGMA source directory before attempting to compile.

## Lessons Learned
- Always verify that all required files are present in the source directory before compiling.
- Missing files can lead to compilation errors, even if all dependencies are correctly loaded.
- Double-check the presence of essential scripts and tools when encountering "Command not found" errors during compilation.

- Python dependency management

### 2022091342003917_Transition%20Emmy%20-%3E%20Meggie%20%3A%20kein%20modul%20intel64.md
# Ticket 2022091342003917

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: Transition Emmy -> Meggie : kein modul intel64

## Keywords
- Transition Emmy to Meggie
- intel64 module
- intelmpi
- mkl
- VASP
- Slurm Batch Job

## Problem
- User is transitioning from Emmy to Meggie.
- The `intel64` module available on Emmy is not present on Meggie.
- User needs to know if the order of loading modules matters and if older versions of modules will work.

## Root Cause
- The `intel64` module, which loads multiple Intel-related modules, is not available on Meggie.
- Older versions of modules (17.xx) are not available on Meggie.

## Solution
- The `intel64` module is not available on Meggie, and its components need to be loaded manually.
- The order of loading modules does not matter.
- Older versions (17.xx) are not available; use the recommended versions (2021.X).
- The VASP Slurm Batch Job documentation will be updated to reflect the changes.

## General Learnings
- Always check for module availability when transitioning between systems.
- Ensure that the documentation is updated to reflect changes in module availability and usage.
- Communicate with users about recommended module versions and any changes in the system.
```
---

### 2022031142002549_Consultation%20on%20the%20extend%20the%20quota%20of%20%24WORK%20file%20system.md
# Ticket 2022031142002549

 # HPC Support Ticket: Extend $WORK Quota

## Keywords
- Quota extension
- GPU utilization
- SSH connection reset
- Scheduled downtime

## Summary
- **User Request**: Increase $WORK quota from 500GB to 1000GB for deep learning tasks.
- **Admin Response**: Conditional approval based on GPU utilization optimization.
- **SSH Issue**: User encountered `ssh_exchange_identification: read: Connection reset by peer`.

## Detailed Conversation

### User Request
- **Reason**: Insufficient $WORK quota for storing large datasets (600GB for tracking, 300GB for segmentation).
- **Justification**: Published and upcoming research papers in object tracking and segmentation.

### Admin Response
- **Initial Feedback**: GPU utilization fluctuation observed; optimization needed before quota increase.
- **Follow-up**: Quota increased to 1TB after user explained low GPU utilization during model evaluation.

### SSH Connection Issue
- **Problem**: User encountered `ssh_exchange_identification: read: Connection reset by peer`.
- **Cause**: Scheduled downtime of the HPC system.
- **Solution**: Inform user about the scheduled downtime.

## Lessons Learned
- **Quota Management**: Conditional quota increases can incentivize resource optimization.
- **GPU Utilization**: Evaluate job performance before granting additional resources.
- **SSH Issues**: Scheduled downtimes can cause connection resets; inform users proactively.

## Action Items
- **User**: Optimize GPU utilization by separating training and evaluation jobs.
- **Admin**: Monitor user jobs and provide feedback for optimization.
- **Communication**: Ensure users are aware of scheduled downtimes to avoid confusion.

## Conclusion
Effective communication and conditional resource allocation can lead to better utilization of HPC resources. Proactive notification of scheduled downtimes can prevent user frustration.
---

### 2018121742001584_Kontakt%3A%20Elmer_Ice%20Kompilierung.md
# Ticket 2018121742001584

 # HPC Support Ticket: Elmer/Ice Compilation Issue

## Keywords
- Elmer/Ice
- Compilation
- LD_LIBRARY_PATH
- Shared Object File
- LIMA
- Woody
- NetCDF
- Intel Compiler

## Problem Description
The user encountered issues with compiling and running Elmer/Ice on the Woody system after the LIMA system was shut down. The compilation process resulted in warnings and runtime errors related to missing shared object files and libraries.

## Root Cause
- Incorrect or incomplete setting of the `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` environment variable.
- Possible path length issues with Elmer.
- Missing NetCDF library paths.

## Solution
1. **Set Environment Variables Correctly:**
   ```bash
   export ELMER_HOME=/path/to/elmerice
   export PATH=${PATH}:$ELMER_HOME/bin
   export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=$ELMER_HOME/lib:$ELMER_HOME/share/elmersolver/lib
   ```

2. **Include Existing LD_LIBRARY_PATH Values:**
   ```bash
   export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=${LD_LIBRARY_PATH}:$ELMER_HOME/lib:$ELMER_HOME/share/elmersolver/lib
   ```

3. **Load Intel Compiler Module:**
   ```bash
   module add intel64/19.0up01
   ```

4. **Add NetCDF Library Path if Necessary:**
   ```bash
   export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=${LD_LIBRARY_PATH}:$ELMER_HOME/../netcdf/4.6.2-4.4.4/lib
   ```

5. **Ensure Correct Paths and Compilation:**
   - Verify that all paths are correctly set and that the compilation process includes the necessary libraries.
   - Check for any additional warnings or errors during compilation and runtime.

## Additional Information
- The user was advised to provide detailed information for future troubleshooting, including `$ELMER_HOME`, job scripts, and output files.
- A personal consultation was offered for further assistance.

## Conclusion
The issue was resolved by correctly setting the `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` and ensuring that all necessary libraries were included. The user confirmed that the solution worked and thanked the support team for their assistance.
---

- **Check User Permissions**: Verify the user's permissions and roles within the HPC system.
- **Consult HPC Admins**: If the issue persists, escalate to HPC Admins for further investigation and resolution.
- **Update Documentation**: Ensure that the support knowledge base includes detailed procedures for handling authorization issues.

## Additional Notes
- **Training**: Consider providing training sessions on common authorization issues and their resolutions to the support team.
- **Collaboration**: Encourage collaboration between the 2nd Level Support team and HPC Admins to streamline the resolution process.
---

### 2020072942002521_vasp%20rechnungen%20am%20meggie.md
# Ticket 2020072942002521

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: VASP Calculations Aborted on Meggie

## Keywords
- VASP
- Meggie
- OmniPath Network
- $FASTTMP
- Job Abortion
- Parallel Filesystem

## Problem Description
- **User Issue**: VASP calculations on Meggie aborted around 2:45-2:47 AM. This has happened multiple times in the past few weeks.
- **System Report**: The system indicates that the calculations were completed successfully, but VASP did not finish and did not report any errors.

## Root Cause
- **HPC Admin Analysis**: There have been repeated issues with the OmniPath network and $FASTTMP. The hardware is aging.

## Solution
- **HPC Admin Recommendation**:
  - Report all issues with job IDs for better tracking.
  - Consider migrating to Titan or Saturn, as VASP may not fully utilize the parallel filesystem on Meggie.

## General Learnings
- **Hardware Issues**: Aging hardware can cause recurring problems with network and storage.
- **Job Reporting**: Always report issues with job IDs for better troubleshooting.
- **Migration**: Consider migrating to more suitable systems if the current one is not fully utilized by the application.
```
---

### 2023090142002411_TinyGPU%20extension.md
# Ticket 2023090142002411

 # HPC Support Ticket: TinyGPU Extension

## Keywords
- Account Extension
- TinyGPU
- HPC
- Expiration Date
- Chair Request

## Summary
A user requested an extension for their TinyGPU and HPC account for the next semester, as their current account was set to expire at the end of September.

## Root Cause
The user's HPC application specified an expiration date at the end of September, and the user needed to continue using the resources beyond this date.

## Solution
The HPC Admin informed the user that the chair of their department must request the extension of the HPC account.

## General Learning
- Users cannot extend their HPC accounts themselves; the request must come from the chair of their department.
- Always check the expiration date specified in the initial HPC application.
- Communicate clearly with users about the necessary steps for account extensions.

## Next Steps
- The user should contact their department chair to request the extension.
- The HPC Admin should await the formal request from the chair before processing the extension.

## Related Parties
- HPC Admins
- Department Chair
- User
---

### 2024073042003091_Fwd%3A%20Cluster%20Access%20for%20non-university%20people.md
# Ticket 2024073042003091

 # Cluster Access for Non-University People

## Keywords
- Cluster access
- Non-university affiliation
- DFN authorization
- IdM account
- SSO-Berechtigung
- Gastwissenschaftlerantrag

## Problem
A colleague from a company involved in a research project failed the DFN authorization process due to lack of university affiliation. The colleague needs access to the HPC cluster.

## Root Cause
The DFN authorization process requires university affiliation, which the colleague does not have.

## Solution
1. **IdM Account with SSO-Berechtigung**: Attempt to provide the colleague with an IdM account with SSO-Berechtigung through the TU-Dortmund.
2. **Gastwissenschaftlerantrag**: If the above is not possible, the Professur für HPC can submit a Gastwissenschaftlerantrag for the colleague. This process is formal and time-consuming, involving the international office of FAU and the Exportkontrollbeauftragte.

## General Learning
- Non-university affiliates involved in research projects may require special procedures for cluster access.
- IdM accounts with SSO-Berechtigung can be a solution for providing access.
- Gastwissenschaftlerantrag is an alternative but involves a formal and time-consuming process.
---

### 2008021942255_ProEngineer.md
# Ticket 2008021942255

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: ProEngineer Library Issue

## Keywords
- ProEngineer
- libXm.so.3
- Shared libraries
- dempsey1
- dempsey2
- Ubuntu 6.04-LTS

## Problem Description
User encountered an error while attempting to start a new optimization with ProEngineer. The error message indicated a missing shared library:
```
/apps/ProEngineer/proeWildfire3.0-M010/i486_linux/obj/proe.exe: error while loading shared libraries: libXm.so.3: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory
```

## Root Cause
The `libXm.so.3` library was missing on the system (dempsey1), which is required for ProEngineer to run.

## Solution
HPC Admin manually copied the `libXm.so.3` library from dempsey2 to dempsey1. The 32-bit `libXm.so.3` library is not available as a package for 64-bit Ubuntu 6.04-LTS and was previously manually copied to dempsey1.

## General Learning
- Ensure that all required shared libraries are available on the system where the application is being run.
- Manual copying of libraries may be necessary if they are not available through standard package management.
- Verify the availability of libraries on all systems where the application is expected to run.
```
---

### 2023052642001785_Anfrage%20Poliezi%20N%C3%83%C2%BCrnberg%20wg.%20HPC%20f%C3%83%C2%BCr%20Passwort-Cracken.md
# Ticket 2023052642001785

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

## Keywords
- HPC Access Request
- Password Cracking
- Security
- Personalausweis
- Rainbow Tables
- Data Protection
- Termination of Request

## Summary
- **Request**: A user from the police department in Nürnberg requested access to HPC resources for password cracking.
- **HPC Admin Response**: The admin provided information on the application process, including the need to fill out an application and present a personal ID. They also inquired about the user's methodology and data sensitivity.
- **Outcome**: The user later informed the admin that the need for HPC resources had been resolved due to fortunate circumstances.

## Root Cause of the Problem
- The user initially required HPC resources for password cracking but later found an alternative solution.

## Solution
- The user was informed about the application process and requirements for accessing HPC resources.
- The request was closed as the user no longer needed the resources.

## General Learnings
- **Application Process**: Users need to fill out an application and present a personal ID to gain access to HPC resources.
- **Data Sensitivity**: It is important to inquire about the sensitivity of data that will be processed on HPC systems.
- **Flexibility**: Users' needs can change rapidly, and it is essential to be prepared for such changes.

## Documentation for Support Employees
- **Access Request**: Ensure users are aware of the application process and the need to present a personal ID.
- **Data Handling**: Always inquire about the type of data and its sensitivity to ensure compliance with security protocols.
- **Communication**: Maintain open communication with users to stay updated on their needs and any changes in their requirements.
```
---

### 2024062742001485_Kurs%20gwku%20Homefolder%20on%20Vault.md
# Ticket 2024062742001485

 # HPC Support Ticket: Kurs gwku Homefolder on Vault

## Keywords
- Homefolder
- Vault
- Lehrveranstaltungen
- $WORK
- $HPCVAULT
- /home/vault
- /home/woody

## Problem
- User requested home folders on Vault for course participants (gwku100).

## Root Cause
- Standard practice does not include creating home folders on Vault for course participants due to its intended use for medium to long-term storage.

## Solution
- Use $WORK (/home/woody) instead of $HPCVAULT (/home/vault) for course-related storage.

## General Learning
- Vault is intended for medium to long-term storage, not suitable for short-term course-related storage.
- $WORK (/home/woody) is the recommended directory for course-related storage.

## Conversation Summary
- User inquired about the possibility of home folders on Vault for course participants.
- HPC Admin explained the standard practice and suggested using $WORK instead.
- User agreed to use $WORK and will update course materials accordingly.
---

### 2023033142003366_HPC-Account%20verl%C3%83%C2%A4ngern_Zugeh%C3%83%C2%B6rigkeit.md
# Ticket 2023033142003366

 # HPC-Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

## Keywords
- HPC Account Verlängerung
- Zugehörigkeit
- Zugriffsrechte
- Reaktivierung
- Benutzernamen
- Fairshare
- Quota
- Accounting

## General Learnings
- **Account Verlängerung**: Users may need to submit a Verlängerungsantrag (extension request) for their HPC account.
- **Zugehörigkeit**: Accounts can be associated with multiple groups, but primary affiliation should be clearly defined.
- **Zugriffsrechte**: Access rights may take time to propagate across all systems after reactivation.
- **Benutzernamen**: Ensure correct usage of usernames, including any suffixes.
- **Fairshare, Quota, Accounting**: Proper allocation of resources and accounting is crucial to avoid misuse.

## Root Cause of the Problem
- The user's HPC account expired, and they needed an extension for continued access.
- The user encountered access issues even after the account was reactivated due to propagation delays.
- The user attempted to log in with an incorrect username.

## Solution
- The user submitted an extension request, which was processed by the HPC Admins.
- The user was advised to wait for the access rights to propagate across all systems.
- The user was informed about the correct usage of their username and the importance of proper resource allocation.

## Documentation for Support Employees

### Account Extension and Reactivation
1. **Submission of Extension Request**: Users should submit a Verlängerungsantrag for their HPC account before it expires.
2. **Processing by HPC Admins**: The HPC Admins will process the request and update the account's affiliation and access rights.
3. **Propagation Delay**: Inform users that it may take up to a day for the access rights to be fully propagated across all systems.

### Username and Resource Allocation
1. **Correct Username Usage**: Ensure users are aware of the correct format of their usernames, including any suffixes.
2. **Resource Allocation**: Educate users about the importance of proper resource allocation and accounting to avoid misuse and ensure fairness.

### Troubleshooting Access Issues
1. **Check Username**: Verify that the user is using the correct username.
2. **Wait for Propagation**: Advise users to wait for the access rights to propagate if they encounter access issues immediately after reactivation.
3. **Contact HPC Admins**: If the issue persists, escalate to the HPC Admins for further investigation.

By following these guidelines, support employees can effectively assist users with account extensions, access issues, and resource allocation.
---

### 2021071442001815_HPC%20und%20Admin%20Account%20abgelaufen.md
# Ticket 2021071442001815

 # HPC Support Ticket Analysis

## Subject: HPC und Admin Account abgelaufen

### Keywords:
- HPC Account
- Admin Account
- IDM
- Organisationseinheit
- RRZE
- NHR@FAU
- Account Expiration
- Reaktivation

### Root Cause:
- User's HPC and Admin accounts expired due to a change in organizational affiliation from RRZE to NHR@FAU.

### Conversation Summary:
- User received emails indicating the expiration of their HPC and Admin accounts.
- The change in organizational affiliation from RRZE to NHR@FAU was identified as the likely cause.
- HPC Admin initially suggested that new applications for HPC and Admin accounts should be submitted.
- HPC Admin later reactivated the HPC account and OTRS access, but did not extend the Admin account as it was deemed unnecessary.

### Solution:
- HPC Admin reactivated the HPC account (`unrz139`) and OTRS access.
- The Admin account (`roroehlt`) was not extended as `sudo` access was available on `pollux`.

### General Learnings:
- Account expirations can occur due to changes in organizational affiliation.
- Reactivation of accounts may require administrative intervention.
- Admin accounts may not need to be reactivated if `sudo` access is available.

### Documentation for Support Employees:
- **Account Expiration Due to Organizational Change:**
  - When a user's organizational affiliation changes, their HPC and Admin accounts may expire.
  - Check the user's organizational affiliation and reactivate the accounts if necessary.
  - Ensure that `sudo` access is available before deciding not to reactivate Admin accounts.

- **Reactivation Process:**
  - HPC Admins can reactivate HPC accounts and OTRS access.
  - Admin accounts may not need to be reactivated if `sudo` access is available on relevant systems.

### Next Steps for Similar Issues:
- Verify the user's organizational affiliation.
- Reactivate the HPC account and OTRS access if necessary.
- Check for `sudo` access before deciding on Admin account reactivation.
---

### 2018042742000714_Asking%20for%20access%20to%20big%20queue%20on%20meggie.md
# Ticket 2018042742000714

 # HPC Support Ticket: Access to Big Queue on Meggie

## Keywords
- Access request
- Big queue
- Meggie cluster
- SLURM
- Partition request

## Summary
A user requested access to the big queue on the Meggie cluster to conduct experiments up to 256 nodes. The user provided current results for up to 64 nodes.

## Root Cause
- User needed access to the big queue for larger experiments.

## Solution
- HPC Admin granted access to the big queue.
- Informed the user that there is no routing mechanism in SLURM like in other clusters with PBS/torque.
- Advised the user to explicitly request the "big" partition in their large jobs.

## General Learnings
- Users need to request specific partitions in SLURM for large jobs.
- Access to the big queue requires explicit permission from HPC Admins.
- Providing current results can help in getting access to larger resources.

## Actions Taken
- Access granted to the big queue.
- User informed about SLURM partition request process.

## Follow-up
- Ensure the user understands how to request the "big" partition in SLURM.
- Monitor user's job submissions to verify proper partition usage.
---

### 2025022642000519_Stuck%20hpc%20jobs%20on%20helma.md
# Ticket 2025022642000519

 # HPC Support Ticket: Stuck Jobs on Helma Cluster

## Keywords
- Stuck jobs
- Helma cluster
- Multi-node jobs
- Python-based machine learning
- PyTorch
- CUDA
- GPU activity
- Storage I/O
- Network communication
- Kernel update

## Problem Description
- **Issue**: Multi-node (4 or 16 nodes) Python-based machine learning jobs using PyTorch and CUDA get stuck at a certain point (when loading the model checkpoint) with 0% GPU activity.
- **Symptoms**: Jobs appear to be waiting for storage I/O or network communication between nodes.
- **Environment**:
  - Python versions: 3.10.16 or 3.9.21
  - Relevant Python libraries: Various NVIDIA CUDA libraries, PyTorch 2.6.0
  - Data storage: `/anvme/workspace`

## Timeline
- **First Occurrence**: February 21
- **Last Known Working Date**: February 11

## Potential Causes
- **Cluster Changes**: A kernel update occurred within the mentioned time window.
- **Intermittent Issue**: The problem does not always happen and sometimes resolves with job restarts, especially with fewer nodes.

## Troubleshooting Steps
- **HPC Admin**:
  - Requested live access to an affected job for closer inspection.
  - Suggested alerting the support team via Matrix chat or responding to the ticket when the issue occurs again.

## Resolution
- **Pending**: The issue requires live inspection of a stuck job to determine the root cause.

## Next Steps
- **User**: Alert the HPC support team when the issue occurs again for live troubleshooting.
- **HPC Admin**: Inspect the stuck processes on the compute node during the live occurrence of the issue.

## Additional Notes
- No similar issues reported by other users.
- A logbook of changes is available for reference: [Logbook Link](https://pad.nhr.fau.de/dfZHvN8-RcSA0VjX17qJgw#)

## Contact Information
- **HPC Support**: support-hpc@fau.de
- **HPC Website**: [FAU HPC](https://hpc.fau.de/)
---

### 2021042242001421_Frage%20zur%20Verwendung%20von%20SSHFS%20und%20singularity.md
# Ticket 2021042242001421

 ```markdown
# HPC-Support Ticket: SSHFS and Singularity Usage

## Keywords
- SSHFS
- Singularity
- Data Transfer
- Container Building
- Ramdisk
- SCP
- RSYNC
- SSH Key
- Fakeroot

## Summary
A user inquired about using SSHFS to mount sensitive data from a remote system into a Singularity container and encountered issues with Singularity container building using the `--fakeroot` option.

## Root Cause
1. **SSHFS Availability**: SSHFS is not available on the compute nodes.
2. **Singularity Container Building**: The `--fakeroot` option does not work on the HPC cluster due to missing subuid mapping.

## Solution
1. **Data Transfer**:
   - Use `/dev/shm` (Ramdisk) to temporarily store sensitive data.
   - Transfer data using `scp` or `rsync` with an SSH key for passwordless access.

2. **Singularity Container Building**:
   - Build the container on a local machine instead of the HPC cluster.

## General Learnings
- **SSHFS Restrictions**: SSHFS is not supported on the compute nodes.
- **Alternative Data Transfer**: Use `/dev/shm` for temporary storage and `scp`/`rsync` for data transfer.
- **Singularity Container Building**: The `--fakeroot` option is not functional on the HPC cluster. Build containers locally.

## References
- [HPC-in-a-nutshell Presentation](https://hpc.fau.de/files/2021/02/2021-02-10_hpc_in_a_nutshell.pdf)
- [Singularity Containers Presentation](https://hpc.fau.de/files/2020/05/2020-05-12-singularity-containers.pdf)
```
---

### 2017090542001524_Fwd%3A%20Benutzeraccount%20in%20Erlangen.md
# Ticket 2017090542001524

 # HPC Support Ticket: Benutzeraccount in Erlangen

## Keywords
- User account creation
- IDM-Kennung
- HPC-Kennung
- Anträge
- Unterschriften
- Stempel
- Professur Wellein
- ihpc

## Summary
This ticket involves the creation of a user account for a new user at the HPC center in Erlangen. The process includes verification of personal information, assignment of IDM and HPC identifiers, and the need for signatures and stamps on the application forms.

## Root Cause
The user required an account to be created under the professorship of Wellein with the identifier "ihpc". The process involved verifying personal information and ensuring that the application forms were properly signed and stamped.

## Steps Taken
1. **Verification of Personal Information**:
   - HPC Admin verified the user's personal information, including birthplace, personal number, and date of birth.

2. **Request for Additional Information**:
   - HPC Admin requested the customer number/OU/group for the user account creation.

3. **Account Creation**:
   - HPC Admin created the user account and assigned the HPC identifier `ihpc027h`.

4. **Activation Instructions**:
   - HPC Admin sent activation instructions to the user, including the need to set a password for the new identifier.

5. **Signature and Stamp Requirement**:
   - HPC Admin requested that the application forms be signed and stamped before finalizing the account creation process.

## Solution
The user account was successfully created and the activation instructions were sent to the user. The application forms were signed and stamped, completing the account creation process.

## Lessons Learned
- Ensure that all necessary personal information is verified before proceeding with account creation.
- Request any additional information, such as customer number/OU/group, early in the process.
- Provide clear activation instructions to the user.
- Ensure that all application forms are properly signed and stamped before finalizing the account creation process.

## Conclusion
The ticket was closed after the user account was created and the application forms were signed and stamped. This process ensures that all necessary steps are followed for account creation at the HPC center.
---

### 2023033042002271_HPC-Infrastruktur%20-%20NVIDIA%20GTX%201080%20Ti%20-%20FAPS.md

### 2021011942002451_Nutzung%20HPC.md
# Ticket 2021011942002451

 # HPC Support Ticket Conversation: Nutzung HPC

## Keywords
- HPC Ressourcen
- Machine Learning Training
- Freikontingent
- HPC-Antrag
- Zugriffsrechte
- HPC Grundversorgung
- idm Account
- Linux Zugriffsberechtigungen

## Summary
A user inquires about using HPC resources for machine learning training without a funded project. The user asks about the possibility of a free quota until the end of April and the process for applying for HPC access.

## Root Cause
- User needs HPC resources for machine learning training.
- User lacks a funded project to cover costs.
- User seeks information on applying for HPC access and the associated permissions.

## Solution
- **HPC Grundversorgung**: The use of HPC systems is free for FAU scientists within the scope of basic HPC services for scientific purposes.
- **HPC Accounts**: Accounts are personalized and linked to individual idm accounts. Each user must submit a separate HPC application.
- **Data Sharing**: Permissions for sharing data between accounts can be managed using standard Linux access rights.
- **Documentation**: Further information on using HPC systems is available in the documentation: [HPC Getting Started](https://www.anleitungen.rrze.fau.de/hpc/getting-started/).

## What Can Be Learned
- HPC resources are available for free to FAU scientists for basic usage.
- Each user needs to submit a separate HPC application.
- Data sharing between accounts can be managed using Linux permissions.
- Detailed usage information is available in the HPC documentation.

## Next Steps
- Users should fill out the HPC application form.
- Users can refer to the HPC documentation for additional guidance.

## References
- [HPC-Antrag Form](https://www.rrze.fau.de/files/2017/06/HPC-Antrag.pdf)
- [HPC Getting Started](https://www.anleitungen.rrze.fau.de/hpc/getting-started/)
---

### 2019080642000576_schlechte%20Performance%20%20der%20Jobs%20auf%20Meggie%20_%20iww2002h.md
# Ticket 2019080642000576

 # HPC Support Ticket: Poor Job Performance on Meggie

## Keywords
- Performance issues
- Job optimization
- KONWIHR project
- Thermal solver development

## Summary
- **User**: Experiencing poor job performance on Meggie.
- **HPC Admin**: Noted significant drop in performance metrics (700 DP-MFlop/s and 600 MB/s memory bandwidth).
- **Root Cause**: Development of a new thermal solver causing temporary performance issues.
- **Solution**: Optimization of the new solver planned for September.

## Details
- **Performance Metrics**:
  - Current jobs: 700 DP-MFlop/s, 600 MB/s memory bandwidth.
  - Previous jobs (June): Better performance but noted for high memory bandwidth.
- **KONWIHR Project**:
  - User had submitted a KONWIHR project proposal but received no feedback.
  - HPC Admin to follow up with KONWIHR colleagues for details.

## Action Items
- **User**: Plan to optimize the new thermal solver by September.
- **HPC Admin**: Follow up with KONWIHR colleagues regarding the project proposal.

## Lessons Learned
- Temporary performance drops can occur during the development of new solvers.
- Regular communication regarding project proposals and performance expectations is crucial.
- Optimization plans should be clearly communicated to HPC Admins to manage expectations.

## References
- Job performance links provided by HPC Admin for detailed analysis.

---

This documentation can be used to address similar performance issues in the future, emphasizing the importance of communication and optimization planning.
