### 2025011042000738_TinyGPU%3A%20Bei%20manchen%20Studierenden%20Jobs%20in%20Queue%20nicht%20direkt%20verarbeitet%20%28RE.md
# Ticket 2025011042000738

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: TinyGPU Job Priority Issue

## Subject
TinyGPU: Bei manchen Studierenden Jobs in Queue nicht direkt verarbeitet (REASON Priority) obwohl zu der Zeit auf TinyGPUs noch GPUs frei sind und das Lehrstuhl-Maximum noch nicht erreicht wurde

## Keywords
- TinyGPU
- Job Priority
- Slurm
- Fairshare
- GPU Allocation
- Job Queue

## Problem Description
Some students reported that their TinyGPU jobs were not processed for several days despite available GPUs and the department's limit not being reached. The reason given was "Priority."

## Root Cause
- The batch system (Slurm) assigns a priority to each waiting job based on parameters like waiting time, partition, user group, and recently used CPU/GPU time (fairshare).
- The students had submitted many short jobs, leading to a decrease in their priority.
- The group had already used a significant amount of GPU hours on TinyGPU, affecting their priority.

## Solution
- The issue is a result of the fairshare mechanism working as designed.
- No individual throttling was applied.
- The high number of short jobs and significant GPU usage led to a lower priority.

## Lessons Learned
- Understanding the fairshare mechanism is crucial for job scheduling.
- Submitting many short jobs can negatively impact job priority.
- High GPU usage by a group can lower their priority.
- There is a possibility to increase cluster resources and improve priority by investing from department funds.

## Additional Notes
- The user acknowledged the peak usage and explained the temporary high demand due to project deadlines.
- The user expressed interest in potential future investments in the HPC cluster to improve priority.
- The HPC Admin suggested that investing in cluster resources could improve priority for the department.
```
---

### 2023070242000031_bcpc001h%20login.md
# Ticket 2023070242000031

 # HPC Support Ticket: bcpc001h Login Issue

## Keywords
- SSH keys
- Login issue
- HPC portal
- SSH client configuration

## Problem
- User unable to log in to HPC system with their credentials (bcpc001h).

## Root Cause
- User had not configured their SSH client with the uploaded SSH keys.

## Solution
- Ensure SSH keys are uploaded to the new HPC portal.
- Configure the SSH client with the uploaded SSH keys.

## General Learnings
- SSH keys are mandatory for logging into HPC systems.
- Users must upload their SSH public keys to the HPC portal.
- Accepted SSH key types: RSA (4096 bits), ECDSA (512 bits), ED25519.
- It may take up to two hours for all HPC systems to recognize updated SSH public keys.
- Documentation and FAQs are available for users unfamiliar with SSH keys.
- Recommended SSH clients for Windows users: OpenSSH built into Windows (Power)Shell or MobaXterm.

## References
- [HPC Portal](https://portal.hpc.fau.de)
- [SSH Documentation](https://hpc.fau.de/systems-services/documentation-instructions/ssh-secure-shell-access-to-hpc-systems/)
- [FAQs](https://hpc.fau.de/faqs/#ID-230)
---

### 2022110742002989_Optimization%20of%20ressource%20usage%20for%20simulation%20analyses%20on%20Fritz%20%5Bp101ae10%5D.md
# Ticket 2022110742002989

 ```markdown
# Optimization of Resource Usage for Simulation Analyses on Fritz

## Keywords
- SLURM
- Parallel Computing
- Job Optimization
- Background Processes
- `srun`
- `gmx_mpi`
- `trjcat`
- `trjconv`

## Problem
- User's simulation analysis jobs were running for a short time and using one node each.
- The `gmx_mpi` calls were running serially, which was inefficient for a parallel computer.

## Root Cause
- The user's scripts were not optimized for parallel execution, leading to underutilization of available CPU cores.

## Solution
- Combine the two trajectory preparation steps into one script per directory.
- Parallelize the `COUNTER` loop over each trajectory part conversion by putting each `srun` into the background with `--oversubscribe`.
- Run the concatenation of trajectory files and final frame skipping in parallel by putting them in the background and ensuring `trjconv` starts only after `trjcat` has finished.
- Use `wait` to ensure that the following lines are executed only after the background processes have terminated.

## Example Script
```bash
#!/bin/bash -l
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node=72    #number of tasks
#SBATCH --time=06:54:00         #time limit of the job, format=hours:minutes:seconds
#SBATCH --export=NONE
#SBATCH --partition=singlenode
#SBATCH --job-name=14-
#SBATCH -o ./run.out
#SBATCH -e ./run.err
#SBATCH --export=NONE

unset SLURM_EXPORT_ENV

module load gromacs/2022-gcc11.2.0-impi-mkl

srun --oversubscribe -n 1 gmx_mpi trjcat -f run1_8-p.xtc run1-9-p.xtc run1-10-p.xtc -o run1_10-p.xtc &
srun --oversubscribe -n 1 gmx_mpi trjcat -f run2_8-p.xtc run2-9-p.xtc run2-10-p.xtc -o run2_10-p.xtc &
srun --oversubscribe -n 1 gmx_mpi trjcat -f run3_8-p.xtc run3-9-p.xtc run3-10-p.xtc -o run3_10-p.xtc &
wait

srun -n 1 gmx_mpi trjconv -f run1_10-p.xtc -skip 100 -o run1_10-100.xtc &
srun -n 1 gmx_mpi trjconv -f run2_10-p.xtc -skip 100 -o run2_10-100.xtc &
srun -n 1 gmx_mpi trjconv -f run3_10-p.xtc -skip 100 -o run3_10-100.xtc &
```

## General Learning
- Optimize job scripts for parallel execution to make better use of available CPU cores.
- Use background processes and `wait` to manage parallel tasks efficiently.
- Combine multiple steps into a single script to streamline workflows.
```
---

### 2022062942004535_Application%20for%20a%20Test%20project%20on%20the%20A100%20GPU%20partition%20of%20Alex%20-%20b123dd%.md
# Ticket 2022062942004535

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket Conversation Summary

## Keywords
- HPC
- GPU
- OpenGadget3
- Compilation
- Linking
- OpenACC
- NVIDIA
- NVHPC
- SSH
- DFN-AAI
- Out-of-Memory
- Runtime Error

## What to Learn Generally
- The conversation highlights various issues encountered during the compilation and runtime of a GPU-accelerated code (OpenGadget3) on the HPC system.
- Key problems include missing preprocessor defines, linking errors, and out-of-memory issues.
- The support team provided detailed guidance on resolving these issues, including specific compiler flags and environment configurations.
- The conversation also touches on user authentication and SSH key management.

## Root Cause of the Problem
- The main issue revolved around missing preprocessor defines and linking errors during the compilation of OpenGadget3 with OpenACC.
- Additionally, out-of-memory errors were encountered during runtime.

## Solution
- The user was advised to switch to a different version of the NVHPC compiler (21.9 instead of 22.5) to resolve compilation issues.
- Specific compiler flags and environment configurations were suggested to address linking errors and runtime issues.
- For SSH key management, the user was advised to ensure the correct public key was used and to re-upload it if necessary.

## Detailed Conversation

### Initial Issue
- The user encountered compilation errors with OpenGadget3 using OpenACC on the HPC system.
- Specifically, the error `nvvmCompileProgram error 9: NVVM_ERROR_COMPILATION` was reported, indicating a missing preprocessor define.

### Support Team Response
- The support team identified that the `ACC_INCLUDE_DENSITY` define was not set, causing the compiler to skip the relevant code.
- They suggested adding the define and provided specific compiler flags to resolve the issue.

### Further Issues
- After resolving the initial compilation error, the user encountered linking errors and out-of-memory issues during runtime.
- The support team provided guidance on adjusting the environment and compiler flags to address these issues.

### SSH Key Management
- The user experienced issues with SSH key authentication, which was resolved by ensuring the correct public key was used and re-uploading it if necessary.

### Final Resolution
- The user successfully compiled and ran the code after following the support team's advice.
- They were also provided with information on monitoring job performance and GPU utilization.

## Conclusion
- The support team effectively resolved the user's issues by providing detailed guidance on compiler flags, environment configurations, and SSH key management.
- The user was able to compile and run the code successfully, and additional monitoring tools were suggested for further performance analysis.
```
---

### 2022012142001927_Early-Alex%20%22Maximilian%20Schmidt%22%20_%20bcpc000h.md
# Ticket 2022012142001927

 # HPC Support Ticket Analysis

## Keywords
- GPGPU cluster 'Alex'
- Nvidia A100 GPGPUs
- Nvidia A40 GPGPUs
- Gromacs 2021.1
- Gromacs 2021.4
- RTX3080 GPUs
- Unbiased MD simulations
- GPCRs
- System size around 80,000 atoms
- Faster computation time
- HPC resources (meggie, tinyGPU rtx nodes)

## Summary
- **User Request:** Access to GPGPU cluster 'Alex' for Nvidia A100 and A40 GPGPUs. Required software: Gromacs 2021.1. Application: Unbiased MD simulations of GPCRs with a system size around 80,000 atoms. Expected outcome: Faster computation time compared to currently used HPC resources.
- **HPC Admin Response:** User granted access to A40 GPGPUs. A100 GPGPUs are not economical for Gromacs. A40 GPGPUs have comparable speed to RTX3080 GPUs. Gromacs 2021.1 is not available; use Gromacs 2021.4 instead.
- **User Follow-up:** User acknowledges the recommendation and agrees to switch to Gromacs 2021.4.

## Root Cause of the Problem
- User requested access to both A100 and A40 GPGPUs without knowing which is more suitable.
- User requested Gromacs 2021.1, which is not available.

## Solution
- HPC Admin granted access to A40 GPGPUs and recommended using Gromacs 2021.4.
- User agreed to use the recommended version of Gromacs.

## General Learnings
- A100 GPGPUs are not economical for Gromacs.
- A40 GPGPUs have comparable speed to RTX3080 GPUs.
- Always check the availability of specific software versions and be prepared to use recommended alternatives.
- Documentation and instructions for the Alex cluster can be found at [Alex Cluster Documentation](https://hpc.fau.de/systems-services/systems-documentation-instructions/clusters/alex-cluster/).
---

### 2024032642002446_AW%3A%20ACL%20f%C3%83%C2%BCr%20Server%20bdlweb.md
# Ticket 2024032642002446

 # HPC-Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

## Subject
AW: ACL für Server bdlweb

## Keywords
- ACL (Access Control List)
- IP Address
- VPN
- HPC
- SSH
- Ports
- Networks

## Summary
A user requested guidance on configuring ACLs for a server to enable SSH access from various networks, including VPN, HPC, and specific university locations. The user also inquired about which networks should be configured for copying data to and from the HPC.

## Root Cause
The user needed to configure ACLs to allow SSH access to the server from specific networks and locations. Additionally, the user required information on which networks should be configured for data transfer between the HPC and the server.

## Solution
- The user's IP address and VPN credentials were provided to the ACL team for SSH access configuration.
- The ACL team recommended opening HTTP/HTTPS ports (80 and 443) globally and provided guidance on SSH access from specific networks.
- The HPC Admin provided a list of relevant HPC networks that should be configured for data transfer.

## Networks for Data Transfer
- Fritz: 10.28.64.0/21
- Alex: 10.28.52.0/23
- Woody: 10.28.244.0/22 + 10.188.82.0/23
- TinyGPU: 10.28.48.0/23 + 10.188.5.0/24
- Meggie: 10.28.24.0/21
- cshpc: 131.188.3.39
- csnhr: 131.188.202.2

## General Learnings
- Users may require guidance on configuring ACLs for specific access needs.
- The ACL team can provide recommendations on which ports to open and from which networks.
- HPC Admins can provide the necessary network information for configuring data transfer between the HPC and other servers.

## Next Steps
- Ensure that the recommended networks are configured for data transfer.
- Verify that the user can access the server via SSH from the specified networks.
- Document the configuration for future reference and troubleshooting.

### 2020042242002235_Hilfe%20bei%20Ausf%C3%83%C2%BChrung%20von%20Python-Skript.md
# Ticket 2020042242002235

 # HPC-Support Ticket Conversation Summary

## Subject: Hilfe bei Ausführung von Python-Skript

### Keywords:
- Python-Skripte
- Memoryhog
- TinyFat Cluster
- SSH
- Python-Packages
- Batch-System
- Resource Monitoring
- Connection Reset
- Threads
- Batch Jobs

### General Learnings:
- **Direct Execution on Memoryhog**: Users can directly execute scripts on memoryhog after logging in via SSH.
- **Resource Monitoring**: Commands like `who`, `top`, and `free -h` can be used to monitor resource usage.
- **Python Package Management**: Modules can be loaded using `module load python/X.X-anaconda`, and additional packages can be installed using `conda` or `pip`.
- **Internet Access**: Some nodes like memoryhog do not have internet access, which can affect package installations.
- **SSH Session Management**: Tools like `screen` or `tmux` can be used to maintain remote sessions even if the SSH connection is lost.
- **Resource Limits**: Processes consuming too many resources may be terminated without notice.
- **Thread Management**: High thread counts can lead to excessive load and process termination.
- **Batch System**: Submitting jobs via the batch system is recommended for scalable and long-term solutions.

### Root Causes and Solutions:
- **Internet Access Issue**:
  - **Problem**: Unable to install Python packages due to lack of internet access on memoryhog.
  - **Solution**: Use a frontend like woody for package installations.

- **Connection Reset**:
  - **Problem**: SSH connection reset due to inactivity or resource limits.
  - **Solution**: Use tools like `screen` or `tmux` to maintain sessions. Reduce resource usage to avoid automatic termination.

- **High Thread Count**:
  - **Problem**: Python processes creating too many threads, leading to high load and process termination.
  - **Solution**: Reduce the number of threads per process or submit jobs via the batch system.

### Recommendations:
- **Resource Management**: Monitor and manage resource usage to avoid process termination.
- **Batch System Usage**: Use the batch system for submitting jobs to ensure scalability and avoid resource conflicts.
- **Thread Management**: Optimize the number of threads per process to reduce load and improve performance.

### Additional Resources:
- **HPC Cafe**: Provides resources and tutorials on tools like `tmux` for remote work.
- **Documentation**: Refer to the HPC documentation for detailed information on resource management and batch system usage.

This summary provides a concise overview of the key points discussed in the support ticket conversation, including general learnings, root causes, solutions, and recommendations for future reference.
---

### 2022061442001351_Re%3A%20Zugriff%20auf%20woody_mpwm_shared.md
# Ticket 2022061442001351

 # HPC Support Ticket: Access to Gurobi Module

## Keywords
- Gurobi
- Access Permissions
- License Management
- Floating License
- Token Server

## Problem
- User requires access to the Gurobi module located in `/home/woody/mpwm/shared/modules`.
- User lacks access permissions to the `/shared` directory.

## Root Cause
- Insufficient access permissions for the user to the required directory.

## Solution
- **Access Permissions**: The HPC Admin granted access to the `/home/woody/mpwm/shared` directory for the group "dsam".
- **Alternative Solution**: User can install Gurobi locally and configure it to use the floating license from the token server.

## Steps for Local Installation
1. Download Gurobi from [Gurobi Website](https://www.gurobi.com/).
2. Obtain an academic license as a student of FAU.
3. Replace the academic license in the `gurobi.lic` file with the following content:
   ```
   TOKENSERVER=lic.mi.uni-erlangen.de
   ```
   This configuration ensures that Gurobi will request a valid license from the token server.

## Conclusion
- Access permissions were successfully granted by the HPC Admin.
- Alternative local installation steps were provided for future reference.

## Notes
- For any access permission issues, users should contact `support-hpc@fau.de`.
- The HPC Admin team includes Thomas Zeiser, Michael Meier, Anna Kahler, Katrin Nusser, and Johannes Veh.
- The 2nd Level Support team consists of Lacey, Dane (fo36fizy), Kuckuk, Sebastian (sisekuck), Lange, Florian (ow86apyf), Ernst, Dominik (te42kyfo), and Mayr, Martin.
- Gerhard Wellein is the Head of the Datacenter, and Georg Hager is the Training and Support Group Leader.
- Harald Lanig handles NHR Rechenzeit Support and Applications for Grants.
- Jan Eitzinger and Gruber are Software and Tools developers.
---

### 2024042442001387_Fw%3A%20HPC%20Cluster%20-%20c102fd11.md
# Ticket 2024042442001387

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

## Keywords
- HPC Cluster
- Queue Management
- File Server Issues
- GPU Performance
- Job Submission Errors
- Data Access During Runs
- Temporary Storage

## General Learnings
- **Queue Management**: `squeue` shows user-specific jobs, while `sinfo` provides information about all nodes.
- **File Server Issues**: Ongoing server issues were acknowledged, and users were advised to contact support for data handling assistance.
- **GPU Performance**: Gromacs performance on A40 GPUs can be better than on A100 GPUs for systems with fewer than 500,000 atoms.
- **Job Submission Errors**: Access to specific clusters (e.g., Meggie) is restricted to certain accounts. Users should check their account permissions and use appropriate clusters.
- **Data Access During Runs**: Users can attach to running jobs and access data in `$TMPDIR`.
- **Temporary Storage**: Users should store simulation data in their `$WORK` directory and important data in `$HOME` for regular backups.

## Root Causes and Solutions
- **Queue Management Misunderstanding**:
  - **Root Cause**: User misunderstood the difference between `squeue` and `sinfo`.
  - **Solution**: Clarified that `squeue` shows user-specific jobs, while `sinfo` provides information about all nodes.

- **File Server Issues**:
  - **Root Cause**: Ongoing server issues.
  - **Solution**: Acknowledged the issue and advised the user to contact support for data handling assistance.

- **GPU Performance**:
  - **Root Cause**: Unexpected performance difference between A40 and A100 GPUs.
  - **Solution**: Explained that A40 GPUs can perform better for smaller systems and provided resources for further optimization.

- **Job Submission Errors**:
  - **Root Cause**: Invalid account or account/partition combination.
  - **Solution**: Clarified that access to Meggie is restricted to Tier3 accounts and suggested using the Fritz cluster for NHR users.

- **Data Access During Runs**:
  - **Root Cause**: User had no permissions to directly login to compute nodes.
  - **Solution**: Advised the user to attach to running jobs and access data in `$TMPDIR`.

- **Temporary Storage**:
  - **Root Cause**: User needed guidance on where to store temporary data.
  - **Solution**: Recommended storing simulation data in the `$WORK` directory and important data in `$HOME` for regular backups.

## Additional Resources
- **Documentation Links**:
  - [Batch Processing with Slurm](https://doc.nhr.fau.de/batch-processing/batch_system_slurm/#manage-and-control-jobs)
  - [Gromacs Performance on Different GPU Types](https://hpc.fau.de/2022/02/10/gromacs-performance-on-different-gpu-types/)
  - [Multiple Walker Metadynamic on Multiple GPUs](https://doc.nhr.fau.de/apps/gromacs/#multiple-walker-metadynamic-on-multiple-gpus-on-tinygpu)
  - [Fritz Cluster Documentation](https://doc.nhr.fau.de/clusters/fritz/)
  - [Attach to a Running Job](https://doc.nhr.fau.de/batch-processing/batch_system_slurm/#attach-to-a-running-job)
  - [Filesystems Documentation](https://doc.nhr.fau.de/data/filesystems/)

## Training and Support
- **Online Introduction**: NHR@FAU offers a two-hour online introduction on using the HPC systems.
  - **Next Session**: Wednesday, May 8, 2024, 4:00 p.m.
  - **Location**: [Zoom Link](https://fau.zoom.us/j/63416831557)
  - **Slides**: [HPC in a Nutshell](https://hpc.fau.de/files/2024/04/2024-04-10_HPC_in_a_Nutshell.pdf)
```
---

### 2024060342003643_Probleme%20mit%20Ansys%20Maxwell%20TDM%20Rechnungen%20auf%20Cluster%20PC.md
# Ticket 2024060342003643

 # HPC Support Ticket: Ansys Maxwell TDM Simulation Issue

## Keywords
- Ansys Maxwell
- TDM Solver
- Shell Script
- SLURM
- Internal Solver Error
- Machine Configuration

## Problem Description
The user is experiencing issues with starting Ansys Maxwell simulations using the TDM solver on cluster PCs. The simulation is initiated via a shell script with the following content:

```bash
#!/bin/bash -l
#SBATCH --job-name=Rechnung_Maxwell
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=32
#SBATCH --time=24:00:00
#SBATCH --export=NONE
#SBATCH --mail-user=marcel.heilmann@thws.de
unset SLURM_EXPORT_ENV
# load environment module
echo 'Loading ansysedt...'
module load ansysedt/2022R2
# execute maxwell with command line parameters
echo 'Executing ansysedt...'
ansysedt -machinelist list="localhost:32:32:90%" -scriptargs Target -runscriptandexit ./Ansteuerung_Maxwell_vs_Python.py -ng ./Maxwellmodel.aedt
```

The error message received is:

```
[error] Project: Maxwellmodel, Design:Target (Transient, XY), Maxwell2d solver, process solver2d error: Internal Solver Error:  'Periodic or Half-periodic Time Decomposition Method request the active machine configuration to support distributing transient solutions. Please make sure more than 1 tasks in transient solver level and "Transient solver" option is checked.'. Please contact Ansys technical support.
```

## Root Cause
The error suggests that the machine configuration does not support distributing transient solutions for the TDM solver. The user needs to ensure that more than one task is defined in the transient solver level and that the "Transient solver" option is checked.

## Solution
The HPC Admin suggested contacting the software vendor for further assistance, as they lack specific experience with this issue. The user should reach out to Ansys technical support or the software provider (support@cadfem.de) for detailed guidance on configuring the machine list and solver options.

## General Learning
- Ensure proper configuration of machine lists and solver options when using TDM solver in Ansys Maxwell.
- For complex software-specific issues, it is often necessary to consult the software vendor's support team.
- SLURM job scripts should be carefully reviewed to ensure they meet the requirements of the software being used.

## Next Steps
- The user should contact Ansys technical support for assistance with the machine configuration and solver options.
- The HPC Admin can document this issue for future reference and provide guidance on contacting software vendors for specialized support.
---

# HPC-Support Ticket: Problem beim Einloggen

## Keywords
- Login issue
- Account not available
- Certificate expired
- NHR@FAU HPC Portal
- $HOME, $WORK, $HPCVAULT directories

### 2024051342003322_Unable%20to%20connect%20to%20Alex%20or%20Fritz%20clusters.md
# Ticket 2024051342003322

 # HPC Support Ticket Analysis: Unable to Connect to Alex or Fritz Clusters

## Keywords
- ProxyJump
- SSH
- Connection Issue
- Debug Information
- Exit Status 1
- Broken Pipe
- Account Availability

## Summary
A user reported being unable to connect to the Alex or Fritz clusters via ProxyJump. The user could connect to `csnhr` but faced issues connecting further to Alex or Fritz. The debug information provided indicated a connection closure with the message "This account is currently not available."

## Root Cause
The root cause of the problem appears to be related to account availability. The debug log shows the message "This account is currently not available," suggesting that the user's account might be temporarily disabled or restricted.

## Debug Information
```
debug1: client_input_channel_req: channel 0 rtype exit-status reply 0
debug1: client_input_channel_req: channel 0 rtype eow@openssh.com reply 0
debug2: channel 0: rcvd eow
debug2: chan_shutdown_read: channel 0: (i0 o1 sock -1 wfd 4 efd 8 [write])
debug2: channel 0: input open -> closed
debug2: channel 0: rcvd close
This account is currently not available.
debug2: channel 0: obuf empty
debug2: chan_shutdown_write: channel 0: (i3 o1 sock -1 wfd 7 efd 8 [write])
debug2: channel 0: output drain -> closed
debug2: channel 0: almost dead
debug2: channel 0: gc: notify user
debug2: channel 0: gc: user detached
debug2: channel 0: send close
debug2: channel 0: is dead
debug2: channel 0: garbage collecting
debug1: channel 0: free: client-session, nchannels 1
Connection to alex.nhr.fau.de closed.
Transferred: sent 2376, received 5336 bytes, in 0.0 seconds
Bytes per second: sent 57244.2, received 128558.5
debug1: Exit status 1
debug2: channel 0: read failed rfd 5 maxlen 32768: Broken pipe
debug2: channel 0: read failed
debug2: chan_shutdown_read: channel 0: (i0 o0 sock -1 wfd 5 efd -1 [closed])
debug2: channel 0: input open -> drain
debug1: compress outgoing: raw data 408, compressed 273, factor 0.67
debug2: channel 0: ibuf empty
debug2: channel 0: send eof
debug1: compress incoming: raw data 7716, compressed 3513, factor 0.46
debug2: channel 0: input drain -> closed
debug2: channel 0: rcvd eof
debug2: channel 0: output open -> drain
debug2: channel 0: obuf empty
debug2: chan_shutdown_write: channel 0: (i3 o1 sock -1 wfd 6 efd -1 [closed])
debug2: channel 0: output drain -> closed
debug2: channel 0: rcvd close
debug2: channel 0: almost dead
debug2: channel 0: gc: notify user
debug1: stdio forwarding: done
```

## Solution
- **Check Account Status:** Verify the status of the user's account on the Alex and Fritz clusters. Ensure the account is active and not temporarily disabled.
- **Contact HPC Admins:** If the account status is unclear, contact HPC Admins (Thomas, Michael Meier, Anna Kahler, Katrin Nusser, Johannes Veh) for further assistance.
- **Review Portal Conditions:** Ensure that the user's conditions in the portal are correctly set and do not restrict access to the clusters.

## Notes
- The HPC Admin expressed frustration with the level of user issues, indicating a potential need for improved user training or documentation.
- The ticket was not resolved by the initial HPC Admin and was left for another team member to address.

## Next Steps
- **Follow Up:** Ensure the user's account status is checked and resolved.
- **Documentation:** Update documentation to include common connection issues and their resolutions.
- **Training:** Consider additional training sessions for users to understand common issues and troubleshooting steps.
---

### 2018010842001871_Xilinx%20Vivado%20Cluster.md
# Ticket 2018010842001871

 # HPC-Support Ticket: Xilinx Vivado Cluster

## Keywords
- Xilinx Vivado

- Docker

- User requires significant computational resources for hardware synthesis using Xilinx Vivado.
- Process is compute-intensive, taking up to 20-30 minutes for medium-complexity designs.
- User inquires about the existence of a Xilinx Vivado Cluster.
- User suggests using Docker or other OS-level virtualization technologies.

- HPC Admin inquires about the type of license (node-locked or floating) and the number of simultaneous syntheses.

- No existing Xilinx Vivado Cluster is known.
- User needs to provide additional information about the license type, number of simultaneous syntheses, and resource requirements.
- The feasibility of using Docker or other virtualization technologies depends on the provided information.

## Action Items
- User needs to provide more details about the license type and resource requirements.
- HPC team will evaluate the feasibility of setting up a Xilinx Vivado Cluster or using Docker based on the provided information.

## Conclusion
- The ticket is pending further information from the user.
- The HPC team is waiting for additional details to proceed with a solution.

---

### 2024020542003763_HPC%20Verl%C3%83%C2%A4ngerung.md
# Ticket 2024020542003763

 # HPC Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

## Keywords
- HPC Account Extension
- Data Transfer
- SSH Key Issue
- HPC-Cafe
- Documentation
- Password Setup

## General Learnings
- **Account Extension**: Users may request extensions for their HPC accounts.
- **Data Transfer**: Data can be transferred between accounts, but accounts themselves cannot be moved between chairs.
- **SSH Key Issue**: SSH keys may need to be re-uploaded if an account is deactivated and reactivated.
- **HPC-Cafe**: A resource for new users to get help and learn about the HPC environment.
- **Documentation**: Important for users to refer to for setting up and troubleshooting their accounts.

## Root Causes and Solutions

### Root Cause: Account Extension Request
- **User Request**: Extension of an HPC account.
- **Solution**: HPC Admin created a new account (iwso122h) and provided instructions for setting up the password and initializing services.

### Root Cause: Data Transfer Request
- **User Request**: Transfer of specific directories from an old account to a new one.
- **Solution**: HPC Admin initiated the data transfer process and provided the location of the copied data.

### Root Cause: SSH Key Issue
- **User Issue**: Unable to log in due to "Permission denied (publickey)" error.
- **Solution**: HPC Admin confirmed no SSH key was uploaded and provided a link to user debugging tips for resolving the issue.

## Additional Notes
- **HPC-Cafe**: Recommended for new users to attend for additional support and learning opportunities.
- **Documentation**: Users should refer to the provided documentation for detailed instructions on setting up and using their HPC accounts.

## Conclusion
This conversation highlights the importance of clear communication between users and HPC support, as well as the availability of resources like the HPC-Cafe and documentation for troubleshooting common issues.
---

### 2023100242000347_SSH%20Access%20Issue%20to%20tinyx.nhr.fau.de.md
# Ticket 2023100242000347

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: SSH Access Issue to tinyx.nhr.fau.de

## Keywords
- SSH Access Issue
- Account Expiration
- Validity Extension
- Error 500

## Problem Description
- **User Issue**: Unable to SSH into the HPC server `tinyx.nhr.fau.de`.
- **Error Message**:
  ```
  Last login: Sun Oct  1 22:10:43 2023 from cshpc.rrze.uni-erlangen.de
  This account is currently not available.
  Connection to tinyx.nhr.fau.de closed.
  ```

## Root Cause
- The user's HPC account had expired at the end of September.

## Solution
- **HPC Admin**: Informed the user that their account had expired and that Mr. Mai could extend the validity.
- **2nd Level Support**: Attempted to extend the account validity but encountered an error (Status: 500).
- **HPC Admin**: Confirmed the error was on their side and resolved it. The account `barz108h` was correctly extended until `2023-12-31 00:00`.

## General Learnings
- Account expiration can cause SSH access issues.
- Extending account validity can resolve such issues.
- Error 500 during validity extension may indicate an internal system error that requires HPC Admin intervention.

## Steps for Similar Issues
1. Check if the user's account has expired.
2. If expired, extend the account validity.
3. If an error occurs during the extension process, escalate to HPC Admin for resolution.
4. Confirm the account validity extension and verify SSH access.
```
---

### 2024103142002851_Powerlimits%20%C3%83%C2%A4ndern%20am%20Wochenende.md
# Ticket 2024103142002851

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: Power Limits Change on Weekend

## Keywords
- Power limits
- Reservation
- H100 Node
- Tinygpu
- Benchmarks
- `nvidia-smi`
- `sudoers.d`

## Summary
A user requested a reservation for an H100 node on Tinygpu and changes to the power limits for benchmarking purposes.

## Root Cause
- User needed to explicitly request the reservation with `--reservation=bench`.
- User needed guidance on setting and resetting power limits using `nvidia-smi`.

## Solution
- **Reservation**: The user was instructed to request the reservation explicitly with `--reservation=bench`.
- **Power Limits**: The user was informed that they could set power limits using `sudo /usr/bin/nvidia-smi --power-limit=...` and reset them after use.
- **Frequency Setting**: The user was also informed about setting frequencies using `sudo /usr/bin/nvidia-smi --applications-clocks=...,...`.

## Actions Taken
- The `sudoers.d` entry was deleted.
- The power limit of the H100 node was reset to 700W.
- The reservation was deleted.

## General Learnings
- Always explicitly request reservations with the correct parameters.
- Users can set and reset power limits and frequencies on nodes using `nvidia-smi`.
- Ensure to reset any changes made to power limits or frequencies to avoid issues for other users.
```
---

### 2024120942003175_jobs%20on%20Fritz%20using%20only%201%20core%20%5Bv116bb10%5D.md
# Ticket 2024120942003175

 # HPC Support Ticket: Jobs Using Only 1 Core

## Problem Description
- **Issue**: Jobs on Fritz using only 1 of the 72 available cores per node.
- **User Observation**: CPU load visible in ClusterCockpit.
- **Additional Concern**: Restart mechanism submitting multiple new jobs at the end, possibly unintentional.

## Communication
- **Initial Contact**: HPC Admin notifies user about the issue.
- **User Response**: Confirms potential problem with job file and restart mechanism.

## Diagnostic Steps
- **CPU Utilization**: Checked via ClusterCockpit.
- **Restart Mechanism**: User confirms break statement in script but inconsistent behavior observed.

## Root Cause
- **CPU Utilization**: Code not utilizing multiple cores.
- **Restart Mechanism**: Inconsistent job resubmission behavior.

## Solution
- **Parallelization**:
  - User added `#pragma omp parallel for` before non-sequential loops.
  - HPC Admin suggests adding `export OMP_NUM_THREADS=$SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK` before simulation start.
  - Recommended setting `--cpus-per-task=72` for full node utilization.

- **Job Script**:
  - Break statement confirmed as sufficient.
  - Output file handling clarified: `mv out.out $filename` moves output, creating a new `out.out` with end message.

## Follow-Up
- **Testing**: User to test with suggested changes.
- **Further Assistance**: HPC Admin offers to review code if necessary.

## Resources
- **ClusterCockpit**: [Job Monitoring with ClusterCockpit](https://doc.nhr.fau.de/job-monitoring-with-clustercockpit/)
- **Contact**: HPC Support (support-hpc@fau.de)

## Keywords
- CPU utilization
- Job restart mechanism
- OpenMP parallelization
- Job script
- ClusterCockpit
- SLURM

## Conclusion
- **CPU Utilization**: Ensure code is parallelized and job script requests appropriate resources.
- **Restart Mechanism**: Verify job script logic for consistent resubmission behavior.
---

### 2023091342001015_CPU%20nodes%20on%20tinygpu.md
# Ticket 2023091342001015

 # HPC Support Ticket: CPU Nodes on tinygpu

## Keywords
- Snakemake
- tinygpu
- CPU jobs
- Submitter node
- Slurm
- TinyFat

## Problem
- User is using Snakemake to submit jobs on tinygpu.
- Some parts of the workflow do not require GPU.
- Snakemake does not natively support connecting to another submit node to submit jobs.
- User wants to avoid overloading the submitter node.

## Solution
- From the frontend `tinyx`, jobs can be submitted to both TinyGPU and the CPU-only cluster TinyFat.
- Use wrapper scripts (e.g., `sbatch.tinyfat`) or the Slurm option `-M tinyfat` to submit jobs.
- The AMD Rome nodes in the "work" partition are allocated in core-granularity, similar to woody.

## General Learning
- Understanding how to submit CPU-only jobs from a GPU cluster.
- Utilizing Slurm options and wrapper scripts for job submission.
- Managing workflow managers like Snakemake in an HPC environment.

## References
- [TinyFat Cluster Documentation](https://hpc.fau.de/systems-services/documentation-instructions/clusters/tinyfat-cluster/)
---

- Provide clear instructions and links to the necessary forms.

### 2024061042001319_Running%20out%20of%20memory%20in%20the%20job.md
# Ticket 2024061042001319

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: Running out of memory in the job

## Problem Description
- User is running a batch job that iterates through `tar.bz.2` archives and converts them into parquet archives.
- The process is killed for larger archives (over 200MB before conversion, around 500MB to 1.2GB after conversion).
- The job is flagged with 'out of memory' errors.

## Root Cause
- Insufficient memory allocation for the job.
- Use of `/tmp` for unpacking, which is a ramdisk, increases memory footprint.

## Solutions Attempted
1. **Change from `/tmp` to `$TMPDIR`**:
   - Initially suggested to reduce memory footprint.
   - Resulted in slower processing times.

2. **Increase Memory Allocation**:
   - Allocate a job with 2 tasks (`--ntasks 2`) to double the memory.
   - Solved the 'out of memory' problem.

## Key Learnings
- **Memory Allocation**:
  - On the `woody` cluster, each job gets a fraction of the available memory.
  - Allocating more tasks increases the available memory.

- **Temporary Directory Usage**:
  - `/tmp` is often a ramdisk, increasing memory usage.
  - `$TMPDIR` points to a fast local disk, reducing memory footprint but may result in slower processing times.

- **Monitoring**:
  - The monitoring system takes samples every minute, which may not capture peak memory usage.

## Final Solution
- Increase memory allocation by using more tasks (`--ntasks 2`) to handle larger archives without running out of memory.
- Use `/tmp` for faster processing if memory allows, otherwise use `$TMPDIR` to reduce memory footprint.

## Additional Notes
- The user will rerun tests to confirm the performance difference between `/tmp` and `$TMPDIR`.
- The sysadmins will review the job IDs for further insights.
```
---

### 2023022342003614_%21%21HPC%20Application%21%21.md
# Ticket 2023022342003614

 ```markdown
# HPC Application Process

## Keywords
- HPC Application
- Account Creation
- HPC Systems (Woody, TinyGPU, TinyFAT)
- Job Size (multi-node 32 cores)
- Computing Time (20 hours)
- Memory Space (1 TB)
- PhD Work
- IdM
- RRZE
- HPC-Café
- Documentation

## General Learnings
- **Application Process**: Users submit HPC applications through a contact form, which is then processed by HPC Admins.
- **Account Creation**: HPC Admins create accounts based on the provided IdM and forward the application to the RRZE service desk.
- **Queue Management**: HPC applications should be moved to the RRZE::Servicetheken queue for processing.
- **Namensraum**: Accounts are created under specific namensraum (e.g., iwso or empk) based on the provided customer number.
- **User Notification**: Users are notified via email about their account creation and provided with instructions for setting up their password and accessing HPC resources.
- **Support Resources**: Users are encouraged to participate in the HPC-Café and utilize the provided documentation for getting started with HPC systems.

## Root Cause of the Problem
- **Clarification Needed**: There was a need to clarify which namensraum (iwso or empk) the account should be created under.

## Solution
- **Account Creation**: The account was created under the iwso namensraum based on the provided customer number.
- **User Instructions**: Detailed instructions were provided to the user for setting up their account and accessing HPC resources.
```

## Keywords

- Dokumentation

- HPC Admins created HPC IDs for each user and provided instructions for setting up passwords and accessing services.
- Users were invited to the HPC-Cafe and online introduction for new users.

- New users required HPC access and resources.

## Solution
- HPC Admins created HPC IDs for each user.
- Users were instructed to set up passwords using their IdM-Kennung.
- Users were advised to wait up to 2 days for services to be fully set up on HPC servers.
- Users were invited to the HPC-Cafe and online introduction for further assistance.
- Relevant documentation was shared for getting started with HPC resources.

## Notes
- The ticket was initially deferred and later closed after all users were provided with the necessary information and HPC IDs.
- The next HPC-Cafe was announced as a hybrid event.
- Users were assigned to specific clusters (woody, tinyFAT, tinyGPU).

## Follow-up
- Users should follow the provided instructions to set up their passwords and access HPC services.
- Users are encouraged to attend the HPC-Cafe and online introduction for additional support.

### 2023032942003361_Fwd%3A%20Manager%20acces%20granted%20for%22Lg_SurfCat_AIMD_MLFF%20-%20Computational%20modeling%20of%.md
# Ticket 2023032942003361

 # HPC Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

## Keywords
- NHR-Portal
- Project Invitation
- Account Generation
- Cluster Access
- SSH Keys Distribution
- Directory Availability

## General Learnings
- When a user is invited to a project via the NHR-Portal, an HPC account is generated on the fly.
- Access to clusters and directories ($HOME, $WORK, $HPCVAULT) typically takes until the day after accepting the invitation.
- SSH keys from the portal are distributed automatically across HPC systems, which may take several hours (typically 2 hours).
- Each NHR project has a separate storage area and user/group settings, preventing the linking of existing HPC accounts to new projects.

## Root Cause of the Problem
- The user was unable to log in to the "Fritz" cluster immediately after accepting the project invitation.

## Solution
- Wait for the account generation and directory setup process to complete, which usually takes until the day after accepting the invitation.
- Ensure that SSH keys have been distributed, which may take up to 2 hours.

## Documentation for Support Employees
When troubleshooting login issues after a user accepts a project invitation, advise the user to wait for the account generation and directory setup process to complete. This process typically takes until the day after accepting the invitation. Additionally, ensure that SSH keys have been distributed, which may take up to 2 hours. Inform the user that each NHR project has a separate storage area and user/group settings, preventing the linking of existing HPC accounts to new projects.
---

### 2023121142002986_Performance%20measures%20missing%20on%20monitoring.nhr.fau.de.md
# Ticket 2023121142002986

 # HPC Support Ticket: Performance Measures Missing on Monitoring

## Keywords
- Performance measurements
- Roofline diagram
- ClusterCockpit
- LIKWID
- `#SBATCH --constraint=hwperf`

## Problem Description
The user noticed that performance measurements and roofline diagrams were not available for recent simulations on the monitoring website.

## Root Cause
The job script included the constraint `#SBATCH --constraint=hwperf`, which prevented the monitoring system (ClusterCockpit) from collecting certain data.

## Solution
Remove the `#SBATCH --constraint=hwperf` line from the job script for simulations where LIKWID measurements are not planned.

## General Learning
- The monitoring system relies on LIKWID for performance data.
- Certain constraints in the job script can interfere with data collection.
- Adjust job scripts based on the intended use of performance monitoring tools.

## Ticket Status
The issue was resolved, and the ticket was closed.
---

### 2021080342003574_efficiency%20of%20jobs%20on%20TinyGPU%20-%20581321.md
# Ticket 2021080342003574

 # HPC Support Ticket: Efficiency of Jobs on TinyGPU - 581321

## Keywords
- GPU utilization
- CPU-intensive phases
- GPU-intensive phases
- Job efficiency
- `nvidia-smi`
- Memory management

## Problem Description
- The user's job alternates between GPU-intensive and CPU-intensive phases.
- CPU-intensive phases are not parallelizable and cannot be parallelized between each other.
- Long periods of no GPU utilization observed in system monitoring.

## Root Cause
- The job's workload alternates between GPU and CPU-intensive tasks, leading to periods of low GPU utilization.

## Discussion
- HPC Admin informed the user about the low GPU utilization phases.
- User explained the alternating nature of the job's workload.
- User suggested potential solutions such as creating separate processes for GPU and CPU tasks or conceding GPU access during CPU-intensive phases.

## Solution
- No immediate solution was implemented as the job's structure limits optimization options.
- HPC Admin acknowledged the limitations and understood that significant improvements might not be feasible.

## Learnings
- Monitoring GPU utilization using `nvidia-smi` can help identify inefficiencies.
- Understanding the job's workload characteristics is crucial for optimizing resource usage.
- Some jobs may have inherent limitations that prevent full optimization of GPU resources.

## Next Steps
- Users should consider job restructuring or advanced memory management techniques if feasible.
- HPC Admins can provide guidance on best practices for job optimization and resource management.
---

### 2023122242001288_VASP%20calculations%20slow%20down%20randomly%20-%20b163cb15.md
# Ticket 2023122242001288

 ```markdown
# VASP Calculations Slow Down Randomly

## Problem Description
- User observed that some VASP calculations slow down significantly from a random point in the calculations.
- Jobs are not able to finish within 24 hours even though they should.
- Gflops go down to zero as soon as the problem occurs, even if the CPU load stays constant.
- Example JobID: 1086731.

## Root Cause
- High I/O load on the file server.
- Large files (CHG and CHGCAR) being written at the end of every SCF.
- Possible issues with the VASP binary file (vasp_gam).
- HDF5 files (vaspout.h5) causing additional I/O load and potential bugs.

## Solutions Attempted
1. **Set `LWAVE = .FALSE` and `LCHARG = .FALSE` in INCAR file:**
   - Reduces the probability of encountering I/O problems by not writing large CHG files.
   - Did not solve the problem completely.

2. **Use `vasp_std` instead of `vasp_gam`:**
   - Gflop breakdown seemed to be less likely but still occurred in some calculations.
   - Introduced another error related to HDF5 calls.

3. **Disable HDF5 support in VASP binary:**
   - Eliminated the error connected to I/O of HDF5 files but did not solve the Gflop breakdown issue.

4. **Test different versions of VASP and compilers:**
   - None of the tries were completely successful, but they had some impact on the behavior of VASP.

## Additional Observations
- The problem seems to be related to high load on the file server and I/O operations.
- Other users performing MD simulations with VASP do not encounter the problem.
- The issue might be specific to the tungsten oxide system and the POTCAR file used.

## Next Steps
- Investigate the possibility of running calculations on Lustre to reduce I/O load.
- Consider using a different POTCAR file with semi-core electrons (POTCAR_W_sv_LDA) to see if it resolves the issue.
- Continue monitoring and testing to gather more data on the problem.

## Conclusion
- The issue is complex and may involve multiple factors, including I/O load, VASP binary, and specific system configurations.
- Further investigation and testing are required to identify a definitive solution.
```
---

### 42133495_problem%20with%20simultaneously%20running%20jobs.md
# Ticket 42133495

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: Problem with Simultaneously Running Jobs

## Keywords
- Turbomole jobs
- Simultaneous job execution
- Job crashes
- Power saving mode error
- Temporary file corruption
- kill_user.sh script

## Problem Description
- User experiences crashes when running two Turbomole jobs simultaneously.
- Error message: `/var/spool/PBS/mom_priv/epilogue: line 62: echo: write error: Invalid argument`.
- Jobs finish properly when run one by one.

## Root Cause
- Initially suspected to be due to temporary file corruption when running jobs from the same directory.
- Later identified as a configuration error in the `kill_user.sh` script, causing all processes on a node to be killed if one exits.

## Solution
- HPC Admin identified and fixed the configuration error in the `kill_user.sh` script.
- User confirmed that the problem was resolved after the fix.

## Lessons Learned
- Power saving mode error messages can be safely ignored as they do not affect job execution.
- Running multiple jobs from the same directory can lead to temporary file corruption.
- Configuration errors in scripts like `kill_user.sh` can cause unexpected job terminations.
- Always check for configuration errors when troubleshooting job crashes.
```
---

### 2021072942002091_Zus%C3%83%C2%A4tzliche%20HPC-Accounts.md
# Ticket 2021072942002091

 # HPC Support Ticket: Additional HPC Accounts

## Summary
- **Subject:** Additional HPC Accounts
- **User Request:** Additional HPC accounts for two other students with similar configurations to an existing account.
- **Issues:**
  - Validity of an unstamped document for the application.
  - Request for a customer number for the research project "KLÄFFIZIENT".

## Keywords
- HPC Accounts
- TinyGPU Cluster
- Application Process
- Customer Number
- IDM (Identity Management)
- HPC-Cafe

## Conversation Highlights
- **User:** Requested additional HPC accounts for two students with configurations similar to an existing account.
- **HPC Admin:** Confirmed that an unstamped document is acceptable since the user is a registered contact person in IDM. Provided the customer number `ilev000` for the research project.
- **User:** Sent the application documents as attachments.
- **HPC Admin:** Confirmed the creation of HPC accounts `ilev003h` and `ilev004h`. Provided instructions for setting up passwords and initializing services.

## Learnings
- **Document Validity:** Unstamped documents can be accepted if the user is a registered contact person in IDM.
- **Customer Number:** The customer number `ilev000` can be used for the research project "KLÄFFIZIENT".
- **Account Setup:** New HPC accounts require password setup through IDM and may take up to 2 days for initial service setup.
- **HPC-Cafe:** Recommended for new HPC users, held every second Tuesday of the month.

## Root Cause and Solution
- **Root Cause:** User needed additional HPC accounts and clarification on the application process.
- **Solution:** HPC Admin provided guidance on the application process, accepted unstamped documents, and created the requested HPC accounts.

## Additional Notes
- **HPC-Cafe:** A resource for new HPC users, held every second Tuesday of the month. Details available on the [HPC-Cafe website](https://hpc.fau.de/services/hpc-cafe/).
- **IDM:** Used for setting up passwords and initializing services for new HPC accounts.

## Conclusion
The user successfully obtained additional HPC accounts for their students with the help of the HPC Admin. The process involved accepting unstamped documents and providing a customer number for the research project. The HPC-Cafe was recommended as a resource for new users.
---

- User unable to get a shell on the testcluster node gracesup1.
- Node allocation with `salloc` works, but no terminal is spawned.
- SSH is disallowed to the node.
- `srun --jobid --pty bash` does not work.
- Non-interactive jobs do not execute and are cancelled after the time limit.
- No `.bashrc` or `.profile` present, ruling out issues with sourced files.
- Other accounts can connect to the node without problems.
- User can connect to other nodes without issues.

## Troubleshooting Steps
- User provided a screenshot of the terminal session and job script output.
- HPC Admins restarted the node gracesup1.

## Solution
- Restarting the node resolved the issue.

## Lessons Learned
- Node restarts can resolve issues with shell access and job execution.
- Ensure to check node-specific issues when other accounts and nodes are functioning correctly.
- Quick response from HPC Admins can resolve user issues efficiently.
```

### 2024041542000832_Assistance%20Needed%3A%20Dataset%20Download%20and%20Storage%20Limitation%20Issue.md
# Ticket 2024041542000832

 # HPC Support Ticket: Dataset Download and Storage Limitation Issue

## Keywords
- Dataset download
- Storage limitation
- Vimeo Triplet dataset
- $WORK directory
- Node-local SSD
- wget command

## Problem
- User unable to download and unzip a large dataset (Vimeo Triplet dataset, 33GB zipped, 47GB unzipped) due to storage limitations on their end.
- User requested assistance to download and unzip the dataset directly on the HPC server.

## Root Cause
- User's local storage limitations prevented them from handling the large dataset.

## Solution
- **Download**: Use the `wget` command to download the dataset directly from the cluster frontend nodes (tinyx) into the user's `$WORK` directory.
- **Storage**: Do not unpack the dataset onto `$WORK`, `$HOME`, or `$HPCVAULT` due to performance and storage constraints.
- **Unpacking**: Unpack the zip file directly to the node-local SSD at the start of the job to improve performance and avoid network filesystem issues.

## References
- [Dataset Information](http://toflow.csail.mit.edu/)
- [Data Management Guidelines](https://doc.nhr.fau.de/data/datasets/)
- [Staging Data](https://doc.nhr.fau.de/data/staging/)

## Additional Notes
- Ensure users are aware of the best practices for handling large datasets on the HPC server to optimize performance and storage usage.
- Provide clear instructions and references for users to follow when dealing with similar issues in the future.
---

### 2023090542002244_Fritz%3A%20long%20waiting%20times%20and%20warnings%20with%20spack%20-%20a105cb10.md
# Ticket 2023090542002244

 ```markdown
# HPC-Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

## Subject
Fritz: long waiting times and warnings with spack - a105cb10

## Keywords
- Spack
- Long waiting times
- Missing dependency warnings
- Configuration errors
- Fileserver load
- Missing packages

## Summary
A user reported long waiting times and numerous warnings when using Spack on Fritz. The warnings indicated missing dependencies not found in the database. Additionally, configuration errors were encountered during the installation process.

## Root Cause
- **Fileserver Load**: High load on the fileserver due to user activities.
- **Missing Packages**: Some packages were missing from the Spack database, causing dependency warnings.
- **Configuration Issues**: Configuration errors in the `modules.yaml` file.

## Solution
- **Fileserver Load**: HPC Admins identified the issue and advised that the high load was due to user activities. No immediate solution was provided, but the issue was acknowledged.
- **Missing Packages**: HPC Admins confirmed that some packages were missing and advised to ignore the warnings unless they caused hard errors. Rebuilding the dependent packages was suggested as a potential solution.
- **Configuration Issues**: HPC Admins mentioned recent changes to the configuration files to address issues with Spack 0.19.1. The specific error in `modules.yaml` could not be reproduced, but ongoing efforts to stabilize the configuration were noted.

## General Learnings
- High load on the fileserver can cause slow response times for Spack operations.
- Missing dependencies in the Spack database can lead to warnings and potential installation issues.
- Configuration changes in Spack can introduce new errors, requiring ongoing maintenance and updates.
- Regular monitoring and maintenance of the Spack environment are essential to ensure stability and performance.
```
---

### 2025021442001013_Error%20on%20Alex%20after%20cluster%20maintenance.md
# Ticket 2025021442001013

 # HPC Support Ticket: Error on Alex after Cluster Maintenance

## Keywords
- Alex cluster
- Job submission error
- Cluster maintenance
- sbatch error
- Node configuration
- Partition a100
- GPU A100

## Summary
A user encountered an error when submitting jobs to the Alex cluster after a maintenance period. The error message indicated that the requested node configuration was not available. The user's script, which had previously worked, was unchanged.

## Root Cause
- The cluster maintenance likely resulted in changes to the node configurations or availability, causing the job submission to fail.

## User Script
```bash
#!/bin/bash -l
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --partition=a100
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:a100:1
#SBATCH --time=24:00:00
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8
#SBATCH --export=NONE
```

## Solution
- The user should check the current node configurations and availability on the Alex cluster.
- The user should verify if the partition `a100` and the GPU type `a100` are still available and correctly configured.
- If necessary, the user should update the script to match the current cluster configuration.

## Next Steps
- HPC Admins should investigate the changes made during the maintenance period.
- 2nd Level Support team should assist the user in verifying the current cluster configuration and updating the script if needed.

## General Learning
- Cluster maintenance can affect job submission due to changes in node configurations.
- Users should be informed about potential impacts of maintenance on their job scripts.
- Regular updates on cluster configurations should be communicated to users to avoid submission errors.
---

### 2023091442002816_HPC%20Account%20an%20Promotion%20kn%C3%83%C2%BCpfen.md
# Ticket 2023091442002816

 ```markdown
# HPC Account Retention During Job Transition

## Keywords
- HPC Account
- Promotion Status
- IDM Account
- Job Transition
- Account Retention

## Problem
A user is changing their job position but will continue to pursue their PhD at the university. They want to know if their HPC account can be retained.

## Root Cause
The user is concerned about losing access to their HPC account due to a job transition.

## Solution
The HPC account is linked to the user's promotion status. As long as the user maintains a valid IDM account and promotion status, the HPC account will remain active.

## What Can Be Learned
- HPC accounts can be retained during job transitions if the user continues to meet the necessary criteria (valid IDM account and promotion status).
- Users should verify their account status and requirements with the HPC support team to ensure continued access.

## Actions Taken
- The HPC Admin confirmed that the user's account is linked to their promotion status.
- The user was informed that their account will remain active as long as they have a valid IDM account and promotion status.
- The support ticket was closed after the user acknowledged the information.
```
---

### 42040484_altix.md
# Ticket 42040484

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: Altix MPI Issue

## Keywords
- Altix
- MPI
- mpirun
- libarray.so
- Special queue
- MOTD

## Summary
A user reported that the Altix system is running again but encountered an error when using `mpirun`:
```
mpirun -np 6 lesocc
MPI: Could not dlopen libarray.so
```

## Root Cause
The root cause of the problem is the missing or inaccessible `libarray.so` library required by MPI.

## HPC Admin Response
- The Altix system is not yet officially released for general use.
- The user's jobs are running because they have access to a special queue used for testing.
- Users should check the Message of the Day (MOTD) for system status updates.
- The issue with `libarray.so` may not be immediately resolvable.

## Solution
- Users should avoid submitting jobs until the system is officially released.
- Check the MOTD for updates on system readiness.
- The HPC Admin is working on resolving the issue but cannot guarantee a quick fix.

## General Learning
- Always check the MOTD for system status updates.
- Be aware of special queues and their purpose.
- Issues with shared libraries (e.g., `libarray.so`) can cause MPI errors.
```
---

### 2024071042002432_IO%20Burst%20on%20alex%20%5Bb204dc11%5D.md
# Ticket 2024071042002432

 # HPC Support Ticket: IO Burst on alex

## Keywords
- IO Burst
- Checkpoint Frequency
- Disk Writing
- Job Optimization

## Problem Description
- **Root Cause:** Frequent checkpoint writing causing IO bursts.
- **Symptoms:** Burst-like IO pattern observed in running jobs.

## Conversation Summary
- **HPC Admin:** Noticed frequent checkpoint writing (every ~3 minutes) causing IO bursts.
- **User:** Acknowledged the issue and agreed to reduce checkpoint frequency.

## Solution
- **Action Taken:** User modified the training script to reduce checkpoint frequency.
- **Recommendation:** Reduce checkpoint writing to 1-2 times per hour to minimize IO load.

## General Learnings
- Regularly monitor job IO patterns to identify potential issues.
- Optimize checkpoint frequency to balance between data safety and system performance.
- Communicate with users to address and resolve IO-related issues promptly.

## Closure
- The ticket was closed after the user confirmed the modification to reduce checkpoint frequency.
---

### 2018032042002031_Rechenzeit.md
# Ticket 2018032042002031

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: Rechenzeit

## Summary
- **User**: Researcher from Friedrich-Alexander Universität Erlangen-Nürnberg
- **Project**: EAM-BTS, investigating the bioavailability of poorly water-soluble drugs
- **Issue**: Need for additional compute resources for intensive simulations
- **Solution**: Discussion and planning for additional compute resources on Meggie and potential application for SuperMUC

## Detailed Conversation

### Initial Request
- **User**: Requests additional compute resources for intensive simulations to address fundamental theoretical assumptions and practical relevance.
- **HPC Admin**: Offers a meeting to discuss further steps and potential application for SuperMUC.

### Meeting and Planning
- **HPC Admin**: Suggests a meeting in April to discuss compute resources and potential SuperMUC application.
- **User**: Confirms availability for the meeting in April.

### Follow-up Discussions
- **User**: Provides a proposal for the simulation at Re=6000.
- **HPC Admin**: Requests detailed resource requirements and goals for the simulation.
- **User**: Provides detailed simulation plan, including steps for generating turbulent entry conditions and initializing the simulation.

### Simulation Preparation
- **User**: Prepares the grid for Re=6000 and outlines the simulation steps.
- **HPC Admin**: Advises on job script requirements and reservation for large jobs.

### Performance Issues
- **User**: Reports performance fluctuations and I/O issues affecting simulation performance.
- **HPC Admin**: Explains network blocking and suggests monitoring tools for performance analysis.

### Data Storage
- **User**: Inquires about storage options for large datasets.
- **HPC Admin**: Provides information on available storage solutions and potential future storage options.

### Job Monitoring and Reservations
- **User**: Requests access key for job monitoring.
- **HPC Admin**: Provides access key and explains job monitoring process.

### Resolution
- **User**: Completes initial simulations and requests removal of reservation.
- **HPC Admin**: Confirms removal of reservation and closes the ticket.

## Key Learnings
- **Compute Resources**: Planning and allocation of compute resources for intensive simulations.
- **Performance Monitoring**: Tools and methods for monitoring job performance and I/O issues.
- **Data Storage**: Options for storing large datasets generated by simulations.
- **Job Management**: Handling job reservations and monitoring for large-scale simulations.

## Root Cause and Solution
- **Root Cause**: Performance fluctuations and I/O issues affecting simulation performance.
- **Solution**: Monitoring tools for performance analysis, network blocking explanation, and storage solutions for large datasets.
```
---

### 2022120342000512_Fwd%3A%20Cresset%20Flare%20Pro%20Plus%20and%20FEP%3A%20Free%20License%20Request.md
# Ticket 2022120342000512

 # HPC Support Ticket: Cresset Flare Pro Plus and FEP Installation

## Keywords
- Cresset Flare Pro Plus
- Cresset Engine Broker (CEB)
- AlmaLinux
- SLURM
- Security Analysis
- CISO
- Port Authentication

## Summary
A user requested the installation of Cresset Flare Pro Plus and Cresset Engine Broker (CEB) on the HPC clusters `alex` or `woody`. The software was installed, but concerns were raised about the security implications of starting the CEBroker3 daemon.

## Root Cause
- The CEBroker3 daemon opens a port that is accessible to anyone within the FAU network without further authentication.
- The daemon executes commands (`sbatch` and subsequent calculations) under the user ID that started it, which prevents centralized starting of the daemon as jobs cannot be submitted as root.

## Solution
- The software was installed on `woody` at `/apps/Cresset/`.
- The CEBroker3 daemon was not started centrally due to security concerns.
- The user was advised to perform a security analysis through the CISO or the Security Operations Center before starting the daemon on any login node.

## Lessons Learned
- Always perform a security analysis for software that opens ports or executes commands under user IDs.
- Ensure that software is compatible with the cluster's operating system and job scheduler.
- Coordinate with the CISO or Security Operations Center for security-related concerns.

## Follow-up
- The user should provide a security analysis or concept for the CEBroker3 daemon before starting it.
- The HPC Admins will not centrally start the daemon due to security concerns.
- The user should contact Cresset support for further assistance with setting up CEB and Flare/FEP.
---

### 2023101242000051_gnuplot%20on%20compute%20nodes.md
# Ticket 2023101242000051

 # HPC Support Ticket: gnuplot on Compute Nodes

## Keywords
- gnuplot
- compute nodes
- software installation
- RAM usage
- dependencies
- modules

## Summary
A user requested the installation of gnuplot on compute nodes for scripting purposes. The HPC admin discussed the feasibility and potential impact on system resources.

## Root Cause
- User needs gnuplot on compute nodes for scripting.

## Discussion
- **Initial Request**: User requested gnuplot for project B158CB.
- **Admin Response**: gnuplot will be installed during the next reinstallation, which may reduce available RAM by 0.1 GB.
- **User Suggestion**: Proposed installing gnuplot as a module to avoid RAM reduction.
- **Admin Concerns**: Mentioned complex dependencies, particularly with fonts, and preferred RHEL packages over Spack.

## Solution
- gnuplot will be installed on the compute nodes during the next reinstallation.
- The impact on RAM (0.1 GB reduction) was acknowledged.
- No immediate solution for installing gnuplot as a module was provided.

## General Learnings
- Software installations on compute nodes can impact system resources.
- Dependencies and package management (e.g., RHEL vs. Spack) are important considerations.
- User requests for specific software should be evaluated for their impact on the overall system.

## Next Steps
- Monitor the installation of gnuplot during the next reinstallation.
- Consider exploring module-based installations for future software requests to minimize resource impact.
---

### 2024110742002823_Login%20Probleme%20-%20Alex%20Cluster.md
# Ticket 2024110742002823

 # HPC Support Ticket: Login Probleme - Alex Cluster

## Keywords
- Login Problem
- Account Expiration
- Verlängerungsantrag (Extension Request)
- Projekt-Manager (Project Manager)
- Rechenzeit (Computing Time)

## Summary
A user encountered login issues on the Alex Cluster after their account expired. The user had submitted an extension request and was under the impression that the account could still be used pending approval.

## Root Cause
- The user's HPC accounts had expired.
- There was confusion regarding the account's usability during the extension request process.

## Ticket Conversation
1. **User**: Reported login issues and mentioned an expired account with a pending extension request.
2. **HPC Admin**: Informed the user that both of their HPC accounts had expired.
3. **User**: Provided details of an email confirming that they could continue using the existing project pending the extension request approval.
4. **HPC Admin**: Clarified that the account extension could only be done by the project manager and advised the user to contact the project manager.

## Solution
- The user needs to contact the project manager (Prof. Andreas Maier) to extend the account.
- The account will be usable once the extension request is approved.

## Lessons Learned
- Ensure clear communication regarding account usability during the extension request process.
- Users should contact their project managers for account extensions.
- HPC Admins should verify the status of extension requests and communicate accordingly.

## Next Steps
- Update the user on the status of their extension request.
- Ensure the project manager is aware of the need for account extension.
- Document the process for handling account extensions to avoid future confusion.

### 42156335_Job%20on%20WOODCRESST%20cluster.md
# Ticket 42156335

 # HPC Support Ticket: Job on WOODCRESST Cluster

## Keywords
- Job submission
- WOODY cluster
- qsub
- .pbs script
- Job rejection
- Queue configuration
- Node limits
- LiMa-Cluster

## Problem
- User attempted to submit a job on the WOODY cluster using a `.pbs` script.
- Job submission command: `qsub sb.pbs`
- Error message: `qsub: Job rejected by all possible destinations (check syntax, queue resources, ...)`
- Previously successful job submission now fails.

## Root Cause
- Recent Betriebssystemupgrade on WOODY cluster.
- Changes in queue configuration to improve scheduling efficiency.
- Maximum job size on WOODY cluster reduced to 32 nodes.

## Solution
- Reduce job size to a maximum of 32 nodes.
- Alternatively, switch to the LiMa-Cluster for larger jobs (up to 64 nodes).

## General Learnings
- Regular updates and changes in cluster configurations can affect job submissions.
- Always check for recent updates or changes in cluster policies.
- Consider alternative clusters for larger jobs if current cluster has limitations.

## Additional Notes
- User account: `mpp450`
- Program location: `/home/woody/capn/mpp450/SB_work`
- Program name: `superbayes`
- HPC Admins provided detailed information on cluster changes and alternative solutions.
---

### 2021080542000082_Anf%C3%83%C2%A4ngerfehler.md
# Ticket 2021080542000082

 # HPC Support Ticket: Anfängerfehler

## Keywords
- Permission denied
- Python script
- Torque job
- TinyGPU Cluster
- Error code
- Bash script
- Pip install

## Summary
A user encountered a "Permission denied" error when attempting to run a Python script on the TinyGPU Cluster. The error occurred after the job was completed, and the user suspected that the issue might be related to the use of `!pip install --user` in their Python script.

## Root Cause
The root cause of the problem was a permission issue with the Python script file. The script did not have the necessary execute permissions.

## Solution
To resolve the issue, the user needs to ensure that the Python script has the appropriate execute permissions. This can be done by running the following command:
```bash
chmod +x RetryAuto.py
```

## General Learning
- Always check file permissions when encountering "Permission denied" errors.
- Ensure that scripts have the necessary execute permissions before submitting them as jobs.
- When using `pip install --user`, ensure that the installed packages are accessible to the script.

## Next Steps
- Verify that the Python script has execute permissions.
- Resubmit the job and monitor for any further errors.
- If the issue persists, consider seeking assistance from the 2nd Level Support team.

## Relevant Teams
- HPC Admins
- 2nd Level Support team

## Related Tools and Software
- Torque job scheduler
- Python
- Pip
- CUDA

## Additional Notes
- The user provided the error code and the bash script used for the Torque job submission.
- The user was willing to provide the Python script if necessary.

This documentation can be used to assist other users who encounter similar permission-related errors when running scripts on the HPC cluster.
---

### 2024112742003606_Segmentation%20fault%20on%20Fritz%20-%20b243cb10.md
# Ticket 2024112742003606

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: Segmentation Fault on Fritz - b243cb10

## Summary
A user encountered issues with the `$WORK` environment variable pointing to different directories and segmentation faults when running jobs on the Fritz HPC cluster.

## Keywords
- Segmentation fault
- $WORK environment variable
- FHI-AIMS
- Spack
- Job script
- Dynamic libraries
- MKL
- mpifort
- Intel MPI
- OpenMPI

## Issues and Solutions

### Issue 1: $WORK Environment Variable
- **Problem**: The `$WORK` environment variable was pointing to different directories on different days.
- **Root Cause**: The user logged in during a fileserver issue, causing the login script to fall back to `/home/woody`.
- **Solution**: Log out and log back in to resolve the issue.

### Issue 2: Segmentation Fault
- **Problem**: Segmentation faults occurred when running jobs on both `$WORK` and `$HOME`.
- **Root Cause**:
  - The user did not load the necessary Spack and user-spack modules.
  - The user explicitly provided the full path of the FHI-AIMS binary, causing `srun` to find the binary but not the other dependencies.
  - The user built with `mpifort` instead of `mpiifort` while linking libraries from Intel MPI and loading the OpenMPI module, causing issues with MKL.
- **Solution**:
  - Load the necessary modules using `spack load` and `module load user-spack`.
  - Rebuild FHI-AIMS using everything from Intel.
  - Ensure correct usage of `mpiifort` and Intel MPI.

## Additional Notes
- The user was advised to send future communications to the support email for better assistance.
- A Zoom call was scheduled to provide further assistance.
- The problem was resolved by rebuilding FHI-AIMS using Intel tools and ensuring correct module loading.

## Conclusion
The issues were resolved by addressing the environment variable problem, ensuring proper module loading, and rebuilding the application with the correct tools and libraries.
```
---

### 2021102642002616_HPC%20accounts%20for%20Jupyter%20server.md
# Ticket 2021102642002616

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: HPC Accounts for Jupyter Server

## Keywords
- HPC accounts
- Jupyter server
- IDM accounts
- Password setting
- Account reactivation
- Autoantwort

## Summary
This ticket involves the creation and management of HPC accounts for students to access the Jupyter server. It includes initial account setup, handling late requests, and account reactivation.

## Key Points

### Initial Request
- **User Request**: List of IDM accounts needing HPC accounts for Jupyter server access.
- **HPC Admin Response**: Accounts created and available the next day. Users can set their HPC passwords in the IdM portal.

### Late Requests
- **User Request**: Additional IDM account needing an HPC account.
- **HPC Admin Response**: Account created and user informed via auto-reply.

### Account Reactivation
- **User Request**: Reactivation of previous accounts and creation of new accounts for additional students.
- **HPC Admin Response**: Accounts reactivated and new accounts created. Users informed via auto-reply.

### General Information
- **Password Setting**: Users need to set their HPC passwords in the IdM portal. It may take several hours for password changes to be effective on HPC systems.
- **Auto-reply**: Automated emails are sent to users with instructions on setting up their HPC accounts.

## Root Cause of Problems
- **Late Requests**: Some students were added after the initial request, requiring additional account creation.
- **Account Reactivation**: Previous accounts needed to be reactivated for the new semester.

## Solutions
- **Account Creation**: HPC Admins created new accounts as requested.
- **Account Reactivation**: HPC Admins reactivated the previous accounts.
- **Communication**: Automated emails were sent to users with instructions on setting up their accounts.

## Notes
- **Delay in Password Changes**: It generally takes a few hours for password changes to be effective on HPC systems.
- **HPC Cafe**: New HPC users are encouraged to attend the HPC Cafe for additional support.

## Conclusion
This ticket demonstrates the process of creating and managing HPC accounts for students, including handling late requests and account reactivation. Effective communication and automated responses are key to ensuring users are informed and can set up their accounts correctly.
```
---

- **Attachments**: tpr file, submit script, log file.

## Keywords
- GROMACS
- Alex cluster
- Job submission
- User input inconsistency
- tpr file
- Submit script
- Log file

## What Can Be Learned
- **Root Cause**: The error indicates an issue with the user input, likely in the tpr file or the submit script.
- **Diagnostics**: The log file and the attached scripts are crucial for diagnosing the problem.
- **Potential Solution**: Review the tpr file and submit script for any inconsistencies or errors. Ensure that all parameters are correctly set and compatible with the GROMACS version on the Alex cluster.

## Support Actions
- **HPC Admins**: Review the attached files to identify the specific inconsistency in the user input.
- **2nd Level Support Team**: Assist in debugging the submit script and tpr file. Provide guidance on correcting any identified issues.
- **Gehard Wellein**: Oversee the resolution process and ensure proper documentation.
- **Georg Hager**: Ensure that the user receives appropriate training and support to prevent similar issues in the future.

## Resolution
- **Solution**: If the root cause is identified, document the specific steps taken to resolve the issue. This may include correcting parameters in the tpr file or modifying the submit script.
- **Follow-up**: Ensure the user is able to successfully run the GROMACS job after the issue is resolved.

## Notes
- **NVIDIA-SMI Output**: Provided for reference, indicating GPU usage and status.
- **Job Details**: Job 812402 on a1721 at Sun Aug 20 14:13:09 CEST 2023.
```
