### 2021090642003629_Gromacs-Job%20auf%20Meggie%20%5Bbcpc002h%5D.md
# Ticket 2021090642003629

 # HPC Support Ticket: Gromacs-Job auf Meggie [bcpc002h]

## Keywords
- Gromacs
- Meggie
- TinyGPU
- CUDA error #700
- cudaErrorIllegalAddress
- 1md.gro
- Performance
- GPU
- CPU
- Simulation
- Monitoring
- PBS-Skript
- Gromacs-Logfile
- .tpr-Datei

## Summary
The user was running Gromacs jobs on Meggie using an outdated version of the software. The HPC admin advised the user to switch to the latest version of Gromacs and to move their simulations to TinyGPU for better performance. After making these changes, the user encountered a CUDA error #700 (cudaErrorIllegalAddress) during a molecular dynamics (MD) simulation.

## Root Cause
The error was caused by a corrupted `1md.gro` file. Attempts to continue the simulation with this file resulted in the same error.

## Solution
The user restarted the MD simulation using a previous trajectory, which resolved the issue. The simulation ran for 300ns without any errors.

## Lessons Learned
- Always use the latest version of software for better performance and stability.
- Moving simulations to GPU-based systems like TinyGPU can significantly improve performance.
- Corrupted input files can cause unexpected errors in simulations.
- Restarting simulations with a previous trajectory can help resolve issues caused by corrupted files.

## References
- [Gromacs Shootout: Intel IceLake vs NVIDIA GPU](https://hpc.fau.de/2021/08/10/gromacs-shootout-intel-icelake-vs-nvidia-gpu/)
- [Multi-GPU Gromacs Jobs on TinyGPU](https://hpc.fau.de/2021/06/18/multi-gpu-gromacs-jobs-on-tinygpu/)
---

### 2024121842000581_tempor%C3%A4rer%20LLM-Service%20auf%20Alex_A40.md
# Ticket 2024121842000581

 # HPC Support Ticket: Temporärer LLM-Service auf Alex/A40

## Keywords
- LLM (Large Language Model)
- API Endpoints
- OpenAI-compatible
- Quantisierte Modelle
- A40 GPUs
- Authorization Header
- OpenAI Toolkit
- Python Requests
- OpenAI Client

## Summary
A temporary solution for LLM services was set up on Alex/A40 GPUs. Several OpenAI-compatible API endpoints were created for different LLM models. These models are heavily quantized and may not always be active.

## Details
- **API Endpoints**:
  - `https://hub.nhr.fau.de:8443/llm/ollama/llama3.1-70b/`
  - `https://hub.nhr.fau.de:8443/llm/ollama/llama3.2-3b/`
  - `https://hub.nhr.fau.de:8443/llm/ollama/llama3.1-405b`
  - `https://hub.nhr.fau.de:8443/llm/vllm/teuken-7b`
  - `https://hub.nhr.fau.de:8443/llm/vllm/llama3.3-70b`

- **Authorization**:
  - Manually set the header `Authorization: Bearer aa7oTohzooloGhier1Aeph6fooS1quuG`
  - Or use `api_key=aa7oTohzooloGhier1Aeph6fooS1quuG` with the OpenAI toolkit

## Examples
### Example 1: Using Python Requests
```python
import requests

url = "https://hub.nhr.fau.de:8443/llm/ollama/llama3.2-3b/v1/chat/completions"
headers = {
    "Authorization": "Bearer wtpKi5AE33ToZ7vQgIKbxlRvCMPmzwCL",
    "Content-Type": "application/json",
}
payload = {
    "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Hallo, wie geht es dir?"}]
}

try:
    response = requests.post(url, json=payload, headers=headers, verify=False)
    response.raise_for_status()  # Check for HTTP errors
    print(response.json())  # Print the server response
except requests.exceptions.RequestException as e:
    print(f"Request failed: {e}")
```

### Example 2: Using OpenAI Client
```python
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(
    base_url='https://hub.nhr.fau.de:8443/llm/ollama/llama3.2-3b/v1',
    api_key="wtpKi5AE33ToZ7vQgIKbxlRvCMPmzwCL",
)

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="llama3.2:3b",
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Who won the world series in 2020?"},
        {"role": "assistant", "content": "The LA Dodgers won in 2020."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Where was it played?"}
    ]
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)
```

## What Can Be Learned
- **Temporary LLM Services**: Understanding the setup and usage of temporary LLM services on HPC systems.
- **API Endpoints**: How to access and use API endpoints for different LLM models.
- **Authorization**: Methods for authorizing API requests.
- **Python Examples**: Practical examples of using Python requests and the OpenAI client to interact with the LLM services.

## Root Cause of the Problem
- The need for temporary LLM services on Alex/A40 GPUs.

## Solution
- Provided API endpoints and authorization methods for accessing the LLM services.
- Examples of how to use these services with Python requests and the OpenAI client.
---

### 2023013142002861_Jobs%20on%20TinyGPU%20does%20not%20use%20allocated%20GPUs%20%5Biwso061h%5D.md
# Ticket 2023013142002861

 # HPC Support Ticket: Jobs on TinyGPU does not use allocated GPUs

## Keywords
- GPU utilization
- Job monitoring
- Resource allocation
- Debugging
- Interactive job
- Entrance node

## Problem Description
- User's job on TinyGPU uses only one GPU out of four allocated GPUs.
- User reports issues with debugging on the entrance node (tinyx), including unresponsive terminal and slow performance.

## Root Cause
- Inefficient GPU utilization by the user's job.
- Debugging on the shared entrance node (tinyx) leading to resource contention.

## Solutions and Recommendations
- **GPU Utilization:**
  - HPC Admin provided instructions to check GPU utilization using `nvidia-smi` after attaching to the running job with `srun --pty --jobid bash`.
  - User advised to allocate GPU resources only if their code can utilize them effectively.

- **Debugging:**
  - HPC Admin recommended using an interactive job on one of the tinyGPU nodes for debugging instead of the shared entrance node (tinyx).
  - This approach prevents blocking resources for other users and provides a more stable debugging environment.

## General Learnings
- Always monitor GPU utilization to ensure efficient resource usage.
- Use interactive jobs on appropriate nodes for debugging to avoid blocking shared resources.
- Be mindful of resource allocation to prevent idle modes and ensure availability for other users.
---

# HPC-Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

## Keywords

- **Communication with Users**: Importance of clear communication regarding project extensions and the steps required for approval.

## Root Cause of the Problem
- The user's project was about to expire, and they needed an extension to continue their research.
- The project had not fully utilized its allocated compute budget, requiring an interim report to justify the extension.

## Solution
- **Interim Report**: The user was asked to provide an interim report detailing the work done so far and the plans for utilizing the remaining compute budget.
- **Extension Process**: The project was extended for a month to allow time for the interim report submission. Upon review, the project was extended for an additional year.

## Steps Taken
1. **Initial Notification**: Automated email sent to the user notifying them of the upcoming expiration of their HPC account.
2. **User Request**: The user requested an extension for the project.
3. **Admin Response**: The HPC Admin informed the user about the need for an interim report and the process for extension.
4. **Interim Report Submission**: The user submitted the required interim report.
5. **Project Extension**: Upon reviewing the report, the project was extended for an additional year.

## Conclusion

- **Communication**: Maintain clear and timely communication with users regarding project extensions and account expirations.

### 2024022342001132_Telefonat%20Beschaffung%20GPU-Knoten%20Kliniknetz.md
# Ticket 2024022342001132

 # HPC-Support Ticket Conversation Summary

## Subject
Telefonat Beschaffung GPU-Knoten Kliniknetz

## Keywords
- GPU-Knoten
- Medizindaten
- HPC-Infrastruktur
- Sicherheitslücken
- Nvidia L40/L40S GPUs
- H100-Systeme
- Warmwasserkühlung
- Beschaffung
- Serverrahmenvertrag
- NVMe-Storage-Server
- GPU-Server

## General Learnings
- **Infrastructure Limitations**: The current HPC/NHR infrastructure is not suitable for processing sensitive medical data due to security and compliance concerns.
- **Service Times**: The HPC systems operate on a 5x8 service model, with limited monitoring outside regular hours.
- **Access Control**: The systems are multi-user with around 1,000 users having access. Physical access is restricted but not foolproof.
- **Data Security**: HPC date systems are not encrypted, and defective storage devices are often returned to manufacturers without secure deletion.
- **Network Security**: Rechenknoten are only accessible internally, while login and dialog nodes can be accessed globally via SSH.
- **Administrative Access**: Limited to a small group of administrators.
- **Hardware Options**: Nvidia L40/L40S GPUs are suggested as alternatives to H100 systems due to cost and availability.
- **Budget Considerations**: For a budget of 450k€, the expected configuration includes 5x GPU servers, 1x NVMe server, and 1x switch.

## Root Cause of the Problem
- The current HPC infrastructure is not designed to handle sensitive medical data securely.
- There are concerns about unauthorized access due to potential security vulnerabilities and limited monitoring.

## Solution
- Consider alternative hardware options like Nvidia L40/L40S GPUs.
- Evaluate the need for a "sichere Insel" within the RRZE with specific hardware, operational concepts, and compliance regulations.
- Ensure that any new hardware acquisitions align with the university's server framework agreements and consider EU-wide tenders for larger projects.

## Additional Notes
- The conversation includes detailed configurations for GPU and NVMe storage servers.
- Recommendations for potential suppliers and framework agreements are provided.
- The importance of defining clear processes for reporting security incidents is highlighted.
---

### 2024120942001828_%5Baction%20reqiered%5Dhigh%20load%20on%20atuin%20%5Ba104bc20%5D.md
# Ticket 2024120942001828

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: High Load on Atuin

## Subject
[action required] High load on Atuin [a104bc20]

## Issue
- User's job causing high load on the file system, impacting all users.
- Atuin not designed for heavy parallel I/O.

## Root Cause
- Job 1709346 on Fritz causing problems for the file systems.
- Temporary files for restarting located in `/home/atuin/a104bc/a104bc20/Agama_HD_3e3_ObsBasedDensity_SneN/run_output_10pc_vol_diff_4/restartfiles`.

## Solution
- Move heavy parallel I/O operations to `/lustre`.
- Adjust simulation parameters to reduce parallel file writing (e.g., set `NumFilesWrittenInParallel` to 1).

## Keywords
- High load
- File system
- Parallel I/O
- Atuin
- Lustre
- Restart files

## Lessons Learned
- Ensure heavy parallel I/O operations are performed on the appropriate file system (`/lustre`).
- Avoid storing temporary files in the home directory to prevent file system overload.
- Adjust simulation parameters to optimize file writing and reduce load on the file system.

## References

```

### 2023062042003229_Job%20on%20TinyGPU%20do%20not%20use%20all%20allocated%20GPUs%20%5Biwal058h%5D.md
# Ticket 2023062042003229

 # HPC-Support Ticket: Job on TinyGPU Does Not Use All Allocated GPUs

## Keywords
- GPU utilization
- PyTorch Lightning
- SLURM
- `nvidia-smi`
- Multi-GPU training
- Distributed Data Parallel (DDP)

## Problem Description
The user's job on TinyGPU was only utilizing one GPU out of the four allocated GPUs. The issue was detected by the HPC monitoring system.

## Root Cause
- The user's PyTorch Lightning script reported using all requested GPUs, but the actual utilization showed only one GPU being used.
- The user used a single dataloader instance with the dataset kept in RAM and specified only one worker process.

## Troubleshooting Steps
1. **Monitoring**: HPC Admin attached a screenshot from the monitoring system showing GPU utilization.
2. **Job Inspection**: User was instructed to attach to the running job using `srun --pty --overlap --jobid YOUR-JOBID bash` and check GPU utilization with `nvidia-smi`.
3. **Framework Logs**: User provided logs from PyTorch Lightning, indicating that the framework recognized all GPUs but utilized only one.

## Solution
- The user switched back to training with 1 GPU, which worked fine.
- The issue was likely due to unintended behavior in PyTorch Lightning, where the framework reported using all GPUs, but the epoch time suggested otherwise.

## Additional Notes
- HPC Admin suggested contacting Philipp Grundmauer, who had similar issues with PyTorch Lightning.
- Recommended SLURM job script settings for multi-GPU training:
  ```bash
  #SBATCH --ntasks-per-node=nGPU
  #SBATCH --gres=gpu:nGPU
  ```
- Recommended PyTorch Lightning Trainer settings:
  ```python
  Trainer = pl.Trainer(accelerator='gpu', devices='auto', strategy='ddp')
  ```

## Lessons Learned
- Always verify actual GPU utilization using tools like `nvidia-smi`.
- Be cautious with frameworks that report resource usage, as they may not accurately reflect actual utilization.
- For multi-GPU training, ensure that the data loading and processing pipeline is optimized for parallel processing.
---

### 2022070442003831_Tier3-Access-Alex%20%22Darius%20R%C3%83%C2%BCckert%22%20_%20iwi9012h.md
# Ticket 2022070442003831

 # HPC-Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

## Keywords
- GPU types: A100, A40
- Mixed-precision optimization
- Benchmarking
- File I/O optimization
- Tensorboard usage
- NFS server load

## What Can Be Learned

### GPU Selection and Benchmarking
- **Issue**: User selected both A100 and A40 GPUs without knowing which would perform better for their mixed-precision application.
- **Solution**: User was allowed to benchmark both GPU types. The A100 showed a significant performance advantage due to better FP16 performance and faster memory.

### File I/O Optimization
- **Issue**: User's jobs were causing excessive file I/O operations, impacting the NFS server.
- **Solution**: User was advised to write files initially to `$TMPDIR` and then create a TAR archive to be written to `$WORK` at the end of the job. This reduces the load on the NFS server and minimizes the number of small files.

### Tensorboard Usage
- **Issue**: User was using Tensorboard for job monitoring, which involved frequent file writes.
- **Solution**: User was advised to reduce file writes and run Tensorboard locally with the home directory mounted via SSHFS. This approach minimizes the impact on the NFS server while allowing for job monitoring.

### General Advice
- **Issue**: User needed guidance on optimizing file I/O and GPU selection.
- **Solution**: HPC Admins provided detailed advice on benchmarking GPU performance and optimizing file I/O to reduce the load on the NFS server.

## Conclusion
This conversation highlights the importance of benchmarking GPU performance for specific applications and optimizing file I/O to minimize the impact on shared resources. The solutions provided can be applied to similar cases where users need to select the best GPU for their workload and manage file I/O efficiently.
---

### 2021090942001527_GPU%20Multiple%20Walkers.md
# Ticket 2021090942001527

 # HPC Support Ticket: GPU Multiple Walkers

## Problem Description
User encountered issues with MetaMW simulations on GPUs. The Free Energy Surface (FES) from GPU simulations did not match the expected results obtained on SuperMuc, despite using the same plumed file and similar walkers.

## Root Cause
- **Atom Numbering Issue**: Gromacs numbering the atoms differently in newer program versions.
- **Incorrect Atom IDs**: The atoms specified in the plumed.dat file did not correspond to the correct residues.

## Solution
1. **Verify Atom IDs**: Ensure the atom IDs in the plumed.dat file correspond to the correct residues.
   ```plaintext
   c1: CENTER ATOMS=4522,1996 # center 244TRP-91Val
   d1: DISTANCE ATOMS=c1,561 COMPONENTS # c1 36TYR ligand
   ```
2. **Adjust Gromacs Commands for New Version**:
   - Use `gmx_mpi` instead of `mdrun_mpi`.
   - Change `-multidir` to `-multi`.
   - Adjust the plumed flag to use the relative path: `-plumed ../plumed.dat`.
   - If there is a second plumed file, adjust its path as well: `INCLUDE FILE=../plumed_funnel.dat`.

3. **Stop MPS Server**: Add the following command after the Gromacs command to stop the MPS server:
   ```bash
   echo quit | nvidia-cuda-mps-control
   ```

## Additional Notes
- **Performance**: There was not much improvement in performance when using multiple GPUs. With 1xA100 GPU, the user could reach 165-170ns on a system comprising 120,000 atoms.
- **Testing**: The user successfully ran multiple walkers simulations on GPU machines with the provided script.

## Conclusion
The issue was resolved by verifying the atom IDs and adjusting the Gromacs commands for the new version. The user was able to run multiple walkers simulations successfully on GPU machines.

---

This report provides a summary of the issue, the root cause, the solution, and additional notes for future reference.
---

### 2019121842002954_Seit%20einer%20Woche%20keine%20Rechenzeit%20auf%20emmy.md
# Ticket 2019121842002954

 # HPC Support Ticket Analysis

## Subject
Seit einer Woche keine Rechenzeit auf emmy

## Keywords
- Rechenzeit
- emmy Cluster
- Syntax error
- Job submission
- Walltime

## Problem
- User unable to get compute time on emmy cluster for over a week.
- Short jobs (<1h) also not getting allocated.

## Root Cause
- Incorrect syntax used in job submission command.
- User used a colon (`:`) instead of a comma (`,`) before `walltime`.

## Solution
- Correct syntax for job submission:
  ```
  -lnodes=1:ppn=40:f2.2:ddr1600,walltime=0:20:00
  ```
  or
  ```
  -lnodes=1:ppn=40:f2.2:ddr1600 -lwalltime=0:20:00
  ```

## General Learning
- Ensure correct syntax in job submission commands.
- Use commas to separate properties in the `-l` flag.
- `walltime` is not a property but a separate flag.

## Roles Involved
- **HPC Admins**: Provided the solution.
- **User**: Reported the issue and followed up with the solution.

## Additional Notes
- The cluster was not overloaded; the issue was due to a syntax error.
- Quick response from HPC Admins resolved the user's problem efficiently.
---

### 2024120442002891_AdptiveCpp%20on%20Alex%20Cluster%20b157be22.md
# Ticket 2024120442002891

 # HPC-Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

## Subject
AdptiveCpp on Alex Cluster b157be22

## Keywords
- AdaptiveCpp
- acpp compiler
- GPU
- Linker error
- Interactive job
- Compute node
- System-wide install

## Summary
A user is attempting to install and use the AdaptiveCpp (acpp) compiler on the Alex Cluster. Despite successful compilation, the user encounters a linker error when trying to compile a simple C++ program. The HPC Admin suggests running the job on a compute node with GPUs, but the issue persists. The user suspects a dynamic linking problem and suggests a system-wide installation of AdaptiveCpp.

## Root Cause of the Problem
The root cause of the problem is a linker error related to the `std::condition_variable` symbol, which suggests a possible mismatch or missing dependency in the linked libraries:
```
/home/hpc/b157be/b157be22/AdaptiveCpp/install/bin/../lib//libacpp-rt.so: undefined reference to `std::condition_variable::wait(std::unique_lock<std::mutex>&)@GLIBCXX_3.4.30'
```

## Solution
No definitive solution was found in the conversation. However, the following steps were suggested:
1. **Run on Compute Node**: Ensure the job is run on a compute node with GPUs, as the frontend nodes do not have GPUs.
2. **System-Wide Installation**: Consider a system-wide installation of AdaptiveCpp to potentially resolve dependency issues.

## General Learnings
- **GPU Availability**: Ensure that jobs requiring GPUs are run on compute nodes with the appropriate hardware.
- **Linker Errors**: Linker errors can often be caused by missing or mismatched dependencies.
- **System-Wide Installations**: For complex software with many dependencies, a system-wide installation can simplify troubleshooting and usage.
- **Interactive Jobs**: Use interactive jobs to test and debug issues that may not be apparent on frontend nodes.

## Next Steps
- Further investigate the linker error to identify missing or mismatched dependencies.
- Consider installing AdaptiveCpp system-wide to provide a consistent environment for all users.
- Document the installation process and any additional dependencies required for AdaptiveCpp.
---

### 2022112442002518_Directory%20problem%20in%20the%20HPC%20server.md
# Ticket 2022112442002518

 # HPC Support Ticket: Directory Problem in the HPC Server

## Keywords
- HPC system
- Directory not defined
- Usernames
- Alex cluster
- Unix-Gruppe
- Home directory
- Symlink
- Anlegescript

## Problem Description
- Users with specific usernames (b117ba12, b117ba13, b117ba14) are unable to connect to the Alex cluster due to undefined directories.
- The directories for these users are not listed in the `/home/hpc` path.

## Root Cause
- The home directories for the users are not correctly defined according to the expected schema.

## Solution
- The home directories should follow the schema `/home/hpc/$GROUP/$USERNAME`.
- For the given usernames, the directories should be `/home/hpc/b116ba/b117baN` instead of `/home/hpc/b117ba/b117baN`.
- The HPC Admin will adjust the script to correct the directory structure and create the necessary symlinks.

## General Learnings
- Ensure that user directories are created according to the defined schema.
- Verify the correct Unix group and directory structure for new users.
- Adjust scripts as needed to maintain the correct directory structure.

## Next Steps
- Implement the changes in the script to correct the directory structure.
- Create symlinks as necessary to resolve the issue.

## Relevant Groups and Roles
- **HPC Admins**: Responsible for managing the HPC system and resolving directory issues.
- **2nd Level Support Team**: Provides additional support for HPC-related issues.
- **Head of the Datacenter**: Oversees the datacenter operations.
- **Training and Support Group Leader**: Manages training and support activities.
- **Software and Tools Developer**: Develops software and tools for the HPC system.

## Conclusion
- The issue was identified as a misconfiguration in the directory schema.
- The HPC Admin will correct the script to ensure proper directory creation for future users.
---

### 2020102942001917_Dein%20Job%20auf%20Emmy%20mit%20jobid%3D1363405%20%28mfbi003h%29.md
# Ticket 2020102942001917

 # HPC Support Ticket Analysis

## Subject
Dein Job auf Emmy mit jobid=1363405 (mfbi003h)

## Keywords
- Job monitoring
- Resource wastage
- Serial vs. parallel jobs
- Cluster usage policies
- Software availability (gnuplot)

## Problem
- User submitted a job using the serial version of `cpptraj` on the Emmy cluster.
- Only one of the four requested nodes was actively working, leading to resource wastage.

## Root Cause
- Inappropriate use of serial job on a cluster intended for parallel jobs.
- Lack of awareness about the correct cluster for serial jobs.

## Solution
- HPC Admin advised the user to use the Woody cluster for serial jobs.
- If memory is insufficient on Woody, the Tinyfat cluster can be used.
- For parallel execution on Emmy, the user should use `cpptraj.MPI` or `cpptraj.OMP`.
- HPC Admin installed `gnuplot` on the Woody cluster to meet the user's requirements.

## General Learnings
- Ensure jobs are submitted to the appropriate cluster based on their nature (serial vs. parallel).
- Monitor job performance to identify resource wastage.
- Communicate with users to provide guidance on best practices and available resources.
- Install necessary software on clusters as per user requirements to facilitate their work.

## Actions Taken
- HPC Admin reviewed the job script and identified the issue.
- Provided guidance on the correct cluster for serial jobs.
- Installed `gnuplot` on the Woody cluster to meet user needs.

## Follow-up
- Users should be encouraged to seek assistance if they have questions or need further guidance.
- Continue monitoring jobs to ensure efficient resource utilization.

---

This report can be used as a reference for similar issues in the future to ensure efficient resource management and user support.
---

### 2023101742001872_Fwd%3A%20CAMA%20Zugang%20Verl%C3%83%C2%A4ngern.md
# Ticket 2023101742001872

 # HPC Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

## Keywords
- HPC Account
- Account Extension
- SSH Key
- Password Reset
- Data Recovery
- Snapshots/Backups

## General Learnings
- **Account Validity**: HPC accounts created for courses are valid only for the duration of the semester.
- **SSH Key Login**: HPC systems use SSH keys for login without passwords.
- **Data Recovery**: Data from old accounts may not be available. Active accounts may have snapshots or backups.

## Root Causes and Solutions

### Account Extension
- **Root Cause**: User's HPC account was created for a course and is expiring.
- **Solution**: User needs to apply for a new account for continued access.

### SSH Key Login
- **Root Cause**: User forgot the password for SSH login.
- **Solution**: HPC systems use SSH keys, not passwords. User should upload SSH key via the NHR portal.

### Data Recovery
- **Root Cause**: User accidentally deleted a folder.
- **Solution**: Check if snapshots or backups are available for active accounts. Data from old accounts is not retrievable.

## Documentation for Support Employees

### Account Extension
If a user requests an account extension for an HPC account created for a course, inform them that these accounts are valid only for the semester's duration. They need to apply for a new account for continued access.

### SSH Key Login
If a user has issues with SSH login due to forgotten passwords, remind them that HPC systems use SSH keys instead of passwords. Guide them to upload their SSH key via the NHR portal.

### Data Recovery
For data recovery requests, check if the user's account is active and if snapshots or backups are available. Inform users that data from old accounts is not retrievable.

---

This analysis provides a concise overview of common issues and their solutions, aiding support employees in resolving similar problems efficiently.
---

## Keywords

- Student account

- New application
- Data retention

## Summary
A student needs to reactivate their HPC account for their final thesis. The account was created in 2015 and is likely inactive.

## Root Cause
- The student's HPC account has expired due to inactivity over a long period.

## Solution
- The HPC Admin advises that it doesn't matter whether a new account is created or the old one is reactivated, as the data from the old account is likely gone.
- The student should fill out the application form, selecting either "Neuantrag" or "Verlaengerung" as both options use the same form.

## General Learnings
- Accounts on the HPC cluster can become inactive over time.
- Reactivating an old account or creating a new one follows the same application process.
- Data associated with inactive accounts may be deleted after a certain period.

## Actions Taken

### 2024080842002597_TinyGPU%20issues.md
# Ticket 2024080842002597

 # HPC Support Ticket: TinyGPU Issues

## Keywords
- TinyGPU
- Conda environment
- Interactive job
- Batch job
- GPU allocation
- `module` command
- `nvidia-smi` command
- `cuda.is_available()`

## Problem Description
- User encountered issues with setting up and running a script on TinyGPU.
- Errors included:
  - `module: command not found`
  - `nvidia-smi: command not found`
  - `cuda.is_available() return False`

## Root Cause
- The user did not build the conda environment within an interactive job.
- The frontend node does not have access to GPUs, causing `cuda.is_available()` to return `False`.

## Solution
- Build the conda environment within an interactive job using `salloc.tinygpu –gres=gpu:1 –time=01:00:00`.
- Use the interactive job to ensure the environment is correctly set up and can access the GPU.
- Once the environment is working, submit batch jobs using `sbatch <script>`.

## General Learnings
- Always build conda environments within an interactive job when working with GPUs.
- The frontend node does not have GPU access; interactive jobs are necessary for GPU-related tasks.
- Interactive jobs can take up to 24 hours to allocate resources, but typically take less than an hour.
- Non-interactive jobs (`sbatch <script>`) are the default working mode and do not require user input.

## References
- [Why is my application not using the GPU?](https://doc.nhr.fau.de/faq/#why-is-my-application-not-using-the-gpu)
- [TinyGPU Documentation](https://doc.nhr.fau.de/clusters/tinygpu)
- [Interactive Jobs](https://doc.nhr.fau.de/clusters/tinygpu/?h=interactiv#interactive-jobs)
---

### 2019010342000558_Jobs%20auf%20Emmy%20_%20Prozesszahl%20_%20mpt1002h.md
# Ticket 2019010342000558

 # HPC Support Ticket: Jobs auf Emmy / Prozesszahl / mpt1002h

## Keywords
- Job optimization
- Process count per node
- Floating-point rate
- Memory bandwidth
- Cache utilization
- Inter-node communication
- Load balancing

## Problem
- User's jobs were running ~200 processes per compute node.
- High load observed on the nodes.

## Analysis
- **Floating-Point Rate**: ~30 GFlop/s in double precision.
- **Memory Bandwidth**: Low usage.
- **Memory Usage**: Low.
- **Inter-Node Communication**: Minimal.
- **Cache Utilization**: Working set likely fits into cache.

## Recommendations
- **Experiment with Node Count**:
  - Increase the number of nodes to see if it reduces overall runtime.
  - Monitor the product of node count and runtime to determine if more nodes are beneficial.
- **Avoid Overbooking Cores**:
  - Massive overbooking of cores might be counterproductive.

## Conclusion
- The user was advised to experiment with different node configurations to optimize job performance.
- The ticket was closed due to no further feedback from the user.
- Job monitoring indicated that overbooking cores was not significantly detrimental in this case.

## Solution
- No definitive solution provided as the user was advised to experiment and monitor performance.

## Next Steps
- If similar issues arise, advise users to experiment with node configurations and monitor performance metrics.
- Ensure users are aware of the potential impacts of overbooking cores.
---

### 2018051442001629_Error%20in%20job%20script.md
# Ticket 2018051442001629

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: Error in Job Script

## Keywords
- Job script error
- Node allocation
- SMT threads
- Physical CPU cores
- PBS job scheduler

## Summary
The user submitted a job script that requested more nodes than were actually used. Additionally, the job script utilized SMT threads, which are not recommended for most HPC applications.

## Root Cause
- The user requested 6 nodes but only used 4.
- The job script used SMT threads, which are not beneficial for most HPC applications.

## Solution
- Adjust the job script to request the correct number of nodes.
- Use only 20 processes per node, as the emmy nodes have 20 physical CPU cores (10 per socket).

## Lessons Learned
- Ensure that the number of nodes requested in the job script matches the actual usage.
- Avoid using SMT threads for most HPC applications, as they do not provide significant benefits.
- Use the recommended number of processes per node based on the physical CPU cores available.

## Example Job Script Adjustment
```bash
#PBS -l nodes=4:ppn=20,walltime=23:59:00
NP=80          # number of cores
NPN=20         # number of cores/node
```

## Additional Notes
- Regular checks of running jobs can help identify and rectify such issues.
- Proper communication with users can ensure efficient resource utilization.
```
---

### 2025010942000713_Tier3-Access-Alex%20%22Naga%20Venkata%20Sai%20Jitin%20Jami%22%20_%20iwso114h.md
# Ticket 2025010942000713

 # HPC Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

## Keywords
- TinyGPU
- Alex
- A100
- A40
- Partition
- Job Script
- GPU Access

## Summary
The user requested access to the Alex cluster but was advised to use TinyGPU due to high demand on Alex. The user needed guidance on specifying A100 GPUs in their job script and inquired about using any available GPU regardless of partition.

## Root Cause
- User needed to specify A100 GPUs in their job script.
- User wanted to use any available GPU on TinyGPU.

## Solution
- To specify A100 GPUs, the user should use:
  ```bash
  #SBATCH --partition=a100
  #SBATCH --gres=gpu:a100:1
  ```
- The user was advised to use the 'work' partition if A100 GPUs were not necessary.
- In theory, it is possible to queue jobs in more than one partition, but the user was advised to stick to the 'work' partition if A100 GPUs were not required.

## General Learnings
- Always specify the correct partition and GPU type in the job script.
- Use the appropriate partition based on your GPU requirements.
- Consult the documentation for specific cluster configurations.

## Documentation Reference
- [TinyGPU Cluster Documentation](https://doc.nhr.fau.de/clusters/tinygpu/)
---

### 2024031342003657_gfortran%20on%20ARM%20lukewarm.md
# Ticket 2024031342003657

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: gfortran on ARM lukewarm

## Keywords
- gfortran
- ARM lukewarm
- gcc modules
- static libraries
- armpl

## Problem
- User requested installation of `gfortran` on ARM lukewarm.
- User encountered missing `armpl` static libraries.

## Root Cause
- The system did not have `gfortran` installed.
- The `armpl` module was not available, and the user could not find the static library.

## Solution
- HPC Admins informed the user about available gcc modules (`gnu/12.2.0` and `gcc/13.2.0-gcc12.3.0-n32chg5`) that contain `gfortran`.
- HPC Admins provided the location of static libraries for `armpl` in the available modules and an alternative installation path.

## What to Learn
- Always check available modules before requesting new software installations.
- Modules can be loaded using `module add <module_name>`.
- Static libraries can be found in specific directories within the modules.
- Communication between HPC Admins and users is crucial for resolving software-related issues.
```
---

### 2023010942002895_Reg.%20libsndfile%20installation%20on%20Alex%20cluster..md
# Ticket 2023010942002895

 # HPC Support Ticket: libsndfile Installation on Alex Cluster

## Keywords
- libsndfile
- Alex cluster
- TinyGPU
- libffi
- Python modules
- Speech/Audio processing

## Problem
- User requires `libsndfile` package for Python modules related to Speech/Audio processing.
- Package is installed on TinyGPU at `/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libsndfile.so.1`.
- User couldn't find a matching installation on the Alex cluster.
- User also looking for `libffi.so` file.

## Solution
- HPC Admin confirmed that `libsndfile` is installed on Alex cluster.
- `libsndfile.so.1` is located at `/usr/lib64/libsndfile.so.1`.
- `libffi.so` belongs to a development package which is not installed, but `libffi.so.6` is available at `/usr/lib64/libffi.so.6`.

## General Learnings
- Libraries may be installed but located at different paths on different clusters.
- Always check with HPC Admin for the correct path of installed libraries.
- Some packages may not be fully installed, and only specific versions of libraries may be available.
---

### 2024021442003121_Segmentation%20Fault%20on%20TinyX.md
# Ticket 2024021442003121

 # HPC Support Ticket: Segmentation Fault on TinyX

## Keywords
- Segmentation Fault
- SLURM Script
- Python Script
- Memory Access
- Error Log

## Summary
A user encountered a segmentation fault when executing a Python script via a SLURM script. The user suspected that the issue might be related to improper memory access.

The user was accessing a memory location that they should not have been accessing, leading to a segmentation fault.

## Solution
The HPC Admin confirmed the user's suspicion that they were accessing an invalid memory location. No specific solution was provided in the conversation, but the user was informed that their assumption was correct.

## Lessons Learned

- Users should review their code to ensure they are not accessing invalid memory locations.
- Error logs can provide valuable information for diagnosing segmentation faults.

- Ensure that all memory accesses in the Python script are within valid ranges.
- Consider using debugging tools to trace the execution and identify the root cause of the segmentation fault.

### 2022060342001176_Job%20on%20TinyGPU%20does%20not%20use%20GPU%20%5Biwal091h%5D.md
# Ticket 2022060342001176

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: Job on TinyGPU does not use GPU

## Keywords
- GPU utilization
- CUDA module
- Slurm script
- nvidia-smi
- module load
- module avail

## Summary
A user reported that their job on TinyGPU was not utilizing the GPU. The HPC Admin provided guidance on how to check GPU utilization and identified that the CUDA module was not loaded correctly.

## Root Cause
- The CUDA module was not loaded in the Slurm script, leading to the GPU not being detected.
- The user attempted to load `cuda/11.2`, but the correct module name was `cuda/11.2.0`.

## Solution
- Add `module load cuda/11.2.0` to the Slurm script.
- Use `module avail` to check the available modules on the cluster.

## Steps Taken
1. **Initial Contact**: HPC Admin informed the user that their job was not utilizing the GPU and provided a screenshot from the monitoring system.
2. **User Response**: The user reported an error indicating that the CUDA module could not be located.
3. **Admin Guidance**: The HPC Admin suggested adding `module load cuda/11.2` to the Slurm script.
4. **Correction**: The HPC Admin corrected the module name to `cuda/11.2.0` and advised the user to check available modules using `module avail`.

## Additional Resources
- [Working with NVIDIA GPUs](https://hpc.fau.de/systems-services/systems-documentation-instructions/special-applications-and-tips-tricks/working-with-nvidia-gpus/)

## Conclusion
Ensure that the correct CUDA module is loaded in the Slurm script to enable GPU utilization. Use `module avail` to verify the available modules on the cluster.
```
---

### 2021020342002335_Zugang%20zu%20RRZE%20HPC-Systemen.md
# Ticket 2021020342002335

 # HPC Support Ticket: Access to RRZE HPC Systems

## Keywords
- HPC Access
- Identitätsmanagement (IdM)
- Videoident-Verfahren
- DFNconf
- Personalausweis
- Reisepass
- Aufenthaltstitel
- Home Office
- Pandemie

## Summary
The user requested access to the RRZE HPC systems, including the test cluster. The process involves filling out two application forms for each employee and performing an identity verification via video call.

## Root Cause
- The user needed access to the HPC systems and had to follow the application and identity verification process.

## Solution
1. **Application Forms**: The user was instructed to fill out two application forms for each employee.
2. **Identity Verification**: The identity of each applicant had to be verified via a video call using the Videoident-Verfahren.
3. **Home Office Consideration**: Due to the pandemic, the identity verification process was adapted to accommodate employees working from home.
4. **Video Call Details**: The video call was scheduled for a specific time, and each employee was to log in one after the other to complete the verification process.
5. **Documentation**: The user provided the necessary information about the type and issuing country of the identification documents for each employee.

## Steps Taken
1. The HPC Admin provided instructions on filling out the application forms and the identity verification process.
2. The user collected all the application forms and sent them as a package to the HPC Admin.
3. The HPC Admin scheduled a video call for the identity verification process.
4. The user provided the necessary information about the identification documents for each employee.
5. The HPC Admin sent the invitation for the video call and provided the link to the DFNconf meeting room.
6. The HPC Admin performed the identity verification for each employee and documented the process.
7. The HPC Admin closed the ticket after the accounts were created and the activation letters were sent.

## Lessons Learned
- The identity verification process can be streamlined by scheduling a block of time for multiple employees to log in one after the other.
- The type and issuing country of the identification documents must be provided before the video call.
- The video call should be scheduled in advance, and each employee should log in at a specific time to avoid delays.
- The HPC Admin should follow the guidelines of the Deutschen Forschungsnetzes for the Videoident-Verfahren to ensure the security and accuracy of the identity verification process.

## Conclusion
The user successfully obtained access to the RRZE HPC systems by following the application and identity verification process. The HPC Admin provided clear instructions and adapted the process to accommodate the needs of the user and their employees working from home. The ticket was closed after the accounts were created and the activation letters were sent.
---

### 2020060542000462_Dateien%20_%20Compiler%20verschwinden%20in%20TMPDIR.md
# Ticket 2020060542000462

 # HPC-Support Ticket: Dateien / Compiler verschwinden in TMPDIR

## Keywords
- TMPDIR
- Compiler
- GCC
- Clang
- rsync
- mtime
- cfengine
- Automatisches Aufräumen

## Problem
- User nutzt Woody-Cluster für Masterarbeit.
- Compilerversionen von GCC und Clang werden in `$TMPDIR/$PBS_JOBID` kopiert.
- Nach etwa 2.5 Stunden verschwinden die Ordner für GCC und Clang spurlos.
- Andere Ordner und generierte Programme bleiben erhalten.

## Root Cause
- Automatisches Aufräumen durch cfengine löscht Dateien/Ordner in `$TMPDIR` mit einem Änderungsdatum (mtime) > 14 Tage.
- User kopiert Ordner mit `rsync -a`, wodurch das Änderungsdatum beibehalten wird.
- Änderungsdatum der Compiler-Ordner ist > 14 Tage, wodurch sie gelöscht werden.

## Lösung
- Entfernen der `-a` Option beim Kopieren mit `rsync`, um das Änderungsdatum zu aktualisieren.

## Diskussion
- HPC Admins diskutieren, ob das Löschen in `/scratch` durch cfengine notwendig ist oder besser im Epilog erfolgen sollte.
- User fragt nach Dokumentation des automatischen Aufräumens, was jedoch nicht dokumentiert ist.

## Fazit
- Automatisches Aufräumen in Kombination mit `rsync -a` führt zum Löschen von Dateien/Ordnern.
- Entfernen der `-a` Option beim Kopieren verhindert das Löschen.

## Weitere Schritte
- Überprüfen, ob das automatische Aufräumen durch cfengine notwendig ist.
- Eventuell Dokumentation des automatischen Aufräumens auf der Webseite hinzufügen.
---

### 2024010942003258_bcpc002h%20account%20validity.md
# Ticket 2024010942003258

 ```markdown
# HPC-Support Ticket: Account Validity Extension

## Keywords
- Account Validity
- Account Extension
- HPC Portal
- Manager Role
- Archived Accounts

## Problem
- User's Tier3 account "bcpc002h" expired at the end of 2023.

## Solution
- The account has been migrated to the HPC portal.
- The user needs to request an extension from the project managers (Peter Gemeiner, Nico Hommes, or Wolfgang Utz).
- Managers can extend the account by following these steps:
  1. Navigate to Manager -> Projekte -> $PROJEKT -> Accounts -> Archivierte Accounts -> $ACCOUNT -> Account bearbeiten.
  2. Set the status to "Aktiv" and update the new validity date.
  3. Note that changes may take a few hours to reflect on the frontends.

## General Learnings
- Account extensions require manager intervention.
- Managers can reactivate and extend account validity through the HPC portal.
- Changes to account status and validity may take a few hours to propagate.
```
---

### 2020072042003492_pigz%20job%20on%20Meggie%20-%20iww1003h.md
# Ticket 2020072042003492

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: pigz Job Optimization

## Keywords
- pigz
- Multi-threading
- Node allocation
- Oversubscription
- Job optimization

## Problem
- User allocated 8 nodes for a pigz job, which only uses multi-threading on a single node.
- Specified `-p 160` for pigz, leading to oversubscription of the local nodes.

## Root Cause
- Misunderstanding of pigz's multi-threading capabilities and optimal resource allocation.

## Solution
- Allocate only a single node for pigz jobs.
- Adjust the `-p` parameter to an optimal value that does not oversubscribe the node.

## Lessons Learned
- **Resource Allocation**: Understand the resource requirements of the software being used.
- **Oversubscription**: Avoid oversubscribing nodes to ensure optimal performance.
- **Communication**: Promptly cancel jobs that are not optimally configured to free up resources.

## Recommendations
- Educate users on the proper use of multi-threading and resource allocation for specific software.
- Monitor job submissions for optimal resource usage and provide feedback to users as needed.
```
---

- User required a specific node configuration for benchmarking.

- After completion, the reservation was lifted, and the node was reset to default settings.

1. **Request**: User requested a tg09x-node with NPS=1, THP=always, and numa_balancing=0.
2. **Reservation**: HPC Admin reserved tg097 and confirmed the settings.

- Proper communication and confirmation of node settings are crucial for user satisfaction.
- Reserving nodes for specific configurations ensures smooth benchmarking processes.

## Follow-Up
- Ensure users report completion of benchmarks to return nodes to normal operation promptly.
- Maintain clear communication channels for any follow-up questions or issues.

### 42031705_shell%20script%20l%C3%83%C2%A4sst%20sich%20nicht%09ausf%C3%83%C2%BChren.md
# Ticket 42031705

 ```markdown
# Shell Script Execution Issue on HPC Cluster

## Keywords
- Shell script
- Permission denied
- Execution error
- HPC cluster
- Script execution

## Problem Description
The user encountered an error when trying to execute a shell script on the HPC cluster. The error message was:
```
/usr/bin/. : Permission denied
```
The user had already changed the permissions for the script, but the issue persisted.

## Root Cause
The issue was due to incorrect script execution commands. The user was attempting to execute the script using a command that is not valid in the current shell environment.

## Solution
The HPC Admin provided the following solutions:
1. Ensure the script has execute permissions (`x` bit set) and run it using `./run.sh`.
2. Alternatively, use `source run.sh` to execute the script in the current shell. Note that `source` works in (t)csh, while `.` works in bash.

## General Learning
- Ensure the script has the correct permissions to be executed.
- Use the appropriate command to execute the script based on the shell environment.
- Understand the difference between `source` and `.` commands in different shells.
```
---

### 201808239000628_CFS-Jobs%20auf%20Woody%20_%20mp24002h.md
# Ticket 201808239000628

 ```markdown
# Lessons Learned from HPC-Support Ticket Conversation

## Subject: CFS-Jobs auf Woody / mp24002h

### Keywords:
- Performance Issues
- Thread Management
- Compiler Optimization
- PHIST/GHOST
- Complex Arithmetic
- Intel vs. GCC Compiler

### Root Cause:
- The user's CFS jobs were not running efficiently on Woody due to several issues:
  - Excessive thread creation leading to more threads than available cores.
  - Inefficient use of packed instructions and SSE/AVX.
  - Significant time spent in the GCC helper routine `__muldc3` due to complex multiplications.

### Detailed Issues:
1. **Thread Management:**
   - The job was creating more threads than available cores, leading to inefficient CPU usage.
   - Example: `14115 mp24002h 20 0 6950544 4.408g 28816 S 0.0 14.1 92:35.55 cfs_icc_rel`

2. **Performance:**
   - The job was performing only scalar FLOPS, not utilizing packed instructions or SSE/AVX.
   - Peak performance was significantly lower than the node's capability (4-5 GFlop/s vs. 200 GFlop/s).

3. **Complex Arithmetic:**
   - The job spent a significant amount of time in the GCC helper routine `__muldc3`, likely due to complex multiplications.
   - This routine was not optimized, leading to performance bottlenecks.

### Solutions:
1. **Compiler Optimization:**
   - Switching to the Intel compiler and ensuring the use of `BUILD_TYPE=Release` improved performance significantly.
   - Example: `Intel: bei komplex double (Z) ist die roofline Performance vileler Kernel ungefaehr die Haelfte der Zahl die ich bei reell double (D) kriege.`

2. **Thread Management:**
   - Ensure that the number of threads does not exceed the number of available cores.
   - Example: `Ghost erzeugt teilweise deutlich mehr threads als Kerne da sind. Die werden fürs tasking verwendet.`

3. **Compiler Flags:**
   - Avoid overriding compiler flags for external libraries like PHIST/GHOST.
   - Example: `wir hatten inzwischen festgestellt, dass im CFS Buildsystem die Compiler Flags fuer externe Bibliotheken wie ghost und phist vorgegeben werden, dadurch wurden die Flags die wir (ghost/phist) fuer sinnvoll halten ganz oder teilweise ueberschrieben.`

### Conclusion:
- The user's job performance improved significantly by switching to the Intel compiler and ensuring proper thread management.
- Avoiding the override of compiler flags for external libraries also contributed to better performance.
- The issue with complex arithmetic and the `__muldc3` routine was mitigated by using the Intel compiler.

### References:
- [OTRS Ticket](https://www.helpdesk.rrze.fau.de/otrs/index.pl?Action=AgentZoom&TicketID=666201)
- [Stack Overflow Discussion on `__muldc3`](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/49438158/why-is-muldc3-called-when-two-stdcomplex-are-multiplied)
```
---

### 2021111942001665_Early-Alex%20%22Anselm%20Horn%22%20_%20mfbi05.md
# Ticket 2021111942001665

 # HPC Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

## Keywords
- HPC Cluster Access
- GPU Partition
- Slurm Job Scripts
- Amber20, AmberTools21
- Molecular Dynamics Simulation
- Web Interface for Cluster Status
- sinfo Command

## General Learnings
- **Cluster Access and Configuration**: Users need to be granted access to specific partitions and GPUs.
- **Software Availability**: Specific software versions (e.g., Amber20, AmberTools21) are available as modules.
- **Job Script Adaptation**: Slurm job scripts from other clusters may need minor adjustments for partition and GPU specifications.
- **Cluster Status Monitoring**: While a web interface for monitoring cluster status is not a high priority, the `sinfo` command provides an overview.

## Root Cause of Problems
- **User Access**: Users need to be explicitly granted access to specific partitions and GPUs.
- **Job Script Configuration**: Job scripts need to specify the correct partition and GPU resources.

## Solutions
- **User Access**: HPC Admins can grant access to specific users for required partitions and GPUs.
- **Job Script Configuration**: Users should update their job scripts to include the correct partition and GPU specifications (e.g., `#SBATCH --partition=a40`, `#SBATCH --gres:gpu:a4:1`).
- **Cluster Status Monitoring**: Users can use the `sinfo` command to get an overview of the cluster status.

## Example Conversation
### User Request
- **Access Request**: User requests access to the HPC cluster for specific GPUs and software.
- **Job Script Configuration**: User inquires about adapting job scripts from another cluster.
- **Cluster Status Monitoring**: User asks about a web interface for monitoring cluster status.

### HPC Admin Response
- **Access Granted**: HPC Admin grants access to the specified partition and GPUs.
- **Software Availability**: HPC Admin provides information on available software modules.
- **Job Script Adaptation**: HPC Admin suggests minor adjustments to job scripts for partition and GPU specifications.
- **Cluster Status Monitoring**: HPC Admin recommends using the `sinfo` command for monitoring cluster status.

## Additional Notes
- **Documentation**: Users are directed to the cluster documentation for detailed information.
- **Future Plans**: While a web interface for cluster status is planned, it is not a high priority.

This analysis provides a concise overview of the key points and solutions discussed in the HPC support ticket conversation, which can be useful for future reference and troubleshooting similar issues.
---

### 2022032942003827_Meggie%20Fehlermeldungen.md
# Ticket 2022032942003827

 # HPC Support Ticket: OVITOS Script Errors

## Keywords
- OVITOS
- Qt platform plugin
- Dependencies
- Libraries
- Reinstallation

## Problem Description
The user encountered an error when running OVITOS scripts on the HPC system:
```
qt.qpa.plugin: Could not load the Qt platform plugin "minimal" in ""
even though it was found.
This application failed to start because no Qt platform plugin could be
initialized. Reinstalling the application may fix this problem.
Available platform plugins are: minimal, offscreen, vnc, xcb.
```

## Root Cause
The error was due to missing dependencies for OVITOS on the compute nodes. Specifically, the following libraries were missing:
- `libfontconfig.so.1`
- `libfreetype.so.6`
- `libcrypto.so.1.1`

Additionally, other dependencies such as `dejavu-fonts-common`, `dejavu-sans-fonts`, `fontpackages-filesystem`, and `libpng` were required.

## Solution
The HPC Admin identified the missing dependencies and installed them on the compute nodes using `dsh`. The package list was updated accordingly.

## Follow-up
The user confirmed that OVITOS was functioning again after the dependencies were installed. However, a workaround for LAMMPS involving copied libraries was still necessary at the time of the follow-up.

## General Learning
- Ensure all required dependencies for software are installed on the compute nodes.
- Use `dsh` to distribute and install missing libraries across nodes.
- Update the package list to include new dependencies.
- Confirm with the user that the issue is resolved after making changes.
---

### 42157922_ADF-lima%20problem.md
# Ticket 42157922

 ```markdown
# HPC-Support Ticket: ADF-lima Problem

## Subject
ADF calculations systematically failing on Lima

## User Issue
- **Error Message**:
  ```
  PBS: job killed: node 2 (l0525) requested job terminate, 'EOF' (code 1099)
  received SISTER_EOF attempting to communicate with sister MOM's
  forrtl: error (78): process killed (SIGTERM)
  ```
- **Description**: ADF calculations consistently fail with the above error, regardless of the number of nodes or different inputs. The same calculation works for another user.

## HPC Admin Response
- **Root Cause**: Jobs are crashing the nodes due to running out of memory.
- **Possible Reasons**:
  1. The job uses too much memory.
  2. Large files are being placed in `/tmp`, which is located in RAM on Lima nodes.
- **Solution**:
  - Use the environment variable `$TMPDIR` instead of `/tmp` for temporary files.
  - Ensure that the job does not exceed the available memory on the nodes.

## Keywords
- ADF calculations
- Memory issues
- `/tmp` usage
- `$TMPDIR`
- Node crash
- PBS error
- SIGTERM

## General Learning
- **Memory Management**: Be mindful of memory usage in jobs to prevent node crashes.
- **Temporary File Storage**: Use `$TMPDIR` for temporary files instead of `/tmp` to avoid consuming RAM.
- **Error Analysis**: Understand common error messages and their implications for troubleshooting.
```
---

# HPC Support Ticket: HPC Extension

- Communicate clearly with all parties involved to ensure timely submission and processing of forms.
- Be aware of account expiration dates and plan extensions accordingly to avoid disruptions.

### 2023042842001952_Computestunden%20MASCARA.md
# Ticket 2023042842001952

 # HPC Support Ticket: Computestunden MASCARA

## Keywords
- ALEX-Cluster
- MASCARA Projekt
- HPC Portal
- SSO Login
- SSH Key
- PI (Principal Investigator)
- Technical Contact

## Problem
- User requested access to the ALEX-Cluster to utilize the open capacity of the MASCARA project.
- The PI (Principal Investigator) of the project had left the institution, and their account was no longer active.

## Root Cause
- The PI's account was inactive, making it impossible for them to invite the user to the project via the HPC portal.
- The actual PI was not correctly registered in the portal, leading to confusion about project ownership.

## Solution
1. **Identify the Correct PI**: The actual PI of the MASCARA project was identified and confirmed.
2. **SSO Login**: The user was instructed to log in to the HPC portal using SSO (Single Sign-On).
3. **Project Invitation**: Once the user was logged in and visible in the user list, they were added to the project.
4. **SSH Key Upload**: The user was instructed to upload an SSH key to the portal to enable login to the ALEX-Cluster.

## General Learnings
- Ensure that PIs and technical contacts are correctly registered and active in the HPC portal.
- In case a PI is not reachable, the project can be transferred to a new PI or technical contact.
- Users need to log in to the portal via SSO and upload an SSH key to access the cluster.

## References
- [HPC FAQs](https://hpc.fau.de/faqs/)
- [HPC Portal](https://portal.hpc.fau.de/)
---

### 2017081542000802_Zugriff%20auf%20Windows%20Cluster%20geht%20nicht.md
# Ticket 2017081542000802

 # HPC-Support Ticket: Zugriff auf Windows Cluster geht nicht

## Keywords
- Windows Cluster
- Passwort
- Domain
- Matlab Compiler Runtime
- Installation
- Rechte

## Problem
- User kann nicht auf den Windows Cluster zugreifen, da das Passwort nicht funktioniert.
- User benötigt die Matlab Compiler Runtime Version 8.2 (Matlab 2013b 32 bit), hat aber keine Rechte zur Installation.

## Ursache
- Falsche Domain beim Login verwendet.
- Fehlende Rechte zur Installation von Software auf dem Windows Cluster.

## Lösung
- Verwendung der korrekten Domain "fauad" beim Login.
- Installation der Matlab Compiler Runtime Version 8.2 durch den zuständigen Admin nach dessen Rückkehr aus dem Urlaub.

## Allgemeine Erkenntnisse
- Bei Login-Problemen sollte die korrekte Domain überprüft werden.
- Software-Installationen auf dem Windows Cluster können nur durch den zuständigen Admin durchgeführt werden.
- Bei Abwesenheit des zuständigen Admins müssen alternative Lösungen oder Geduld gefunden werden.

## Status
- Ticket geschlossen, da das Problem gelöst wurde.
---

 ```markdown
# HPC-Support Ticket: Missing Modules from /opt on Testcluster

## Keywords
- Module
- /opt
- Testcluster
- Shell
- cfengine
- cfagent.conf
- Reboot

## Problem Description
User reported that after logging into `testfront`, the modules from `/opt` were missing, which were previously available. The user had to manually use `module use -a /opt/modules/modulefiles/testcluster` to restore them.

## Root Cause
The modules from `/opt` were not automatically loaded upon login, which was the expected behavior.

## Troubleshooting Steps
1. HPC Admin initially mentioned that the issue should be resolved in a few hours as the configuration was being distributed via `pollux:/cfengine/masterfiles/inputs/cfagent.conf`.
2. HPC Admin later noted that the issue was still not resolved and questioned if a reboot was necessary.

## Solution
The issue was expected to be resolved automatically through the distribution of the configuration file. However, the final resolution or confirmation of the fix was not explicitly stated in the conversation.

## Lessons Learned
- Ensure that module paths are correctly configured and automatically loaded upon user login.
- Verify the distribution and application of configuration files using tools like `cfengine`.
- Consider the need for a system reboot when applying certain configurations.
```
---

### 2021050342001545_Fehler%20qdel.tinygpu.md
# Ticket 2021050342001545

 # HPC Support Ticket: Error with `qdel.tinygpu`

## Keywords
- `qdel.tinygpu`
- `qdel.tinyfat`
- Job ID
- Torque server
- DNS issue
- Configuration problem

## Problem Description
A user reported that the `qdel.tinygpu` command was not functioning correctly to stop jobs. The command returned an error indicating a nonexistent job ID. The issue was reproducible with both `qdel.tinygpu` and `qdel.tinyfat`.

## Root Cause
- The Torque server was assigning job IDs with the full domain name (e.g., `nummer.tgadm1.rrze.uni-erlangen.de`) instead of the traditional format (e.g., `nummer.tgadm1`).
- The DNS server (131.188.0.10) was returning incorrect domain names, causing issues with the automatic appending of the domain name.

## Solution
- The issue was identified as an internal configuration problem related to DNS.
- The configuration was corrected, and the `qdel` command should now function normally.

## Lessons Learned
- Ensure that the Torque server is correctly configured to assign job IDs in the expected format.
- Verify that the DNS server is functioning correctly and returning the correct domain names.
- Regularly check for configuration issues after system restarts or updates.

## Actions Taken
- HPC Admins identified the issue with the DNS server and corrected the configuration.
- The user was informed that the issue had been resolved and that the `qdel` command should now work as expected.

## Follow-Up
- Monitor the system for any recurrence of the issue.
- Ensure that the DNS server is regularly checked for correct functionality.

---

This documentation can be used to troubleshoot similar issues in the future.
---

- PBS should automatically update `limits.conf` to manage login limits.

### 2021061842001266_Jobs%20auf%20Meggie%20-%20gwgk006h%20894949%2C%20894943%2C%20894947%2C%20894945%2C%20894941.md
# Ticket 2021061842001266

 # HPC Support Ticket Analysis

## Subject
Jobs auf Meggie - gwgk006h 894949, 894943, 894947, 894945, 894941

## Keywords
- Job monitoring
- Performance issues
- Job verification
- Resource allocation
- Job termination

## Summary
- **Issue**: Jobs on Meggie (894949, 894943, 894947, 894945, 894941) were flagged in standard job monitoring for poor performance.
- **Root Cause**: The jobs showed very low FLOPS and memory bandwidth, indicating they were not performing as expected.
- **User Response**: The user initially reported that the scripts were running correctly and producing results.
- **Admin Response**: The HPC Admin questioned the performance of `real.exe` jobs and whether they should be run massively parallel.
- **Solution**: The user was advised to run `real.exe` on a single node due to its low computational requirements. The user requested the HPC Admin to terminate the jobs due to lack of access.

## Lessons Learned
- **Performance Monitoring**: Regular monitoring of job performance is crucial to identify inefficient resource usage.
- **Resource Allocation**: Understanding the computational requirements of jobs is essential for efficient resource allocation.
- **User Communication**: Clear communication with users about job performance and resource usage helps in resolving issues efficiently.
- **Job Termination**: Users may require assistance from HPC Admins to terminate jobs when they lack access.

## Recommendations
- **Performance Analysis**: Conduct a detailed performance analysis of jobs that show poor performance to understand the underlying issues.
- **User Training**: Provide training and documentation on best practices for job submission and resource allocation.
- **Support Communication**: Maintain open lines of communication with users to address performance issues promptly.

## Conclusion
Effective job monitoring and communication with users are key to ensuring efficient use of HPC resources. Understanding the computational requirements of jobs helps in optimizing resource allocation and improving overall system performance.
---

### 2024031442001291_Probleme%20bei%20Verbindung.md
# Ticket 2024031442001291

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: Connection Issues After SSH Key Transition

## Keywords
- SSH Key
- Connection Issues
- Permission Denied
- WinSCP
- Meggie Cluster
- Proxy Jump

## Problem Description
- User unable to connect to Meggie Cluster after transitioning to SSH key authentication.
- Successful connection to proxy jump (`csnhr.nhr.fau.de`) but unable to proceed to Meggie.
- Password authentication fails with "Permission denied."
- WinSCP no longer functional for file transfer.

## Root Cause
- Incorrect SSH key configuration or missing key in the SSH agent.
- WinSCP not configured to use the new SSH key.

## Debug Information
- SSH verbose output (`ssh -vv`) shows attempts to use various private keys but fails to authenticate.
- No Kerberos credentials available.

## Solution
- Follow the steps in the FAQ: [FAQ Link](https://doc.nhr.fau.de/faq/#i-managed-to-log-in-to-t46--with-an-ssh-key--but-get-asked-for-a-password---permission-denied-when-continuing-to-a-cluster-frontend)
- Ensure the SSH key is correctly configured and added to the SSH agent.
- Configure WinSCP to use the new SSH key for file transfers.

## Additional Notes
- If the FAQ steps do not resolve the issue, provide the debug information mentioned in the FAQ to the HPC support team.
- Ensure all necessary SSH keys are present and correctly referenced in the SSH configuration file.
```
---

- Users should explore alternative solutions for LLM requirements.
```

### 2023022142000326_Status%20i%20w%20i%209%200%202%200%20h.md
# Ticket 2023022142000326

 # HPC Support Ticket Analysis

## Subject: Status i w i 9 0 2 0 h

### Keywords:
- HPC Verlängerungs-Antrag (HPC extension request)
- Account Verlängerung (account extension)
- Loginversuch (login attempt)
- SSH-Client (SSH client)
- Benutzernamen (username)

### Summary:
A user reported that an HPC extension request, submitted on January 20, had not been processed. The HPC Admin confirmed that the account was extended but there were issues with the account functionality post-extension.

### Root Cause:
- The user's supervisor reported that the student did not receive the extension.
- The HPC Admin confirmed the extension but noted potential issues with the account.
- Further investigation revealed that there were no login attempts under the correct username, but there was an incorrect login attempt with "ssh iwi9020h" instead of "iwi9020h".

### Solution:
- The HPC Admin confirmed that the account "iwi9020h" was active and no issues were found with the account itself.
- It was suggested that the incorrect login attempt might be due to the user entering "ssh iwi9020h" instead of "iwi9020h" in the SSH client.

### Lessons Learned:
- Always verify that extension requests have been processed correctly.
- Check for incorrect login attempts and ensure users are entering the correct username in SSH clients.
- Communicate clearly with users and their supervisors to resolve any discrepancies in account status.

### Next Steps:
- Inform the user and their supervisor about the correct login procedure.
- Monitor the account for any further issues and provide assistance as needed.

### References:
- HPC Admin: Annette Krisch
- 2nd Level Support Team: Lacey, Dane (fo36fizy), Kuckuk, Sebastian (sisekuck), Lange, Florian (ow86apyf), Ernst, Dominik (te42kyfo), Mayr, Martin

### 2024051942000191_Gel%C3%83%C2%B6schter%20User%20Ordner%20in%20_home_saturn.md
# Ticket 2024051942000191

 ```markdown
# HPC-Support Ticket: Gelöschter User Ordner in /home/saturn

## Keywords
- User data deletion
- Shared folder
- Account expiration
- Data recovery

## Problem Description
- User reported missing data from a shared folder due to account deletion.
- The account `sn0553` was deleted, which contained data needed for the user's PhD work.
- The user assumed the data was safe in a shared folder but discovered it was in a user-specific shared folder.

## Root Cause
- The account `sn0553` expired in August 2023.
- The HPC system's quota management did not handle expired accounts properly, leading to data deletion.

## Solution
- HPC Admin restored the deleted directory.
- The user was able to access the required data again.

## Lessons Learned
- Users should ensure important data is backed up or stored in a truly shared folder, not a user-specific shared folder.
- HPC Admins should regularly check and improve quota management to prevent data loss from expired accounts.
- Communication about account expiration and data management policies should be clear to users.
```
---

### 2024101442006497_hdf5_fortran%20version%201.14.0%20and%20above.md
# Ticket 2024101442006497

 # HPC Support Ticket: hdf5_fortran Version 1.14.0 and Above

## Keywords
- hdf5_fortran
- MPI support
- OpenMPI
- GCC compiler
- Module request

## Summary
A user requested the installation of hdf5_fortran version 1.14.0 or above with MPI support as a module on the HPC system.

## Problem
The user needed hdf5_fortran version 1.14.0 or above with MPI support for their application. The specific MPI and compiler requirements were OpenMPI and GCC.

## Solution
1. **Initial Request**: The user requested hdf5_fortran version 1.14.0 or above with MPI support.
2. **Clarification**: The HPC Admin asked for the specific MPI and compiler requirements.
3. **User Response**: The user specified OpenMPI and GCC (gcc/11.2.0, openmpi/4.1.2-gcc11.2.0).
4. **Module Provision**: The HPC Admin provided the new module "hdf5/1.14.5-ompi-gcc" for the user to try.

## Lessons Learned
- Always specify the MPI and compiler requirements when requesting new modules.
- HPC Admins can quickly provide the necessary modules when given detailed information.

## Conclusion
The user's request was successfully addressed by providing the appropriate module. This ticket serves as a reference for handling similar requests in the future.
---

### 2020051842003008_Cluster%20Anfrage.md
# Ticket 2020051842003008

 # HPC Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

## Keywords
- Cluster access request
- Matlab parallel computing
- HPC systems: TinyEth, Emmy, Woody
- Matlab licenses
- Resource allocation: cores, storage
- Financing and quotas

## General Learnings
- **Cluster Recommendations**: For Matlab parallel computing, clusters like TinyEth, Emmy, or Woody are recommended.
- **License Requirements**: Users must bring their own Matlab licenses for HPC usage.
- **Resource Allocation**: Standard quotas and resource limits apply; special considerations for large binary files on parallel file systems.
- **Financing**: Basic HPC services are free for FAU scientists; special conditions apply for additional resources or external users.

## Root Cause of the Problem
- User requires access to an HPC cluster for Matlab parallel computing.
- Concerns about resource allocation and financing.

## Solution
- **Cluster Selection**: Emmy cluster is suitable for the user's needs.
- **Financing**: User can specify "Lehrstuhlmittel" and the RRZE customer number in the HPC form.
- **Resource Allocation**: The requested resources (100 cores for 6 hours per week and 1 TB storage) are within acceptable limits. Users should be aware of file system usage guidelines to avoid disruptions.

## Additional Notes
- **License Compatibility**: Matlab licenses formally for macOS should not be an issue.
- **File System Usage**: Large binary files can be stored on $FASTTMP with specific usage guidelines to avoid performance issues.

This documentation can be used as a reference for future support cases involving similar requests or issues.
---

### 2019062342000207_Job%20334609%20-%20fhnm001h.md
# Ticket 2019062342000207

 ```markdown
# HPC-Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

## Subject: Job 334609 - fhnm001h

### Problem:
- User's MATLAB/R2019a job failed due to a license error.
- The host ID of the server did not match the user ID.
- User installed MATLAB/R2019a as a private student version.

### Root Cause:
- MATLAB license is tied to a specific host ID and user ID.
- The HPC cluster's dynamic nature makes it difficult to tie the license to a specific node.

### Solutions Attempted:
1. **User's Request:**
   - User asked if it's possible to rent a MATLAB/R2019a Linux license for a few months.
   - User suggested upgrading the TH-Nürnberg license server to version 2019a.

2. **HPC Admin's Response:**
   - HPC Admin explained that the idea of an HPC cluster is to not tie software to a specific node.
   - HPC Admin suggested contacting the TH-Nürnberg rechenzentrum for a license server upgrade.
   - HPC Admin mentioned that RRZE-Campus licenses can only be rented by university institutions or affiliated colleges.

3. **Further Communication:**
   - User forwarded TH-Nürnberg's response to HPC Admin.
   - HPC Admin and TH-Nürnberg discussed the license server ports and connectivity issues.
   - User encountered GPU issues and contacted MathWorks support.

### Final Resolution:
- The license server issue was resolved, and the user was able to access the license.
- User encountered GPU issues, which were diagnosed as not related to the HPC cluster's TinyGPU nodes.
- HPC Admin provided a job script to verify GPU functionality on TinyGPU nodes.

### Keywords:
- MATLAB/R2019a
- License error
- Host ID
- User ID
- HPC cluster
- TH-Nürnberg
- RRZE-Campus license
- GPU issues
- TinyGPU cluster

### General Learnings:
- Licensing issues with node-specific software in an HPC environment.
- Importance of communication between different support teams (HPC Admin, TH-Nürnberg, MathWorks).
- Diagnosing and resolving GPU-related issues on HPC clusters.

### Next Steps:
- Ensure proper communication channels between HPC Admin and external support teams.
- Document common license and GPU issues for future reference.
- Provide users with clear instructions for diagnosing and resolving similar issues.
```
---

### 2023042142002946_Amber%20%28fritz_alex%29%20f%C3%83%C2%BCr%20extrem%20gro%C3%83%C2%9Fe%20Systeme%3F.md
# Ticket 2023042142002946

 # HPC Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

## Keywords
- Amber
- Large systems
- Program constant change
- Recompilation
- Gridhi
- mdin_ewald_dat.F90
- pmemd
- Module
- Memory issues
- A40
- A100
- MPI variants
- CUDA version

## General Learnings
- Users may need to modify program constants and recompile software for large jobs.
- Memory issues can arise with large jobs, requiring specific hardware or additional memory allocation.
- MPI variants and CUDA versions may need to be compiled for certain jobs.
- Users can request specific hardware configurations using SLURM directives.

## Root Cause of the Problem
- The user's large Amber job required a change in the program constant "gridhi" from 512 to 1024 in the file "mdin_ewald_dat.F90" and recompilation of pmemd.
- The job experienced memory issues on A40 and required specific A100 configurations for successful execution.

## Solution
- HPC Admins compiled new Amber modules with the increased gridhi value and made them available to the user.
- The user was advised to use `#SBATCH -C a100_80` to select 80GB A100 nodes for jobs with high memory requirements.
- HPC Admins also compiled MPI variants of the CUDA version for further testing.
---

### 2018071742004733_Down-time%20Lima%20and%20Emmy%20cluster.md
# Ticket 2018071742004733

 # HPC Support Ticket: Down-time Lima and Emmy Cluster

## Keywords
- Down-time
- Lima cluster
- Emmy cluster
- Job queue
- Scheduled maintenance

## Problem
- User noticed that all jobs on the Lima cluster were not running.
- Attempted to use the Emmy cluster but encountered the same issue.
- User expected downtime on Sunday based on previous notices.

## Root Cause
- Long queue with waiting jobs on both clusters.

## Solution
- No downtime or schedule change.
- Jobs will automatically start once it's their turn in the queue.

## Lessons Learned
- Long job queues can give the appearance of cluster downtime.
- Always check the job queue status before assuming downtime.
- Communicate effectively about expected high load periods.

## Action Taken
- HPC Admin informed the user about the long queue.
- No further action was required as the clusters were functioning normally.

## Follow-up
- Monitor job queue lengths to anticipate and communicate potential delays to users.
- Ensure users are aware of how to check job queue status.
---

### 2020021242002098_woodycap4.md

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: woodycap4

## Keywords
- woodycap4
- Login issues
- Jupyter Notebooks
- Fileserver load
- $HOME
- $VAULT

## Summary
- **User Reported Issue**: Unable to log in to woodycap4, existing connections unable to perform simple tasks like running `top`.
- **Root Cause**: High load on fileservers for $HOME and $VAULT due to runaway user jobs.
- **Impact**: Increased response times for file operations, leading to various hangs and unusual behavior.

## Solution
- **Admin Response**: No specific issue with woodycap4; high load on fileservers caused the problem.
- **Recommendation**: Monitor and manage user jobs to prevent excessive load on fileservers.

## Lessons Learned
- High load on fileservers can cause widespread issues, including login problems and performance degradation.
- Regular monitoring of user jobs and fileserver performance is essential to maintain system stability.
- Communicate with users about the impact of their jobs on shared resources to prevent similar issues in the future.
```
---

### 2022011042001501_Jobs%20auf%20TinyGPU%20tg06a%20%5Bbcpc000h%5D.md
# Ticket 2022011042001501

 # HPC Support Ticket: Jobs auf TinyGPU tg06a

## Keywords
- GPU failure
- RTX2080TI
- TinyGPU-Knoten tg06a
- Job monitoring
- Simulation results
- Maintenance

## Summary
A GPU failure on the TinyGPU node tg06a affected several jobs. The HPC Admins identified the issue and notified the user to check their simulation results. The node was reserved for maintenance and will be unavailable until further notice.

## Root Cause
- One of the RTX2080TI GPUs on the TinyGPU node tg06a was malfunctioning.
- Jobs running on the affected GPU were likely running slowly or entirely on the CPU.

## Affected Jobs
- JobID: 204124, JobName: epiGiR2, StartDate: 2022-01-05, StartTime: 18:43:08
- JobID: 204189, JobName: epiGiR2, StartDate: 2022-01-06, StartTime: 08:30:44
- JobID: 204439, JobName: epiGiR3, StartDate: 2022-01-07, StartTime: 10:43:43
- JobID: 204594, JobName: epiGiR3, StartDate: 2022-01-08, StartTime: 00:09:18
- JobID: 204752, JobName: epiGiR3, StartDate: 2022-01-08, StartTime: 13:33:34
- JobID: 204843, JobName: epiGiR3, StartDate: 2022-01-09, StartTime: 02:57:59
- JobID: 204922, JobName: epiGiR3, StartDate: 2022-01-09, StartTime: 16:22:45

## Solution
- The user was advised to check the simulation results of the affected jobs.
- The node was reserved for maintenance and will be unavailable until the issue is resolved.
- The user confirmed that only one job (epiGiR2) crashed and was restarted, while other jobs (epiGiR3) completed normally, albeit slowly.

## Follow-up
- The HPC Admins will continue to monitor the node and perform necessary maintenance.
- The user should continue to monitor their jobs and report any further issues.

## Lessons Learned
- Regular monitoring of GPU health is crucial to prevent job failures.
- Communication with users about potential issues can help mitigate the impact on their work.
- Providing a list of affected jobs helps users to focus their efforts on checking the most relevant results.
---
