### 2022100642004356_Unklare%20Fehlermeldung%20bei%20Benutzung%20des%20Meggie-Clusters.md
# Ticket 2022100642004356

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: Unklare Fehlermeldung bei Benutzung des Meggie-Clusters

## Subject
Unklare Fehlermeldung bei Benutzung des Meggie-Clusters

## Keywords
- Meggie-Cluster
- Star-CCM+
- slurmstepd
- Job Cancellation
- Time Limit
- PBS
- Slurm
- sbatch
- Default-Laufzeit

## Problem Description
Der Benutzer erhält eine Fehlermeldung beim Vernetzen auf dem Meggie-Cluster mit Star-CCM+:
```
slurmstepd: error: *** JOB 2012793 ON m0332 CANCELLED AT 2022-10-06T20:34:09 DUE TO TIME LIMIT ***
```

## Root Cause
1. Der Job wird nach 10 Minuten (Default-Laufzeit) abgebrochen.
2. Verwendung eines Mix aus PBS und Slurm Kommandos.
3. Falsche Nutzung von `sbatch` Syntax.

## Solution
1. Korrekte Nutzung der Slurm-Kommandos anstelle von PBS-Kommandos.
2. Richtige Syntax für `sbatch`:
   ```
   sbatch [OPTIONS(0)...] [ : [OPTIONS(N)...]] script(0) [args(0)...]
   ```
3. Optionen nach dem Jobscript werden bei Slurm an das Jobskript übergeben und nicht mehr vom `sbatch` ausgewertet.

## Additional Information
- Cluster: Meggie
- Job-ID: 2012793
- Pfad zum Output-File: Nicht angegeben
- Pfad zum Script: Nicht angegeben

## Lessons Learned
- Stellen Sie sicher, dass die richtigen Kommandos für das verwendete Job-Scheduling-System (Slurm) verwendet werden.
- Überprüfen Sie die Syntax von `sbatch` und stellen Sie sicher, dass die Optionen korrekt übergeben werden.
- Default-Laufzeiten können zu Job-Abbruch führen, wenn nicht angepasst.
```
---

### 2024030142001949_Ablauf%20HPC%20account%20in%20IDM.md
# Ticket 2024030142001949

 # HPC Support Ticket: Expiration Notice in IDM

## Keywords
- HPC Account Expiration
- IDM Portal
- HPC Portal
- Account Validity
- Migration

## Summary
A user received an email stating that their HPC account had expired via the IDM portal, despite the account being active in the new HPC portal. The user sought clarification on whether the email could be ignored due to the system migration.

## Root Cause
The email was automatically generated by the IDM portal indicating the expiration of the HPC service. This occurred due to the ongoing migration to the new HPC portal.

## Solution
The HPC Admin confirmed that the email could be ignored. The new HPC portal is the authoritative source for account validity, and the expiration date shown there is the relevant one.

## General Learnings
- During system migrations, users may receive automated notifications that can be misleading.
- The new HPC portal is the primary source for account status and validity.
- Users should be informed in advance about such migrations to avoid confusion.

## Actions Taken
- The HPC Admin confirmed the migration process and advised the user to ignore the automated email.
- The user was reassured that their account is still active in the new HPC portal.

## Recommendations
- Ensure clear communication with users during system migrations.
- Provide guidance on which system or portal is the authoritative source for account information.
- Update documentation to reflect changes in account management processes.
---

### 2019032242001393_Regarding%20Meggie%20cluster.md
# Ticket 2019032242001393

 # HPC Support Ticket: Meggie Cluster Simulation Setup

## Keywords
- Meggie cluster
- Emmy cluster
- Batch queuing system
- Job scripts
- mpirun_rrze
- mpirun commands
- Pinning

## Problem
- User can log in to Meggie cluster but is unsure about running simulations.
- User needs to know if there are differences in running simulations on Meggie compared to Emmy cluster.

## Root Cause
- Meggie cluster uses a different batch queuing system than Emmy cluster.
- Job scripts require major modifications.
- `mpirun_rrze` is not available on Meggie; native `mpirun` commands must be used.

## Solution
- **Account Enabled**: HPC Admins have enabled the user's account on Meggie.
- **Job Script Modifications**: User needs to modify job scripts to be compatible with Meggie's batch queuing system.
- **mpirun Commands**: Use native `mpirun` commands instead of `mpirun_rrze`. Note that these commands do not have RRZE's extension for simple pinning.

## General Learnings
- Different clusters may use different batch queuing systems, requiring job script modifications.
- Specific commands like `mpirun_rrze` may not be available on all clusters; native commands should be used instead.
- Always check cluster-specific documentation or consult HPC Admins for guidance on running simulations on a new cluster.

## Next Steps
- User should review Meggie cluster documentation for job script modifications.
- User should test simulations using native `mpirun` commands.
- If further assistance is needed, contact HPC Admins or the 2nd Level Support team.
---

### 2016042942001562_Frage%20bez%C3%83%C2%BCglich%20der%20Dienstleistung%20HPC%20--%20%28Benutzerkennung%3A%20yc27ypuh%29.md
# Ticket 2016042942001562

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket Analysis

## Subject: Frage bezüglich der Dienstleistung HPC -- (Benutzerkennung: yc27ypuh)

### Keywords:
- Account Verlängerung
- Softwarelizenzen
- Verzeichnisse
- Befugnisse
- Lehrstuhl
- Lizenzpools
- RRZE-Verzeichnis
- Active Directory

### Problem:
- User's account expired on 31.03.2016 and was renewed in the first week of April for an additional 6 months.
- Since the renewal, the user has experienced issues obtaining licenses for flow simulation software.
- Concerns about whether all directories, permissions, and affiliations were correctly reinstated during the renewal process.
- Discrepancies noted between entries in the "RRZE-Verzeichnis" and "Active Directory."

### Root Cause:
- Possible incomplete or incorrect reinstatement of directories, permissions, and affiliations during the account renewal process.

### Solution:
- HPC Admins should verify that all directories, permissions, and affiliations were correctly reinstated.
- Check for discrepancies between the "RRZE-Verzeichnis" and "Active Directory."
- Ensure that the user's account is properly linked to the necessary license pools and Lehrstuhl.

### Additional Information:
- Refer to Ticket#2016042942001553 for related information.

### General Learning:
- Account renewals should include a thorough check of all associated directories, permissions, and affiliations to prevent issues with software licenses and access.
- Regularly review and update documentation on account renewal procedures to ensure consistency and completeness.
```

### 2024073142001617_Advice%20on%20updating%20libc.so.6%20from%202.28%20to%20%3E2.32%20-%20b207dd10_b211dd17.md
# Ticket 2024073142001617

 # HPC Support Ticket: Updating libc.so.6 Locally

## Keywords
- libc.so.6
- Local upgrade
- Apptainer container
- RHEL 8

## Problem
- User requires libc.so.6 version >2.32.
- Current system version is 2.28.
- User is not a sudo user and does not plan to upgrade globally.

## Solution
- **Advice from HPC Admin:**
  - libc is an integral part of the Linux distribution and cannot be updated locally.
  - Recommendation: Use a version of the application compatible with RHEL 8.
  - Alternative: Build an Apptainer container with an updated libc version independent of the host OS.

## General Learnings
- libc.so.6 is a critical system library and should not be updated locally.
- Apptainer containers can be used to run applications with different library versions without affecting the host system.
- Always check for application versions compatible with the current OS version before considering workarounds.

## Next Steps
- User should attempt to find a compatible application version for RHEL 8.
- If not possible, user should build an Apptainer container with the required libc version.

## Related Resources
- [Apptainer Documentation](https://apptainer.org/docs)
- [RHEL 8 Compatibility](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/8/)
---

### 2023030942001187_TinyGPU%20Cluster%3A%20RTX3080%20GPU%20auf%20work%20partition%20oder%20rtx3080%20partition.md
# Ticket 2023030942001187

 # HPC Support Ticket: TinyGPU Cluster GPU Partitioning

## Keywords
- TinyGPU Cluster
- RTX 2080ti
- RTX 3080
- Partition
- Host Memory
- `sinfo.tinygpu`
- `--gres=gpu:rtx3080:#`
- `--gres=gpu:#`

## Problem
- User was confused about the distribution and availability of RTX 2080ti and RTX 3080 GPUs in the `work` partition of the TinyGPU cluster.
- User needed clarification on how to submit jobs for RTX 3080 GPUs and the differences between the `work` and `rtx3080` partitions.

## Root Cause
- Lack of clear documentation on the availability and distribution of GPUs across partitions.
- Confusion caused by differing host memory values listed for RTX 3080 GPUs in different partitions.

## Solution
- **GPU Availability**: All RTX 3080 GPUs are accessible through both the `work` and `rtx3080` partitions.
- **Submitting Jobs**:
  - To request any GPU (RTX 2080ti or RTX 3080) in the `work` partition: `--gres=gpu:#`
  - To specifically request RTX 3080 GPUs in the `work` partition: `--gres=gpu:rtx3080:#`
  - The `rtx3080` partition should be used if larger host memory is required.
- **Checking GPU Availability**: Use the command `sinfo.tinygpu` to see the current number of available GPUs in each partition.
- **Documentation**: Refer to the [TinyGPU Cluster Documentation](https://hpc.fau.de/systems-services/documentation-instructions/clusters/tinygpu-cluster/) for more details.

## General Learnings
- Understanding GPU partitioning and resource allocation is crucial for efficient job submission.
- Clear documentation and communication about resource distribution help prevent user confusion.
- Specific commands and flags (e.g., `--gres=gpu:rtx3080:#`) can be used to target specific resources.
---

### 2024112242001199_Request%20for%20extension%20of%20runtime%20on%20TinyGPU.md
# Ticket 2024112242001199

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: Request for Extension of Runtime on TinyGPU

## Summary
- **User**: Master's student working on a thesis involving large models.
- **Request**: Extension of runtime limit for jobs on TinyGPU.
- **Reason**: Jobs require more than the 24-hour runtime limit.

## Key Points
- **Initial Request**: User requested an extension of runtime for jobs due to the complexity and size of the models.
- **Admin Response**: No general extension of time limit per user. Recommended checkpointing and restarting jobs.
- **Job Submission**: User submitted jobs with `#SBATCH --hold` directive and requested specific job IDs for extension.
- **Runtime Extension**: Admin granted a 4-day runtime extension for the jobs.
- **Job Cancellation**: One job was canceled due to preemption by a higher priority job.
- **Final Extension**: Admin extended the runtime for the alternative job.

## Root Cause
- **Resource Limitation**: Jobs require more runtime than the default 24-hour limit.
- **Preemption**: Jobs can be canceled if higher priority jobs require the resources.

## Solution
- **Checkpointing**: Implement checkpointing to save job progress and allow for restarting.
- **Job Submission**: Submit jobs with `#SBATCH --hold` directive and request specific job IDs for extension.
- **Admin Intervention**: Admin can extend the runtime for specific jobs upon request and justification.

## Conclusion
- **Runtime Extension**: Admin can extend the runtime for jobs upon request and justification.
- **Checkpointing**: Recommended to implement checkpointing to handle job preemption and ensure efficient use of resources.
```
---

### 2022040442004611_Available%20GPU.md
# Ticket 2022040442004611

 # HPC Support Ticket: Available GPU

## Keywords
- GPU availability
- Queueing system
- `salloc` command
- `tinygpu` nodes
- `alex` cluster
- Early access

## Problem
- User experiences long waiting times or apparent hanging when using the `salloc` command to request GPU resources on `tinygpu` nodes.

## Root Cause
- High demand and limited availability of GPU resources on the `tinygpu` nodes.

## Solution
- **Waiting Time**: Normal for a queueing system, can take up to a few hours.
- **Alternative Cluster**: A second GPU cluster (`alex`) is available but not yet fully operational.
- **Early Access**: Users interested in early access to the `alex` cluster can refer to the provided link for more information.

## General Learning
- GPU resources are in high demand and may require waiting time.
- Additional GPU clusters may be available but could be in early stages of operation.
- Early access programs can provide users with new resources before full operational status.

## Links
- [Early Adopter Alex](https://hpc.fau.de/early-adopter-alex/)
---

### 2023101142003792_Neoverse%20N1%20-%20Software%20auf%20Ampere%20Altra%20Max%20%27lukewarm%27%20im%20Testcluster.md
# Ticket 2023101142003792

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket Conversation Summary

## Keywords
- Neoverse N1
- Ampere Altra Max
- Testcluster
- lukewarm
- ArmPL
- SLURM
- salloc
- optane1
- milan1
- access request

## General Learnings
- **Platform Availability**: The NHR@FAU has integrated an Ampere Altra Max system named 'lukewarm' in the Testcluster.
- **Access Requests**: Users need to request access for specific accounts to the Testcluster.
- **Software Availability**: ArmPL is included in the Arm Compilers and is available on 'lukewarm'.
- **SLURM Job Submission**: Users must connect to the Testcluster frontend 'testfront' and submit an interactive SLURM job to access 'lukewarm'.
- **Node Boot Time**: Nodes may take up to 10 minutes to boot when powered down.
- **Multiple Requests**: Each request should be submitted in a new ticket to ensure proper handling.

## Root Causes and Solutions
- **Access Issue**: User requested access to 'lukewarm' but was unaware of the need to submit a SLURM job.
  - **Solution**: Informed the user to connect to 'testfront' and submit an interactive SLURM job.
- **Software Availability**: User inquired about ArmPL availability.
  - **Solution**: Confirmed that ArmPL is included in the Arm Compilers and provided the module name `acfl/23.10`.
- **Node Boot Time**: User experienced delays in node availability.
  - **Solution**: Explained that nodes may take up to 10 minutes to boot and to retry the SLURM job if it times out.
- **Multiple Requests**: User submitted multiple requests in a single ticket.
  - **Solution**: Advised the user to open a new ticket for each request to ensure proper handling.

## Documentation for Support Employees
- **Accessing Testcluster Nodes**: Users must connect to the Testcluster frontend 'testfront' and submit an interactive SLURM job to access specific nodes.
- **Software Modules**: ArmPL is available in the Arm Compilers module `acfl/23.10`.
- **Node Boot Time**: Nodes may take up to 10 minutes to boot when powered down. Users should retry the SLURM job if it times out.
- **Ticket Handling**: Each request should be submitted in a new ticket to ensure proper handling and notification of the support team.
```
---

### 2024102142000551_Neues%20OTP%20f%C3%83%C2%BCr%20TurboVNC%20auf%20fviz1%20auf%20Frontend%3F.md
# Ticket 2024102142000551

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: New OTP for TurboVNC on fviz1 on Frontend

## Keywords
- TurboVNC
- OTP (One-Time Password)
- VNC Client
- SSH
- SLURM Job
- Frontend
- fviz1
- submitvirtualgljob.sh
- vncpasswd

## Problem Description
The user is able to start a TurboVNC session on `fviz1` using `/apps/virtualgl/submitvirtualgljob.sh` and authenticate with the provided OTP. However, when attempting to reconnect to the session, a new OTP is required. The user can generate a new OTP within the session on `fviz1` or via SSH while the session is running, but not on the frontend because `/opt/TurboVNC` is not available there.

## Root Cause
The message suggesting how to generate a new OTP is from TurboVNC and cannot be modified. The shell referred to in the message is the one from which the VNC session was started, which is a SLURM job running on `fviz1`.

## Solution
- The message from TurboVNC cannot be altered as it is compiled into the software.
- The shell mentioned in the message is the SLURM job shell running on `fviz1`.
- Installing `vncpasswd` on the frontends is technically possible but feels inconsistent and incorrect.

## General Learning
- Understand the context of shell references in TurboVNC messages.
- Recognize the limitations of modifying compiled software messages.
- Consider the consistency and appropriateness of installing software on different nodes.
```
---

### 2021102142002161_%22module%22-command%20not%20found.md
# Ticket 2021102142002161

 # HPC Support Ticket: "module"-command not found

## Keywords
- GPU acceleration
- ML training
- Script execution
- sbatch
- Conda install
- HTTP-Timeout Error
- Proxy settings

## Problem Description
- User is trying to accelerate ML training using a GPU.
- Script (`test.sh`) starts the job correctly but does not produce the desired result (creating and writing to a `logging.txt` file).
- `stderr` file indicates an issue with the script.
- User cannot install Conda packages when using `salloc.tinygpu` to sit directly on the GPU node, resulting in an HTTP-Timeout Error.

## Root Cause
- The first line of the script is missing the `-l` option.
- The node requires proxy settings to reach the internet.

## Solution
- Modify the first line of the script to include `-l`:
  ```bash
  #! /bin/bash -l
  ```
- Set the following proxy variables to enable internet access:
  ```bash
  export http_proxy=http://proxy:80
  export https_proxy=http://proxy:80
  ```
- Refer to the documentation for more information on Python packages: [Python and Jupyter Documentation](https://hpc.fau.de/systems-services/systems-documentation-instructions/special-applications-and-tips-tricks/python-and-jupyter/)

## Additional Information
- Ensure that the script has the correct shebang line and necessary proxy settings for internet access.
- Check the documentation for specific instructions on Python packages and Jupyter.

## Conclusion
- The issue was resolved by correcting the script's shebang line and setting the required proxy variables for internet access.
- The documentation provides further guidance on Python packages and Jupyter.
---

### 2019010242000612_%28eilt%20nicht%29%20Singularity%20mag%20nicht%20auf%20meggie%20frontend%2C%20singularity%20fehlt%20.md
# Ticket 2019010242000612

 # HPC Support Ticket: Singularity Issues on Meggie Frontend and Memoryhog

## Keywords
- Singularity
- Loop devices
- Meggie frontend
- Memoryhog
- Reboot
- AVX instructions
- Squashfs
- Writable image

## Problem Description
- User encountered issues with Singularity on the Meggie frontend, specifically on `meggie2`.
- Error message: `No more available loop devices, try increasing 'max loop devices' in singularity.conf`.
- Singularity was not available on `memoryhog`.

## Root Cause
- The issue on `meggie2` was related to the lack of available loop devices.
- The issue on `memoryhog` was due to the use of AVX instructions not supported by the older Intel processors on `memoryhog`.

## Troubleshooting Steps
1. **Meggie Frontend:**
   - HPC Admin reproduced the issue on `meggie2` and suggested a reboot as a solution.
   - `meggie1` was functioning correctly and was suggested as an alternative login node.
   - After rebooting `meggie2`, the issue was resolved.

2. **Memoryhog:**
   - HPC Admin installed Singularity on `memoryhog`.
   - Investigated kernel messages indicating invalid opcode traps, suggesting incompatibility with AVX instructions.
   - User confirmed that the image was compiled on a different system with newer processors.

## Solution
- **Meggie Frontend:**
  - Rebooting `meggie2` resolved the loop device issue.
  - Users were advised to use `meggie1` as an alternative until the reboot was performed.

- **Memoryhog:**
  - Singularity was installed on `memoryhog`.
  - The issue with AVX instructions was identified, and the user was informed about the incompatibility.

## General Learnings
- Rebooting can resolve issues related to loop devices in Singularity.
- Ensure compatibility of Singularity images with the target system's CPU architecture, especially regarding advanced instruction sets like AVX.
- Temporary directories (`/tmp`, `/scratch`, `/var/tmp`, `/dev/shm`) should be checked for proper mounting and permissions.
- Kernel logs can provide valuable insights into compatibility issues.

## Conclusion
The issues with Singularity on the Meggie frontend and Memoryhog were resolved through rebooting and identifying CPU instruction set incompatibilities. Regular system maintenance and compatibility checks are essential for smooth operation.
---

### 2024060342002153_Tier%203%20access%20for%20a%20master%20thesis%20student%20-%20iwic%20_%20CS12%20-%20LS%20Teich.md
# Ticket 2024060342002153

 # HPC Support Ticket: Tier 3 Access for a Master Thesis Student

## Keywords
- Tier 3 access
- Master thesis student
- HPC portal
- Principal Investigator (PI)
- Account creation
- Invitation
- Affiliation change
- Reactivation

## Summary
A PhD student with existing Tier 3 access inquired about obtaining similar access for a master thesis student they plan to supervise. The process involves the Principal Investigator (PI) sending an invitation via the HPC portal.

## Problem
- The user needed to know how a master thesis student could obtain Tier 3 access for their thesis work.
- The user's previous HPC account needed reactivation due to a change in affiliation.

## Solution
- **Account Creation for Master Thesis Student:**
  - The PI of the user's account (Prof. Teich) needs to log in to the HPC portal and send an invitation to the master thesis student.
  - The student will receive an email invitation and must accept it. The account will be set up the next day.
  - Detailed instructions are available at: [HPC Portal Management Tab](https://doc.nhr.fau.de/hpc-portal/#the-management-tab-visible-only-for-pis-and-technical-contacts) and [HPC Portal User Tab](https://doc.nhr.fau.de/hpc-portal/#the-user-tab).

- **Account Reactivation:**
  - The user's previous HPC account (iwal114h) was not visible on the HPC systems until the user logged into the HPC portal.
  - The account was manually migrated and is now active.
  - For any issues related to the user's Fraunhofer work, the user should contact the responsible PI (Stefan Turowski).

## Additional Information
- The HPC portal is used for managing HPC accounts and projects.
- Different project categories are available for various types of users (e.g., employees, students, project partners).
- The PI is responsible for managing the accounts and projects within their group.
- The HPC portal offers several advantages, such as electronic invitations, self-service account management, and usage statistics.

## Follow-up
- The user was advised to contact the correct email address (support-hpc@fau.de) for future support requests.
- The user was informed about the need to log in to the HPC portal to complete the account migration process.

## References
- [HPC Portal Management Tab](https://doc.nhr.fau.de/hpc-portal/#the-management-tab-visible-only-for-pis-and-technical-contacts)
- [HPC Portal User Tab](https://doc.nhr.fau.de/hpc-portal/#the-user-tab)
- [HPC Portal](https://portal.hpc.fau.de)
- [NHR Application](https://doc.nhr.fau.de/nhr-application/)
- [ClusterCockpit](https://doc.nhr.fau.de/job-monitoring-with-clustercockpit/)
- [SSH Command Line](https://doc.nhr.fau.de/access/ssh-command-line/)

### 2024042842000157_Lecture%20accounts%20for%20HESP.md
# Ticket 2024042842000157

 # HPC Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

## Keywords
- HPC accounts
- Students
- High-End Simulation in Practice (HESP)
- TinyGPU
- Alex
- HPC-Portal
- Access issues
- Project management

## Summary
- **Request**: HPC accounts for students participating in HESP with access to TinyGPU and Alex, valid until the end of the semester.
- **Issue**: Access to Alex not initially granted.
- **Solution**: HPC Admin granted access to Alex for the specified accounts.

## Detailed Analysis
- **Initial Request**:
  - User requested HPC accounts for students in the HESP course with access to TinyGPU and Alex.
  - No special software installation requirements.

- **HPC Admin Response**:
  - Project `hesp100` created in the HPC-Portal.
  - User designated as the project manager.

- **Follow-up Issue**:
  - User reported that access to Alex was not enabled.

- **Resolution**:
  - HPC Admin enabled access to Alex for the specified accounts (`hesp100h-hesp127h`).

## Lessons Learned
- **Project Setup**: Ensure all requested resources (e.g., TinyGPU, Alex) are correctly configured during project setup.
- **Access Management**: Verify access permissions for all required resources after project creation.
- **Communication**: Clear communication between users and HPC Admins is crucial for resolving access issues promptly.

## Root Cause of the Problem
- Initial project setup did not include access to Alex, leading to user-reported issues.

## Solution
- HPC Admin manually granted access to Alex for the specified accounts, resolving the issue.

## Documentation for Future Reference
- **Project Creation**: Ensure all required resources are included in the initial setup.
- **Access Verification**: After project creation, verify that all resources are accessible.
- **Communication**: Maintain open communication channels for prompt issue resolution.

This documentation can be used to address similar issues in the future, ensuring smoother project setup and resource allocation.

### 42326407__usr_include_stdc-predef.h.md
# Ticket 42326407

 # HPC Support Ticket: /usr/include/stdc-predef.h

## Keywords
- FASTEST
- Emmy Cluster
- Compilation Error
- stdc-predef.h
- make clean
- INTEL64
- Buildsystem

## Problem Description
The user is attempting to compile the FASTEST code from the Chair of Fluid Mechanics on the Emmy Cluster. The compilation works on their local machine but fails on the cluster with the following error:
```
make: *** No rule to make target `/usr/include/stdc-predef.h', needed by `sys/intel64/dep/wavelet.cd'.  Stop.
```
The user has searched for the header file and could not find it.

## Root Cause
The issue is likely related to the FASTEST build system, as the error persists even after running `make clean`.

## Troubleshooting Steps
1. **Module and FHPSYSTEM Verification**: HPC Admins inquired about the loaded modules and the FHPSYSTEM in use.
2. **make clean**: The user confirmed that they had run `make clean`, but the error persisted.
3. **Manual Deletion of Dependency Files**: HPC Admins suggested manually deleting all files in `sys/intel64/dep/` to force the build system to regenerate them correctly.

## Solution
Manually delete all files in the `sys/intel64/dep/` directory and then run `make` again. This should regenerate the dependency files correctly and resolve the compilation error.

## General Learnings
- Always ensure that the build system is clean before attempting a new compilation.
- Manual intervention may be required to reset the build system if `make clean` does not resolve dependency issues.
- Verify the loaded modules and FHPSYSTEM when troubleshooting compilation errors.

## Next Steps
If the issue persists after manually deleting the dependency files, further investigation into the build system configuration may be necessary.

---

This documentation can be used to troubleshoot similar compilation errors in the future.
---

### 2021022242002371_Enable%20resolution%20of%20OS%20kernel%20and%20kernel%20module%20symbols%20for%20VTune.md
# Ticket 2021022242002371

 # HPC Support Ticket: Enable Resolution of OS Kernel and Kernel Module Symbols for VTune

## Keywords
- VTune Profiler
- OS kernel symbols
- kernel module symbols
- /proc/kallsyms
- /proc/sys/kernel/kptr_restrict
- HPC performance analysis

## Problem
- User encountered a warning while using Intel oneAPI VTune Profiler for advanced analysis like "hpc-performance".
- Warning message: `vtune: Warning: Access to /proc/kallsyms file is limited. Consider changing /proc/sys/kernel/kptr_restrict to 0 to enable resolution of OS kernel and kernel module symbols.`

## Root Cause
- The warning indicates that the VTune Profiler lacks the necessary permissions to access kernel symbols, which are required for detailed performance analysis.

## Solution
- The warning suggests changing the `/proc/sys/kernel/kptr_restrict` setting to `0` to enable resolution of OS kernel and kernel module symbols.
- However, the HPC Admin pointed out that this setting is typically needed for debugging or profiling the operating system kernel, which is not the user's intention.
- The actual problem likely lies elsewhere and is not related to the warning message.

## General Learning
- Understanding the purpose of `/proc/sys/kernel/kptr_restrict` and its impact on profiling tools.
- Distinguishing between simple and advanced performance analysis in VTune Profiler.
- Recognizing that warnings about kernel symbol resolution may not be relevant to the user's specific profiling needs.

## Next Steps
- If the warning persists and affects the analysis, further investigation into the actual cause of the performance issue is recommended.
- Consult with the HPC Support team for additional guidance on advanced profiling techniques.
---

### 2024071642002823_Reboot%20without%20Downfall%20patch%20in%20testcluster.md
# Ticket 2024071642002823

 # HPC Support Ticket: Reboot without Downfall Patch in Testcluster

## Keywords
- Reboot
- Downfall patch
- Testcluster
- casclakesp2
- skylakesp2
- icx36
- MD-Bench
- verletlist
- BIOS changes
- kernel-parameter
- gather_data_sampling
- Microcode-Mitigation

## Summary
A request was made to reboot specific systems without the Downfall patch for benchmarking purposes. The systems involved were casclakesp2, skylakesp2, and icx36. The user needed these systems to run MD-Bench with verletlist.

## Problem
The user required the systems to be rebooted without the Downfall patch for specific benchmarking tasks.

## Solution
1. **Initial Request**: The user requested the reboot of the specified systems without the Downfall patch.
2. **Admin Response**: The request was initially deferred due to BIOS changes.
3. **Kernel Parameter Adjustment**: The systems were booted with the kernel parameter `gather_data_sampling=off` and reserved for the user.
4. **Verification**: The admin verified the vulnerability status by checking `/sys/devices/system/cpu/vulnerabilities/gather_data_sampling`.
5. **Completion**: The user confirmed successful tests and informed the admins.
6. **Revert Changes**: The kernel boot parameters were reverted, the systems were rebooted, and the nodes were released for general use.

## Lessons Learned
- **Communication**: Clear communication between users and admins is crucial for timely completion of tasks.
- **Kernel Parameters**: Adjusting kernel parameters can be necessary for specific benchmarking tasks.
- **Verification**: Always verify the changes made to ensure they meet the user's requirements.
- **Revert Changes**: After completing specific tasks, revert any temporary changes to maintain system stability.

## Follow-Up
Ensure that any future requests for similar tasks are handled promptly and that users are informed about the necessity of reverting changes after completion.
---

### 2024071042001228_Nodelimit%20aufheben%20f%C3%83%C2%BCr%2070B%20LLM.md
# Ticket 2024071042001228

 # HPC Support Ticket: Increase Node Limit for 70B LLM Training

## Keywords
- Node limit
- GPU memory limit (OOM)
- A40 GPUs
- sacctmgr
- QoS configuration

## Problem
- User's 70B training jobs were running into GPU memory limits (OOM).
- User requested an increase in the node limit to utilize more available A40 GPUs.

## Solution
1. **Temporary Increase in Node Limit:**
   - HPC Admin temporarily increased the node limit to 16 for the user.
   - Command used: `sacctmgr update qos where qos=a40multi set MaxTRES=node=16`

2. **Revert to Default Configuration:**
   - After the user confirmed completion of tests, HPC Admin reverted the configuration to the default node limit.
   - Command used: `sacctmgr update qos where qos=a40multi set MaxTRES=node=8`

## General Learnings
- **Flexibility in Resource Allocation:** HPC Admins can temporarily adjust resource limits to accommodate specific user needs.
- **Communication:** Users should inform HPC Admins when temporary adjustments are no longer needed to maintain system stability.
- **Command Usage:** `sacctmgr` is used to update Quality of Service (QoS) settings, including node limits.

## Root Cause
- The user's training jobs required more GPU memory than was available with the current node limit.

## Resolution
- Temporarily increasing the node limit allowed the user to utilize more GPUs and complete their training jobs without running into memory limits.
---

### 2024121042003038_Software%20installation%20on%20Alex%20-%20SISSO%2B%2B.md
# Ticket 2024121042003038

 ```markdown
# HPC-Support Ticket Conversation: SISSO++ Installation Issues

## Subject
Software installation on Alex - SISSO++

## Keywords
- SISSO++
- Python bindings
- MPI
- JupyterHub
- Interactive job
- Module loading

## Problem Description
User encountered issues while trying to import the SISSO++ package using Python bindings on the Alex HPC system. The errors included:
- Memory allocation error related to `ulimit -l` on the frontend node.
- `ImportError: libmpicxx.so.12: cannot open shared object file` when using JupyterHub.

## Root Cause
- Attempting to import the module on a frontend node where MPI is not supported.
- Missing MPI module loading in JupyterHub.

## Solution
- Run the import in an interactive job using `salloc` where MPI is supported.
- Load the MPI module before trying to import SISSO++.
- Use advanced options in JupyterHub to load necessary modules or start an interactive job with the required modules.

## Conversation Summary
1. **User**: Described the issue with importing SISSO++ in Python on the frontend node and JupyterHub.
2. **HPC Admin**: Suggested running the import in an interactive job using `salloc` where MPI is supported.
3. **User**: Asked about running JupyterHub as an interactive job.
4. **HPC Admin**: Clarified that interactive jobs using `salloc` should work and suggested loading the MPI module.
5. **User**: Reported issues with loading modules in JupyterHub.
6. **HPC Admin**: Provided instructions to set modules in advanced options or use a bash kernel in JupyterHub.

## General Learnings
- Importing MPI-dependent modules should be done in an environment where MPI is supported.
- JupyterHub can be configured to load necessary modules through advanced options or by using a bash kernel.
- Interactive jobs using `salloc` are suitable for testing MPI-dependent software.
```
---

### 2023071842001261_HPC%20for%20guests.md
# Ticket 2023071842001261

 # HPC Support Ticket: HPC for Guests

## Keywords
- Guest access
- HPC clusters
- IdM accounts
- Industry partners
- Compute time estimation

## Summary
A request was made to grant guest access to HPC clusters for project partners from the German industry. The partners need to test in-house developed simulation software on the parallel clusters Fritz and Meggie.

## Problem
- Request for guest access to HPC clusters for industry partners.
- Need for four guest accounts with access to eight nodes each.
- Project duration until May 31, 2024.

## Solution
1. **IdM Accounts**: Industry partners need to apply for IdM accounts as guest researchers.
2. **Verification**: The appointed IdM contact person from WTM will verify the identity and sign the IdM form.
3. **Compute Time Estimation**: A rough estimate of 32,000 node·hours was provided for the project duration.
4. **HPC Accounts**: Once IdM accounts are obtained, treat the partners like other WTM staff with regard to HPC accounts.

## Additional Information
- Partners are from Siemens AG and MTU Aero Engines AG.
- HPC resources will be used for occasional testing rather than large-scale simulations.
- The total compute time required is approximately 32,000 node·hours, with each account expected to run an average of 10 jobs, each requiring 800 node·hours.

## Conclusion
The process for granting guest access to industry partners involves applying for IdM accounts and then treating them like other WTM staff for HPC accounts. A rough estimate of compute time is necessary for planning resource allocation.
---

### 2021112242003335_Can%27t%20ssh%20into%20the%20Woody%20cluster%20from%20the%20dialog%20server.md
# Ticket 2021112242003335

 # HPC Support Ticket: SSH Access Issue to Woody Cluster

## Keywords
- SSH Access
- Woody Cluster
- Dialog Server
- Password Propagation
- FAU VPN

## Problem Description
- User unable to SSH into Woody cluster from the dialog server using the same password that worked for the dialog server.
- User followed the instructions provided in the getting started guide.

## Root Cause
- Password propagation delay across HPC systems.

## Solution
- Wait for a few hours for the password to propagate to all HPC systems, including Woody.
- If the issue persists, contact HPC support again.

## Additional Notes
- When using FAU VPN, direct connection to Woody is possible without going through the dialog server.
- Password updates may take longer for Woody than for the dialog server.

## Ticket Conversation Summary
- User reported SSH access issue to Woody cluster.
- HPC Admin advised waiting for password propagation and provided additional information on direct VPN access.

## Relevant Links
- [Getting Started with HPC](https://hpc.fau.de/systems-services/systems-documentation-instructions/getting-started/)
---

### 2021120742001006_Early-Alex%20%22Jacqueline%20Calderon%22%20_%20bccc018h.md
# Ticket 2021120742001006

 # HPC Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

## Keywords
- Early Access
- Alex Cluster
- Nvidia A40 GPUs
- Nvidia A100 GPUs
- Molecular Dynamics Simulations
- Gromacs
- Plumed
- Gnuplot
- HPC Usage Report
- System Stability
- Performance
- Feedback

## General Learnings
- **Early Access to New Clusters**: Users are granted early access to new clusters for testing and feedback.
- **Initial Documentation**: Provided to users to help them get started with the new cluster.
- **Monitoring Usage**: HPC admins monitor usage and reach out to users who haven't started using the system.
- **Feedback Collection**: Feedback on system stability, software, performance, and overall experience is collected at the end of the early access phase.
- **HPC Usage Report**: Users are required to submit a usage report following a specific structure.
- **Acknowledgement**: Users are provided with a acknowledgement template for publications.

## Issues and Solutions
- **Issue**: User hasn't started using the system.
  - **Solution**: HPC admin reaches out to offer assistance and gather feedback.
- **Issue**: Delay in submitting the HPC usage report.
  - **Solution**: HPC admin sends reminders and the user eventually submits the report after supervisor approval.
- **Issue**: User requires specific software (Gromacs, Plumed, Gnuplot).
  - **Solution**: HPC admin ensures the required software is provided and the user is satisfied with the software availability.

## Root Cause Analysis
- **User Delay in System Usage**: The user hadn't started using the system initially, possibly due to the need for guidance or transition challenges.
- **Delay in Report Submission**: The user needed supervisor approval before submitting the HPC usage report.

## Solutions Implemented
- **Proactive Communication**: HPC admins proactively reached out to offer assistance and gather feedback.
- **Reminders for Report Submission**: HPC admins sent reminders for the pending usage report.
- **Software Availability**: Ensured the required software was available and the user was satisfied with the provided software.

## Documentation for Future Reference
- **Early Access Procedure**: Document the process of granting early access, monitoring usage, and collecting feedback.
- **Usage Report Template**: Provide a clear template and guidelines for the HPC usage report.
- **Acknowledgement Template**: Provide a template for acknowledging HPC resources in publications.
- **Proactive Communication**: Establish a procedure for proactive communication with users to offer assistance and gather feedback.

This analysis can help HPC support employees understand the process of early access, monitor usage, collect feedback, and handle delays in system usage and report submission.
---

### 2018112342001056_Probleme%20mit%20Serveraccount.md
# Ticket 2018112342001056

 ```markdown
# HPC-Support Ticket: Probleme mit Serveraccount

## Problem Description
- User unable to run ROOT and other X11 applications (e.g., emacs) on `woodycap4` and other systems.
- Error messages include:
  ```
  [xcb] Unknown sequence number while appending request
  [xcb] Most likely this is a multi-threaded client and XInitThreads has not been called
  [xcb] Aborting, sorry about that.
  root.exe: ../../src/xcb_io.c:147: append_pending_request: Assertion `!xcb_xlib_unknown_seq_number' failed.
  ```
  ```
  double free or corruption (fasttop)
  ```
- Issue affects multiple systems (`woodycap3`, `woodycap4`, `cshpc`).

## Troubleshooting Steps
1. **Deleted `.Xauthority` and re-logged in**: No effect.
2. **Used `ssh -Y` for SSH client**: No effect.
3. **Tested on different computers**: Issue persists, indicating it is not client-specific.
4. **Tested with different user accounts**: Issue only affects the user's account, not others.
5. **Tested with `emacs -nw`**: Works, but `emacs` without `-nw` does not.
6. **Tested `display` command**: Fails with authorization error.
7. **Removed `.cshrc`**: No effect.
8. **Used `strace` to debug `emacs`**: Shows repeated `recvmsg` and `poll` calls, indicating a potential hang.

## Potential Causes
- **X11 Configuration Issue**: Possible misconfiguration in the user's X11 setup.
- **Permission Issue**: User may lack permissions to access certain files or libraries required by X11 applications.
- **Recent System Updates**: Changes in `ssh` or X server configurations may have introduced the issue.

## Solution
- **Additional Configuration in ROOT Login Script**: The issue was resolved by adding specific configurations to the ROOT login script.
- **Check for System Updates**: Investigate recent updates to `ssh` or X server on `woodycap3/4` that might have caused the issue.

## Keywords
- X11, ROOT, emacs, .Xauthority, ssh -Y, strace, display, authorization error, system updates

## General Learnings
- **X11 Troubleshooting**: Common steps include deleting `.Xauthority`, using `ssh -Y`, and checking permissions.
- **System Updates**: Recent updates can introduce unexpected issues, especially with X11 applications.
- **Debugging Tools**: `strace` can be useful for identifying where an application is hanging.
- **User-Specific Issues**: Problems that affect only one user may be due to account-specific configurations or permissions.
```
---

### 2022081142001657_problem%20running%20on%20woody.md
# Ticket 2022081142001657

 # HPC Support Ticket: Problem Running on Woody

## Keywords
- LAMMPS simulation
- CUDA driver error 700
- Woody cluster
- TinyGPU cluster
- HPC output file
- Error message
- Job script
- `in.rhodo`

## Summary
A user encountered an issue while running a LAMMPS simulation on the Woody cluster. The job would start but stop at a seemingly random point, with an error message appearing only in the HPC output file.

## Root Cause
- The error message `Cuda driver error 700 in call at file 'geryon/nvd_timer.h' in line 76` indicated a problem with the CUDA driver.
- The issue was not with the Woody cluster but with the TinyGPU cluster.

## Solution
- The HPC Admin suggested checking with `in.rhodo` as mentioned in a [matsci forum post](https://matsci.org/t/problem-with-cuda/30728/2).

## General Learnings
- Always check the error messages in the HPC output file.
- Ensure that the correct cluster is being referred to in the support ticket.
- For CUDA-related errors in LAMMPS, consult relevant forums and documentation.

## Follow-up Actions
- The user should provide feedback on whether the suggested solution resolved the issue.
- If the problem persists, further investigation into the CUDA driver and GPU setup on TinyGPU may be necessary.
---

### 2016051542000177_woodycap%20extrem%20langsam.md
# Ticket 2016051542000177

 # HPC Support Ticket: Slow Performance on woodycap1 and woodycap2

## Keywords
- Slow performance
- woodycap1
- woodycap2
- Program editing issues
- foxtrott.gate
- Packet loss
- Delay

## Problem Description
- **User Report:** woodycap1 and woodycap2 were extremely slow starting from the previous morning, causing issues with editing programs.
- **Root Cause:** The foxtrott.gate was causing delays and significant packet loss between some of its ports.

## Solution
- **Action Taken:** The issue was identified and resolved by the HPC Admin.
- **Details:** The foxtrott.gate was forwarding packets with a 1-second delay and significant packet loss, affecting performance on woodycap1 and woodycap2.

## Lessons Learned
- Regular monitoring of network components like foxtrott.gate is crucial to prevent performance degradation.
- Quick identification and resolution of network issues can prevent disruptions in user activities such as program editing.

## Follow-up Actions
- Ensure continuous monitoring of network devices to detect and address similar issues promptly.
- Document the incident for future reference to expedite troubleshooting in case of recurrence.
---

### 2018032742002474_Re%3A%20bad%20performance%20of%20some%20jobs%20on%20Emmy%20_%20bctc46.md
# Ticket 2018032742002474

 # HPC Support Ticket Analysis: Bad Performance of Jobs

## Keywords
- Performance issues
- LAMMPS
- Node allocation
- Job throttling
- HPC resource management

## Summary
A user experienced severe performance issues with some jobs on the HPC system. The main problem was that only a few nodes were executing significant amounts of floating point operations, which is not efficient for the main work of LAMMPS.

## Root Cause
- **Inappropriate Node Allocation**: The main reason for the performance issues was that 16 nodes were not appropriate for the input data.

## Actions Taken
- **Job Termination**: The user immediately killed the problematic jobs upon receiving the initial notification.
- **Parameter Adjustment**: The user attempted to change some parameters and tested the runs on a local computer, which seemed fine.
- **Communication**: The user did not initially respond to the email, leading to a misunderstanding and potential throttling of usage.

## Lessons Learned
- **Efficient Resource Allocation**: Ensure that the number of nodes allocated is appropriate for the input data to avoid performance issues.
- **Communication**: Always respond to HPC support emails to avoid misunderstandings and potential throttling of usage.
- **Testing**: Test parameter changes on a local computer before running them on the HPC system to ensure they do not cause performance issues.

## Solution
- **Adjust Node Allocation**: Ensure that the number of nodes allocated is appropriate for the input data.
- **Monitor Performance**: Regularly monitor job performance to identify and address any issues early.
- **Communicate with Support**: Maintain open communication with HPC support to address any performance issues promptly.

## Follow-up
- The user's recent jobs showed improved performance, indicating that the issue was likely resolved by adjusting the node allocation.
- No further feedback was received from the user after more than a week, but the last 10 jobs looked better.

## Conclusion
Proper resource allocation and open communication with HPC support are crucial for efficient use of HPC systems. Regularly monitoring job performance and adjusting parameters as needed can help prevent performance issues.
---

 ```markdown
- Ubuntu 20.04

## Summary
- User requested installation of Schrödinger Suite on HPC system "alex".
- Issues with installation due to unsupported operating system (AlmaLinux).
- HPC Admin provided steps to run installation check and gather error logs.
- Schrödinger support confirmed AlmaLinux is not officially supported.
- Suggested using Singularity container with a supported OS or using TinyGPU with Ubuntu 20.04.

## Detailed Conversation

### User Request
- User requested the Schrödinger account to be specified for downloading the advanced suite.
- Schrödinger support mentioned the need for a signed document and provided instructions for downloading the installer.

### Installation Issues
- HPC Admin attempted installation but encountered issues with password-free connections between login and compute nodes.
- Installation check on Alex produced non-interpretable error messages.
- User was instructed to run `installation_check` and gather error logs for Schrödinger support.

### Schrödinger Support Response
- Schrödinger support confirmed that AlmaLinux is not an officially supported platform.
- Provided link to supported platforms: [Schrödinger Supported Platforms](https://www.schrodinger.com/supportedplatforms)

### HPC Admin Suggestions
- HPC Admin suggested building a Singularity container with a supported OS and integrating the Schrödinger Suite.
- Alternatively, suggested using TinyGPU with Ubuntu 20.04, which is officially supported by Schrödinger.

## Root Cause
- The root cause of the installation issues was the unsupported operating system (AlmaLinux) on the HPC system "alex".

## Solution
- Build a Singularity container with a supported OS and integrate the Schrödinger Suite.
- Use TinyGPU with Ubuntu 20.04, which is officially supported by Schrödinger.

## Additional Information
- HPC Admin provided GPU usage statistics for project planning.
- User was advised to submit a project application for additional compute resources.

## Conclusion
- The issue was resolved by identifying the unsupported OS and providing alternative solutions using Singularity containers or a different HPC system with a supported OS.
```
---

### 2024020942002962_%22This%20account%20is%20currently%20not%20available%22.md
# Ticket 2024020942002962

 # HPC Support Ticket: "This account is currently not available"

## Keywords
- Account activation
- SSH configuration
- Cron job
- HPC portal
- User invitation

## Problem Description
A recently added user to the HPC portal is able to connect to the dialog server but is unable to connect to the clusters `fritz` and `alex`. The error message received is:
```
This account is currently not available.
Connection to [cluster] closed.
```

## Root Cause
The user's account was not yet activated on the clusters. Account activations on the clusters occur only once daily through a morning cron job.

## Solution
Wait until the next morning for the cron job to activate the account on the clusters.

## What Can Be Learned
- New user accounts are not immediately activated on the clusters.
- Account activations on the clusters occur once daily through a morning cron job.
- Users should be informed about the activation delay when they accept the invitation.
- The SSH configuration provided by the user was correct and not the cause of the issue.
---

### 42305406_jobs%20auf%20woody.md
# Ticket 42305406

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: Jobs auf Woody

## Keywords
- Woody Cluster
- Job Queue
- Multi-Node Jobs
- Cluster Deprecation
- Account iwpa37

## Summary
A user reported that their jobs on the Woody cluster have been stuck in the queue for a week without any indication of starting. The user inquired about potential issues with the cluster and considered switching to another cluster.

## Root Cause
- Multi-node jobs on the Woody cluster were no longer functioning due to the decommissioning of older hardware components.
- The Woody cluster's older nodes, which were used for multi-node jobs, had been shut down, making it impossible to run jobs requiring more than one node.

## Solution
- The user was advised to switch to another cluster, such as Tinyblue, for their computational needs.
- The user deleted their jobs from the Woody cluster queue and moved to Tinyblue.

## Lessons Learned
- Regularly update users about the decommissioning of older hardware to avoid confusion and delays in job processing.
- Ensure that users are aware of alternative clusters and their capabilities for handling different types of jobs.
- Monitor job queues and provide timely support to users experiencing issues with job processing.
```
---

### 2022070842003341_Problem%20with%20Ovito%20on%20fritz.md
# Ticket 2022070842003341

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: Problem with Ovito on Fritz

## Summary
User encountered issues running Ovito using `srun` on Fritz, despite it working in the terminal. The error involved missing GUI libraries on compute nodes.

## Keywords
- Ovito
- SLURM
- srun
- GUI libraries
- libfontconfig.so.1
- Qt platform plugin

## Problem
- **Root Cause**: Ovito requires GUI libraries that are not installed on the compute nodes.
- **Error Message**:
  ```
  Cannot load library /home/woody/iww8/iww8000h/apps/ovito/lib/ovito/plugins_qt/platforms/libqminimal.so: (libfontconfig.so.1: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory)
  qt.qpa.plugin: Could not load the Qt platform plugin "minimal" in "" even though it was found.
  ```

## Solution
- **HPC Admin Response**: Ovito expects many GUI libraries which are not installed on the compute nodes. There is no guarantee that this can be fixed on Fritz.
- **User Action**: Provided test files and links to the preinstalled Ovito version.
- **Further Investigation**: HPC Admin suggested checking the required libraries for non-interactive batch mode.
- **Final Resolution**: User managed to get Ovito/Python running. HPC Admin provided a link to an Ovito forum thread on parallel execution.

## Lessons Learned
- Ensure that all required libraries for GUI applications are available on compute nodes when running in batch mode.
- Ovito can be configured to use multiple threads for parallel execution on a single compute node.
- Always check for missing dependencies when running GUI applications in a non-interactive environment.

## References
- [Ovito Forum Thread on Parallel Execution](https://www.ovito.org/forum/topic/how-do-ovito-computations-run-in-parallel-on-a-linux-system/)
```
---

### 2023061342001646_request%20-%20fuzzy%20matching%20-%20Binzel.md

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket Conversation Summary

## Subject: Request - Fuzzy Matching

### Keywords:
- Fuzzy matching
- Python
- High memory consumption
- Sensitive data
- HPC resources
- Batch mode
- Linux systems
- Registration process
- Zoom meeting

### General Learnings:
- FAU's HPC services provide compute resources in batch mode on Linux systems, not virtual machines.
- Monthly introductory sessions are available for new/prospective users.
- Registration in the HPC portal is required to access resources.
- Project managers can invite collaborators to access HPC systems.
- Technical requirements and potential solutions should be discussed in Zoom meetings for better communication.

### Root Cause of the Problem:
- High memory consumption involved in the fuzzy matching exercise.
- Sensitivity of the data prevents the use of external services like Amazon Web Services.

### Solution:
- Use FAU's HPC resources, specifically Woody or TinyFat nodes, which offer up to 2 TB of RAM.
- Register in the HPC portal to create a project and invite collaborators.
- Attend introductory sessions and Zoom meetings for guidance on the registration process and technical requirements.

### Detailed Steps:
1. **Initial Contact:**
   - User inquires about using FAU's HPC system for fuzzy matching of large microdata sets.
   - HPC Admin informs that HPC services provide compute resources in batch mode on Linux systems.

2. **Introductory Session:**
   - User attends the monthly introductory session via Zoom.
   - HPC Admin confirms that the session is open to everyone, regardless of FAU login credentials.

3. **Technical Discussion:**
   - HPC Admin suggests running the fuzzy matching on Woody or TinyFat nodes.
   - User needs to register in the HPC portal to create a project and invite collaborators.

4. **Registration Process:**
   - HPC Admin guides the user through the registration process in the HPC portal.
   - User is informed about the project creation and invitation of collaborators.

5. **Zoom Meeting:**
   - HPC Admin schedules a Zoom meeting to discuss technical requirements and potential solutions.
   - User is guided through the process of setting up a Jupyter Notebook for successful data processing.

### Conclusion:
- The user successfully registered in the HPC portal and created a project.
- The user was guided through the process of setting up a Jupyter Notebook for data processing.
- The user is now ready to utilize FAU's HPC resources for their research project.
```

### 2019112642002922_Node%20Property%20SL.md
# Ticket 2019112642002922

 # HPC Support Ticket Conversation: Node Property SL

## Keywords
- Node parameter
- Skylake nodes
- Job assignment
- Logfiles
- Woody cluster

## Problem
- User ran computations on the HPC cluster (Woody) using the old node parameter 'sl', which is no longer supported.
- User needs to know which nodes the jobs were assigned to and if they can be sure about the matching.

## Root Cause
- The old node parameter 'sl' is no longer supported, leading to uncertainty about job assignment.

## Solution
- Use the new parameter `:sl32g` to get the same Skylake nodes as before.
- Jobs with the property `:sl` were always assigned to Skylake nodes, either with 16GB or 32GB.
- Refer to the website for all other available node properties: [Woody Cluster Documentation](https://www.anleitungen.rrze.fau.de/hpc/woody-cluster/)

## General Learnings
- Always check for updates on node parameters and properties.
- Logfiles can provide insights into which nodes were used for computations.
- The HPC support team can provide quick and accurate information regarding node assignments and parameters.

## Roles Involved
- **HPC Admins**: Provided the solution and clarification on node assignments.
- **User**: Reported the issue and sought clarification on job assignments.

## Additional Notes
- The user was concerned about the validity of previous runs due to the use of the old parameter.
- The HPC Admins assured that the old parameter `:sl` was always assigned to Skylake nodes, ensuring the validity of the runs.

### 2020102142003224_Summary%20on%20different%20nodes%20in%20HPC%20server.md
# Ticket 2020102142003224

 # HPC Support Ticket Summary: Scaling Study on Different Nodes

## Keywords
- Scaling study
- HPC nodes
- Job performance
- Steps per second
- Resource optimization

## Problem Description
- User conducted a scaling study using different numbers of nodes on the HPC server.
- The user observed that increasing the number of nodes reduced computation time but was unsure if this was logical given each node has 40 processors.
- The user provided a summary table of runs but the HPC Admins had difficulty understanding the data.

## Root Cause
- The user's table and data interpretation were unclear, leading to confusion among HPC Admins.
- The relevant metrics for analysis should have been "any MFlop/s" and "memory bandwidth."

## Findings
- HPC Admins identified that the steps per second did not significantly increase with more nodes: 18608, 20134, 20149, 20186, 20045.
- It was concluded that using more than 4 nodes might be a waste of resources.

## Solution
- HPC Admins suggested focusing on "any MFlop/s" and "memory bandwidth" for future scaling studies.
- The ticket was closed due to a lack of helpful feedback from the user.

## Lessons Learned
- Clear communication and data presentation are crucial for effective support.
- Understanding the relevant performance metrics is essential for optimizing resource usage.
- Scaling studies should consider both computational efficiency and resource allocation.

## References
- Related Ticket: #2020070142002939
- Job Links:
  - [Job 1354268](https://www.hpc.rrze.fau.de/HPC-Status/job-info.php?USER=iwst053h&JOBID=1354268&ACCESSKEY=8109b6c0&SYSTEM=EMMY)
  - [Job 1354267](https://www.hpc.rrze.fau.de/HPC-Status/job-info.php?USER=iwst053h&JOBID=1354267&ACCESSKEY=d56a2602&SYSTEM=EMMY)

  - [Job 1354265](https://www.hpc.rrze.fau.de/HPC-Status/job-info.php?USER=iwst053h&JOBID=1354265&ACCESSKEY=fdca4b3d&SYSTEM=EMMY)
  - [Job 1354264](https://www.hpc.rrze.fau.de/HPC-Status/job-info.php?USER=iwst053h&JOBID=1354264&ACCESSKEY=4016c626&SYSTEM=EMMY)

## Conclusion

### 2022082542003174_Fehlermeldungen%20auf%20woody-ng.md
# Ticket 2022082542003174

 # HPC Support Ticket: Fehlermeldungen auf woody-ng

## Problem Description
- User encounters errors when running a Python script that calls the SCIP solver on the HPC cluster.
- SCIP binary was compiled on the cluster but fails with "Illegal instruction (core dumped)" when run on interactive nodes or as a job.
- Error message indicates a problem with `libstdc++.so.6`.

## Root Cause
- The issue is due to missing AVX512 instructions on older CPUs.
- The binary was compiled with AVX512 instructions, which are not supported on all nodes.

## Solution
- Compile the code on a node that does not support AVX512 (w1xxx nodes) to ensure compatibility across all nodes.
- Alternatively, use the `#SBATCH --constraint="icx"` flag in SLURM scripts to request nodes that support AVX512 (w2xxx nodes).

## Additional Notes
- The user's jobs were using `#SBATCH --ntasks=4` but only required one core and less than 7.75 GB of memory.
- It is recommended to adjust the job scripts to `#SBATCH --ntasks=1` to reduce resource usage.

## Keywords
- SCIP solver
- AVX512
- Illegal instruction
- libstdc++.so.6
- SLURM
- Interactive nodes
- Compilation issues
- Resource allocation

## Lessons Learned
- Ensure that binaries are compiled with instructions supported by all target nodes.
- Use SLURM constraints to request specific node types if necessary.
- Optimize resource allocation in job scripts to avoid unnecessary blocking of resources.
---

### 2024022242001107_b117ba%20Probleme%20-%20Verbrauchsanzeige%20im%20HPC-Portal%20f%C3%83%C2%BCr%20Projektversorgung%20H.md
# Ticket 2024022242001107

 # HPC Support Ticket: b117ba Probleme - Verbrauchsanzeige im HPC-Portal für Projektversorgung HSC

## Keywords
- Alex Cluster
- Job Allocation
- Timeout
- Nutzungsdaten
- Projekt b117ba
- Projekt b116ba
- HPC4AAI
- Scheduling-Algorithmus
- Downtime
- Bug im Portal

## Problem
- **Job Allocation Timeout**: User reports long wait times and eventual timeout when trying to allocate jobs on the Alex cluster.
- **Nutzungsdaten Anzeige**: User cannot view usage data for project b117ba, despite knowing that data exists.

## Root Cause
- **Job Allocation Timeout**: Recent date system failures led to changes in the scheduling algorithm, causing nodes to be allocated user-wise instead of being shared, resulting in longer wait times.
- **Nutzungsdaten Anzeige**: Technical reasons prevent the separation of usage data for b117ba. The data is included in the overall usage of b116ba. A bug in the portal prevents account-wise display of usage data.

## Solution
- **Job Allocation Timeout**:
  - Short-term: Reserved some nodes in Alex for HSC to improve job allocation.
  - Long-term: Planned downtime and adjustments to the scheduling algorithm to improve overall cluster utilization.
- **Nutzungsdaten Anzeige**:
  - Short-term: Offered to provide usage data manually if needed.
  - Long-term: Planned to fix the bug in the portal once the developer returns from vacation. A recent update has been applied to address the issue.

## Additional Information
- The usage data for b117ba is included in the overall usage of b116ba.
- A recent update has been applied to fix the issue with the usage data display in the portal.

## Follow-up
- Users should report any further issues with job allocation or usage data display.
- The HPC Admin team will monitor the situation and make further adjustments as needed.
---

### 2020081342001326_likwid-perfctr%20geeignete%20performance%20group%20f%C3%83%C2%BCr%20Resourcenauslastung.md
# Ticket 2020081342001326

 # HPC-Support Ticket: Performance Group for Resource Utilization

## Keywords
- likwid-perfctr
- Performance groups
- Resource utilization
- CPU, RAM, GPU
- FLOPS
- Intel Trace Collector
- VTune

## Problem
The user wants to monitor the resource utilization (CPU, RAM, GPU) of their simulations using likwid-perfctr. They are unsure which performance groups to use and have encountered availability issues with certain groups on different clusters.

## Root Cause
- Different clusters have varying hardware events available.
- The user is unsure how to measure CPU utilization effectively.

## Solution
- **Memory Utilization**: Use MEM, L2, and L3 groups. If MEM is not available, create a custom group using DRAM_READS and DRAM_WRITES events.
- **CPU Utilization**: Measure the work done by the CPU, such as FLOPS_DP/SP for floating-point operations. CPU utilization can be indirectly determined by comparing the measured FLOPS to the maximum FLOPS.
- **GPU Utilization**: Use FLOPS_DP/SP and DATA groups. Note that LIKWID currently supports up to Compute Capability 7.
- **Alternative Tools**: Intel Trace Collector for MPI process analysis, VTune for hardware performance counter analysis.

## Additional Notes
- The maximum FLOPS can be estimated from manufacturer data or measured using likwid-bench.
- The HPC group is developing a CPU database with realistic FLOPS rates.
- The user should choose performance metrics that relate to the work done by their specific code.

## References
- [likwid-perfctr Custom Performance Groups](https://github.com/RRZE-HPC/likwid/wiki/likwid-perfctr#defining-custom-performance-groups)
- [Intel Trace Collector/Analyzer](https://hpc-wiki.info/hpc/Intel_Trace_Collector/Analyzer)
- [Intel VTune](https://hpc-wiki.info/hpc/Intel_VTune)
- [Meggie Cluster Peak Performance](https://www.anleitungen.rrze.fau.de/hpc/meggie-cluster/)
---

### 2024041642001517_Monitoring%20and%20Utilization%20of%20A100%20GPUs.md
# Ticket 2024041642001517

 # HPC Support Ticket: Monitoring and Utilization of A100 GPUs

## Keywords
- A100 GPUs
- NHR monitoring tool
- acc_mem_used
- nv_mem_util
- VRAM utilization
- GPU memory interface utilization
- artifacts in model training

## Problem Description
- User allocated an A100 node with 8x80 GB VRAM GPUs.
- The "acc_mem_used" metric in the monitoring tool suggests 8x40GB = 320000 MB, which seems incorrect.
- The "acc_mem_used (MB)" metric suggests hitting the absolute limits of the GPU, while the "nv_mem_util" metric suggests only around ~40% of the GPU's memory is being used.
- The values for "acc_mem_used (Node)" and "Core Metrics Footprint" do not match.
- User is concerned about the likelihood of artifacts occurring during model training if VRAM is maximized.

## Root Cause
- The monitoring tool was missing the appropriate limit configuration for A100 GPUs with 80GB of memory.
- The "acc_mem_used" metric measures the amount of allocated GPU memory, while "nv_mem_util" measures the GPU memory interface utilization.

## Solution
- The limit configuration for A100 GPUs with 80GB of memory was updated to show the correct limit of 640GB.
- Clarified that "acc_mem_used" measures allocated GPU memory, and "nv_mem_util" measures GPU memory interface utilization.
- Reassured the user that there should not be any errors or changes in accuracy of computations solely because the VRAM is full.

## General Learnings
- Understanding the difference between "acc_mem_used" and "nv_mem_util" is crucial for interpreting GPU utilization metrics.
- Proper configuration of monitoring tools is essential for accurate reporting of resource utilization.
- Maximizing VRAM usage does not necessarily lead to artifacts in model training, but it is important to monitor for potential impacts on performance.

## Additional Notes
- The user was advised that reducing batch size might help rule out uncertainties, but given the insights and lack of direct signs of hardware-related numerical instabilities, it should be safe to proceed with the current setup.
---

### 2023062242001969_Best%20practice%20for%20large%20datasets%20-%20TFRecord%20-%20b143dc18_b180dc18_iwai008h.md
# Ticket 2023062242001969

 # HPC Support Ticket: Best Practice for Large Datasets

## Keywords
- Large datasets
- ImageNet
- Custom video dataset
- Data transfer
- Network usage
- Storage quota
- TFRecord
- HDF5
- rsync
- Lustre storage
- Flash storage
- SSD storage server

## Summary
A visiting researcher at FAU, working on the NHR project, needed guidance on the best approach to store and manage large datasets (ImageNet and a custom video dataset) on Alex, an HPC cluster, to minimize network usage and optimize training performance.

## Root Cause of the Problem
- The user was experiencing slow data transfer speeds when copying large datasets from FAU storage to Alex nodes.
- The group storage quota was quickly maxed out, and the user was not the only one using a lot of storage.
- Reading small files from the server caused high load on the fileserver.

## Solutions and Recommendations
- **Aggregate small files into larger aggregates (HDF5, TFRecord, etc.)**: It is suggested to have O(100-1000) aggregated files to optimize data transfer and reduce the load on the fileserver.
- **Store aggregated files on the group's local SSD storage server**: This server should be able to deliver data much faster than the current central HPC NFS servers.
- **Use multiple rsync streams to copy large files**: A single rsync will not saturate a modern Ethernet link. To keep a 10 or 25 Gbit Ethernet link busy, typically 5-10 concurrent rsyncs would be necessary.
  ```bash
  cd $TMPDIR
  ssh user@idea-anubis ls /data/imagenet-opt/ | xargs -n1 -P10 -I% rsync user@idea-anubis:/data/imagenet-opt/% .
  ```
- **Consider using Lustre storage or Flash storage**: Lustre storage might help, but it suffers from random small IO. Flash storage is being worked on and might be available soon.
- **Monitor and optimize data handling**: The HPC Admins can extend the runtime of jobs to further reduce fileserver load once the data handling is optimized.

## Additional Resources
- [CSCS Guide on TFRecord](https://user.cscs.ch/computing/data_science/pytorch/)
- [HPC File Systems Benchmarks](https://hpc.fau.de/files/2022/01/2022-01-11-hpc-cafe-file-systems.pdf)
- [Speed up rsync with simultaneous concurrent file transfers](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/24058544/speed-up-rsync-with-simultaneous-concurrent-file-transfers)

## Conclusion
The user was advised to aggregate small files into larger aggregates, store them on the group's local SSD storage server, and use multiple rsync streams to copy the data. The HPC Admins are working on an HPC SSD-storage solution, which might further improve data transfer speeds. The user was also advised to monitor and optimize data handling to reduce the load on the fileserver.
---

 ```markdown
## Summary
```
