### 2022121342002322_Prioritize%20Jobs%20on%20Tinygpu.md
# Ticket 2022121342002322

 # HPC Support Ticket: Prioritize Jobs on Tinygpu

## Keywords
- Job prioritization
- Deadline
- Group policies (QOSGrpGRES)
- Job extension
- Cluster resource allocation

## Problem
- User has a deadline and needs job evaluations completed.
- Many tasks are pending due to group policies (QOSGrpGRES).
- User requests prioritization of one or two jobs to meet the deadline.

## Conversation Summary
- User requests job prioritization due to an upcoming deadline.
- HPC Admin informs the user that 6 jobs are already running on 10x A100.
- User requests extension of job runtime on Alex cluster and prioritization of a pending job on Tinygpu.
- HPC Admin extends the runtime of 4 jobs on Alex cluster and advises moving pending jobs to Alex due to unavailability of A100 on Tinygpu.
- User requests further prioritization and runtime extension for a pending job on Alex.

## Solution
- HPC Admin extends the runtime of jobs on Alex cluster.
- User is advised to move pending jobs from Tinygpu to Alex due to resource unavailability.
- Further prioritization and runtime extension requests are considered by HPC Admin.

## Lessons Learned
- Users may request job prioritization and runtime extensions to meet deadlines.
- HPC Admins can extend job runtimes and advise on resource allocation based on cluster availability.
- Communication between users and HPC Admins is crucial for managing job priorities and resource allocation.

## Root Cause
- Group policies (QOSGrpGRES) causing job pending status.
- Limited availability of A100 resources on Tinygpu.

## Action Taken
- Job runtime extensions granted on Alex cluster.
- Advice given to move pending jobs to Alex due to resource unavailability on Tinygpu.
- Further prioritization and runtime extension requests considered.
---

### 2022090742003591_Unable%20to%20write_submit%20jobs%20on%20Alex%20or%20TinyGPU%20cluster.md
# Ticket 2022090742003591

 # HPC Support Ticket: Unable to Write/Submit Jobs on Alex or TinyGPU Cluster

## Keywords
- Job submission failure
- Storage quota issue
- $WORK, $HPCVAULT, $HOME
- shownicerquota.pl
- Slurm job errors
- Quota increase request

## Problem Description
- User unable to write files or submit jobs to the HPC system after scheduled downtime.
- User reported issues with accessing $WORK and $HPCVAULT storage systems.
- Jobs automatically cancelled within 5 seconds after assignment to GPU.
- Errors encountered when trying to write or save files on $WORK.

## Root Cause
- User ran out of quota on $WORK, leading to read-only access.
- shownicerquota.pl script broke during downtime, causing misleading information.

## Solution
- HPC Admin increased the user's $WORK quota to resolve the write permission issue.
- User was advised to move unnecessary data to a different location to manage quota effectively.
- HPC Admin increased the user's $WORK quota to 2TB to support research needs.
- User was informed about the archive service provided by CDI for finished research data.

## Additional Notes
- User requested an increase in $HPCVAULT quota from 500GB to 1TB, which was addressed by the HPC Admin.
- User was advised to acknowledge HPC scientific support in their research papers and provide a user report for the website.

## Follow-up Actions
- Monitor user's storage usage to ensure compliance with quota limits.
- Continue working on a fix for the shownicerquota.pl script.
- Encourage users to utilize the archive service for completed research data.

## References
- [HPC Usage Reports](https://hpc.fau.de/systems-services/hpc-usage-reports/)
- [CDI Archive Service](https://www.cdi.fau.de/)

This documentation aims to assist HPC support employees in resolving similar issues related to job submission failures and storage quota management.

### 2024011742000852_Resume%20training.md
# Ticket 2024011742000852

 ```markdown
# HPC-Support Ticket: Resume Training

## Problem Description
- User encounters CUDA out of memory error when resuming training on A100 80GB GPU.
- Initial training job runs without issues but subsequent job fails to allocate memory.
- Error message indicates more memory is being allocated than expected.

## Root Cause
- User's Slurm script lacks the `#SBATCH -C a100_80` directive to enforce the 80GB version of the GPU.
- Previous jobs were started on a100_40, leading to insufficient memory for resuming training.

## Solution
- Add `#SBATCH -C a100_80` to the Slurm script to ensure the job runs on the correct GPU version.
- HPC Admins can manually extend job runtime if requested, provided operational constraints allow it.

## Keywords
- CUDA out of memory
- Slurm job
- A100 80GB
- Resume training
- Memory allocation
- GPU memory
- Job runtime extension

## General Learnings
- Ensure correct GPU version is specified in Slurm scripts to avoid memory allocation issues.
- HPC Admins can assist with extending job runtime upon request.
- Properly configure Slurm scripts to match hardware requirements.
```
---

### 2023010342001585_Some%20issues%20accessing%20FRITZ.md
# Ticket 2023010342001585

 # HPC Support Ticket: Issues Accessing FRITZ

## Keywords
- Alias not working
- Module not found
- Command not found
- SSH warning
- System information

## Problem Description
- Aliases defined in `.bashrc` are not working.
- Unable to load modules (`inte`, `intelmpi`).
- Commands like `mpiifort`, `squeue`, `sinfo` not found.
- SSH warning about identity file not accessible.

## Root Cause
- Possible issues with the user's shell environment or configuration files.
- Module names might be incorrect or not installed.
- SSH configuration issue with the identity file.

## Troubleshooting Steps
1. **Aliases not working:**
   - Verify the syntax and location of alias definitions in `.bashrc`.
   - Ensure `.bashrc` is being sourced correctly upon login.

2. **Module not found:**
   - Check the correct module names using `module avail`.
   - Ensure the required modules are installed on the system.

3. **Command not found:**
   - Verify the installation and path of the required commands.
   - Check if the necessary software packages are loaded.

4. **SSH warning:**
   - Verify the path and permissions of the SSH identity file.
   - Ensure the SSH configuration is correct.

## Solution
- **Aliases:**
  ```bash
  source ~/.bashrc
  ```
- **Modules:**
  ```bash
  module avail | grep intel
  module load <correct_module_name>
  ```
- **Commands:**
  ```bash
  which mpiifort
  which squeue
  which sinfo
  ```
- **SSH:**
  ```bash
  ssh -i /path/to/correct/id_rsa user@hostname
  ```

## General Learning
- Always ensure configuration files like `.bashrc` are correctly sourced.
- Use `module avail` to check available modules.
- Verify the installation and path of required commands.
- Check SSH configuration and identity file path for correctness.

## Follow-up
- If the issue persists, escalate to HPC Admins for further investigation.
- Document any additional findings or solutions for future reference.
---

### 2021020142001974_Zugang%20zu%20AMD%20rome%20nodes%20%2B%20Biite%20qsimcirq%20updaten.md
# Ticket 2021020142001974

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket Conversation Summary

## Subject: Zugang zu AMD rome nodes + Biite qsimcirq updaten

### Keywords:
- qsimcirq update
- AMD rome nodes
- SLURM job submission
- pylikwid installation
- locale settings
- module load
- Python/Anaconda
- LIKWID

### General Learnings:
- **qsimcirq Update**: The user requested an update to qsimcirq version 0.7.1 to fix a sampling slowdown issue.
- **AMD Rome Nodes**: The user inquired about how to use the newly listed AMD rome nodes. The correct SLURM command was provided.
- **SLURM Job Submission**: The user encountered issues with job submission due to account/partition problems. The solution involved fixing account setup issues.
- **pylikwid Installation**: The user had trouble installing pylikwid due to LIKWID library not being found. The solution involved setting the LIKWID_PREFIX environment variable and installing from the GitHub repository.
- **Locale Settings**: The user encountered locale setting issues when logging into the nodes. The solution involved adjusting locale settings to avoid conflicts.
- **Module Load**: The user needed to load specific modules (Python/Anaconda, LIKWID) to run their jobs. The correct commands were provided.

### Root Cause and Solutions:
- **qsimcirq Update**:
  - **Root Cause**: Outdated qsimcirq version causing slowdown.
  - **Solution**: Update qsimcirq to version 0.7.1.

- **AMD Rome Nodes**:
  - **Root Cause**: Incorrect SLURM job submission commands.
  - **Solution**: Use the correct SLURM command to request nodes.

- **SLURM Job Submission**:
  - **Root Cause**: Invalid account or account/partition combination.
  - **Solution**: Fix account setup issues and use the correct SLURM command.

- **pylikwid Installation**:
  - **Root Cause**: LIKWID library not found during installation.
  - **Solution**: Set the LIKWID_PREFIX environment variable and install from the GitHub repository.

- **Locale Settings**:
  - **Root Cause**: Locale settings conflict when logging into nodes.
  - **Solution**: Adjust locale settings to avoid conflicts.

- **Module Load**:
  - **Root Cause**: Required modules not loaded.
  - **Solution**: Load the necessary modules using the correct commands.

### Example Commands:
- **SLURM Job Submission**:
  ```bash
  srun -N 1 --tasks-per-node=128 --time=01:00:00 -M tinyx --pty bash -l
  ```

- **pylikwid Installation**:
  ```bash
  module load python/3.8-anaconda
  module load likwid
  export LIKWID_PREFIX=$(realpath $(dirname $(which likwid-topology))/..)
  pip install --user pylikwid
  ```

- **Locale Settings**:
  ```bash
  unset LC_ALL
  export LANG=en_US.UTF-8
  ```

- **Module Load**:
  ```bash
  module load python/3.8-anaconda
  module load likwid
  ```

### Conclusion:
The user's issues were resolved through a series of troubleshooting steps involving updating software, adjusting SLURM commands, fixing account setup issues, and adjusting locale settings. The provided solutions can be used to address similar issues in the future.
```
---

### 2021112442003091_Call%20for%20Speakers%3A%20Admin%20%26%20Security%20Workshop%2016.12.2021%20NHR%40G%C3%83%C2%B6tting.md

 ```markdown
# HPC-Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

## Subject: Call for Speakers: Admin & Security Workshop 16.12.2021 NHR@Göttingen

### Keywords:

- NHR@Göttingen
- GWDG

### Summary:
The email is a call for speakers for an Admin & Security Workshop organized by GWDG for NHR@Göttingen on December 16, 2021. The workshop focuses on security issues related to cluster operations, including experiences with security breaches, handling security updates, designing security barriers, and configuring systems to prevent or at least complicate breaches and enable forensics.

### Root Cause of the Problem:
- No specific problem mentioned, but the email aims to address the common issue of hackers obtaining legitimate user IDs and the vulnerabilities this poses to HPC centers.

### Solution:
- The workshop aims to provide a platform for sharing experiences, best practices, and networking among professionals to enhance security measures in HPC environments.

### What Can Be Learned:
- The importance of sharing knowledge and experiences in improving security measures.
- The need for continuous updates and improvements in security protocols.
- The value of networking and establishing competence networks within the NHR community.

### Actions Taken:
- The HPC Admin closed the customer request.

### Additional Information:
- For more details, visit: [Workshop Information](https://docs.gwdg.de/doku.php?id=en:services:application_services:high_performance_computing:workshops:admin_workshop_nov_2021)
- Contact: [GWDG Support](https://gwdg.de/support)
```

### 2018032242000467_Lammps%20job%20on%20LiMa%3A%20too%20many%20files%20open.md
# Ticket 2018032242000467

 # HPC Support Ticket: Too Many Files Open

## Keywords
- Lammps job
- Too many files open
- Operating system limits
- Node crashes
- Testing jobs
- Devel queue

## Problem Description
A user's job (ID: 2184176, named `random_move_new`) on the Lima cluster was causing issues by opening more than 20,000 files per process. This excessive file opening exhausted the operating system's limits and resulted in node crashes. The issue had occurred at least once before.

## Root Cause
The root cause of the problem was the user's job opening an excessive number of files, leading to system resource exhaustion.

## Solution
1. **User Acknowledgment**: The user acknowledged the problem and mentioned that the job had produced data but was still running.
2. **Testing**: The user requested nodes for testing the corrected code. The HPC Admin suggested using the `devel` queue, which has a limited number of nodes (8) reserved during daytime for testing purposes.
3. **Monitoring**: The HPC Admin monitored the user's subsequent jobs and noted that the problem was not yet fixed, as the running jobs still appeared strange.

## General Learnings
- Excessive file opening by jobs can exhaust system resources and crash nodes.
- The `devel` queue can be used for testing jobs to ensure they do not cause issues when submitted to the main queue.
- Monitoring user jobs after an issue is reported can help ensure the problem is resolved.

## Follow-up Actions
- If similar issues occur, advise users to check their code for excessive file opening.
- Direct users to the `devel` queue for testing their jobs after code corrections.
- Monitor user jobs to ensure the issue is resolved and does not cause further node crashes.
---

### 2023022442002211_%5Bfritz%5D%20Lange%20Ladenzeiten%20spack-Module.md
# Ticket 2023022442002211

 # HPC Support Ticket: Long Load Times for Spack Modules

## Keywords
- Spack
- Long load times
- Dependency resolution
- Clingo
- Fileserver performance

## Problem Description
Users reported extremely long load times for Spack modules on the HPC system. Small packages like `lua` took minutes to load, while more complex libraries like `gsl` were not available even after an hour. The issue was observed when running commands like `module load user-spack` and `spack install gsl +external-cblas`.

## Root Cause
The root cause of the problem was identified as Spack's dependency resolver, specifically the `clingo` solver, which was taking an excessive amount of time to resolve dependencies. Additionally, there were indications that the fileserver performance might have contributed to the issue.

## Diagnostic Steps
1. **Fileserver Performance Check**: HPC Admins checked the fileserver performance using `arc_summary` and noted that the fileserver was under heavy load, which could contribute to slow performance.
2. **Dependency Resolution Test**: HPC Admins ran `spack spec gsl +external-cblas` and observed that the dependency resolution took approximately 6 minutes, with most of the time spent in the `clingo` solver.

## Solution
- **Dependency Resolution**: The issue with Spack's dependency resolver was identified, and it was noted that the `clingo` solver was the main culprit.
- **Process Management**: Users were advised to use `Ctrl+z` to stop the `spack install` process and then use `kill -9 %%` to terminate it, instead of using `Ctrl+C`, which caused the terminal to hang.

## Additional Notes
- The fileserver performance was noted as a potential contributing factor, but the primary issue was with Spack's dependency resolution.
- The `spack install gsl +external-cblas` command failed even after waiting for a long time, indicating a deeper issue with the dependency resolution process.

## Conclusion
The long load times for Spack modules were primarily due to the inefficiency of Spack's dependency resolver, specifically the `clingo` solver. Users should be aware of this issue and use appropriate methods to manage long-running processes.
---

### 2023041742002543_Problem%20with%20ssh%20key.md
# Ticket 2023041742002543

 # HPC Support Ticket: Problem with SSH Key

## Keywords
- SSH Key
- Key Length
- ssh-rsa
- ssh-keygen
- HPC-Portal
- Connection Issues

## Problem Description
- User is a new HPC-Portal user.
- User encounters an error stating that the SSH key is too short when submitting the key.
- User generated the SSH key using the command prompt.

## Root Cause
- The SSH key generated by the user is likely less than the minimum required length of 3072 bits.

## Solution
- Ensure that the SSH key is generated with a length of at least 3072 bits.
- Use the `-b` option with `ssh-keygen` to specify the key length, e.g., `ssh-keygen -b 4096`.

## Additional Information
- The user was able to connect to `cshpc` but encountered issues connecting to `TinyGPU` or `Alex`.
- Further troubleshooting may be required to resolve the connection issues to `TinyGPU` or `Alex`.

## Follow-Up Actions
- Verify the length of the SSH key.
- Provide guidance on generating a new SSH key with the appropriate length.
- Assist the user in troubleshooting connection issues to `TinyGPU` or `Alex`.

## References
- HPC Admins: Michael Meier
- 2nd Level Support Team: Lacey, Dane (fo36fizy), Kuckuk, Sebastian (sisekuck), Lange, Florian (ow86apyf), Ernst, Dominik (te42kyfo), Mayr, Martin
- Head of the Datacenter: Gerhard Wellein
- Training and Support Group Leader: Georg Hager
- NHR Rechenzeit Support: Harald Lanig
- Software and Tools Developer: Jan Eitzinger, Gruber
---

### 2025021242000867_GPUs%20allocated%20but%20not%20utilized%20-%20b214cb15.md
# Ticket 2025021242000867

 # HPC Support Ticket Analysis: GPUs Allocated but Not Utilized

## Keywords
- GPUs
- Allocation
- Utilization
- Job Script
- `#SBATCH --exclusive`

## Summary
A user's job was allocating 8 GPUs but utilizing only one. The root cause was identified as the use of `#SBATCH --exclusive` in the job script, which exclusively allocates the node to the job.

## Root Cause
- **User Issue**: Job script used `#SBATCH --exclusive`, leading to inefficient GPU allocation.

## Solution
- **HPC Admin**: Informed the user about the inefficient use of resources due to the `#SBATCH --exclusive` directive.
- **User Response**: The user acknowledged the issue and canceled the job as it did not run as intended.

## Lessons Learned
- **Resource Allocation**: Be mindful of job script directives like `#SBATCH --exclusive` that can lead to inefficient resource allocation.
- **Communication**: Prompt communication between HPC Admins and users can help identify and resolve resource utilization issues quickly.

## Recommendations
- **Job Script Review**: Users should review their job scripts to ensure efficient use of allocated resources.
- **Support Guidance**: HPC Admins should provide guidance on best practices for job script configurations to avoid such issues in the future.

---

This documentation can be used to address similar issues related to inefficient GPU allocation in job scripts.
---

### 2023010942000262_Jobs%20auf%20Alex%20-%20b143dc10.md
# Ticket 2023010942000262

 # HPC Support Ticket Conversation Summary

## Keywords
- Job performance
- GPU utilization
- Job runtime limits
- Documentation
- Onboarding
- Virtualization
- Data I/O
- Communication with users

## General Learnings
- Users may be satisfied with CPU-only performance due to lack of GPU utilization.
- Proper module loading is crucial for GPU utilization.
- Job runtime limits can be a significant barrier for certain types of research.
- Documentation and onboarding processes need improvement.
- Virtualization options for project-specific GPU-enabled VMs can be beneficial.
- Efficient data I/O is essential for certain workflows.
- Communication with users should be improved to reduce stress and increase productivity.

## Root Cause of Problems
- Incorrect module loading leading to lack of GPU utilization.
- Job runtime limits causing overhead and inefficiency.
- Insufficient documentation and onboarding processes.
- Lack of virtualization options for project-specific needs.
- Inefficient data I/O processes.
- Communication issues leading to stress and reduced productivity.

## Solutions
- Ensure proper module loading for GPU utilization.
- Consider increasing job runtime limits where feasible.
- Improve documentation and onboarding processes.
- Explore virtualization options for project-specific GPU-enabled VMs.
- Optimize data I/O processes.
- Enhance communication with users to reduce stress and increase productivity.

## Specific Issues and Solutions
### GPU Utilization
- **Problem**: Jobs not utilizing GPUs due to missing `cudnn` module.
- **Solution**: Explicitly load the `cudnn` module in job scripts.

### Job Runtime Limits
- **Problem**: Job runtime limits causing overhead and inefficiency.
- **Solution**: Consider increasing job runtime limits and provide options for manual extensions.

### Documentation and Onboarding
- **Problem**: Users struggling with documentation and onboarding processes.
- **Solution**: Improve documentation and provide scripts for automating job restarts.

### Virtualization
- **Problem**: Lack of virtualization options for project-specific needs.
- **Solution**: Explore options for providing project-specific GPU-enabled VMs.

### Data I/O
- **Problem**: Inefficient data I/O processes.
- **Solution**: Optimize data I/O processes and consider integrating faster network storage options.

### Communication
- **Problem**: Communication issues leading to stress and reduced productivity.
- **Solution**: Improve communication with users to reduce stress and increase productivity.

## Next Steps
- Organize a meeting with relevant stakeholders to discuss and implement solutions.
- Continuously monitor and improve the HPC environment based on user feedback.
---

### 42056719_Dateien%20auf%20%24FASTTMP%20%28_wsfs_iwn2_iwn204%29.md
# Ticket 42056719

 # HPC Support Ticket: Issues with Temporary Files on $FASTTMP

## Keywords
- Temporary files
- $FASTTMP
- $WOODYHOME
- $TMPDIR
- CPLEX jobs
- File system performance
- Small files

## Problem Description
The user had millions of small temporary files on the `/wsfs/iwn2/iwn204` ($FASTTMP) filesystem, causing significant performance issues. The filesystem is designed for a few large files, not many small ones.

## Root Cause
- CPLEX jobs created a large number of small temporary files.
- The user did not clean up temporary files after job completion.

## Solution
- HPC Admins deleted the temporary files, which took approximately 48 hours.
- The user changed the temporary directory to $TMPDIR, which is local to the node and faster than network file systems.
- The user contacted IBM ILOG to inquire about reducing the number of temporary files or increasing their size.

## Lessons Learned
- Always clean up temporary files after job completion.
- Use appropriate file systems for temporary files:
  - $FASTTMP for large files.
  - $WOODYHOME for small files.
  - $TMPDIR for temporary files during job runtime.
- Contact software vendors for assistance with optimizing temporary file usage.
- Be aware that a large number of small files can significantly impact file system performance.

## Follow-up
- The user reported that IBM ILOG would consider their suggestion to reduce the number of temporary files or increase their size.
- No further issues were reported with the $TMPDIR setup.
---

### 2021092642000648_Software%20on%20cluster.md
# Ticket 2021092642000648

 ```markdown
# HPC-Support Ticket Conversation: Software on Cluster

## Summary
User encountered issues running a Python script with Gurobi on different nodes of the TinyFat cluster. The primary issues were related to Python version compatibility and Gurobi license configuration.

## Key Points
- **Python Version Compatibility**: Gurobi offers a conda package only for Python 3.7, but the user needed to run it on nodes with Python 3.8.
- **Gurobi License Issue**: Gurobi was unable to find the license on the tf050-tf057 nodes, despite the license file being in the correct location.
- **Environment Variables**: The user's environment variables for Gurobi were not properly set, causing the license issue.
- **Conda Environment Initialization**: The user encountered issues with initializing a conda environment due to a bug in conda init.

## Solutions
- **Environment Variables**: The user resolved the license issue by re-adding the necessary environment variables to the `.bashrc` file.
- **Conda Environment**: The user was advised to use `source activate myenv` instead of `conda activate myenv` to avoid initialization issues.
- **Firewall Check**: The user was advised to check if the firewall of the license server was blocking the new TinyFat nodes.

## Root Cause
- **Environment Variables**: The root cause of the license issue was the missing environment variables in the `.bashrc` file.
- **Python Version Compatibility**: The root cause of the installation issue was the lack of a Gurobi conda package for Python 3.8.

## Additional Information
- **Gurobi Installation**: The user initially installed Gurobi by downloading the tar.gz file and extracting it to `~/opt`.
- **Conda Init Bug**: The user encountered a known bug with `conda init` that required sudo permissions.

## Conclusion
The user resolved the license issue by re-adding the environment variables. The user decided not to pursue the Python 3.8 compatibility issue further.
```
---

### 2021122042001741_Early-Alex%20%22Markus%20Fritscher%22%20_%20iwi3047h.md
# Ticket 2021122042001741

 # HPC Support Ticket Conversation Summary

## Keywords
- GPU Utilization
- Job Script Optimization
- Module Initialization
- Python Environment
- CUDA
- MPS (Multi-Process Service)
- Cgroups
- Nvidia-smi
- A100 GPUs
- A40 GPUs
- Resource Allocation

## General Learnings
- **GPU Utilization**: Jobs with low GPU utilization should be optimized by combining multiple runs in a single job to increase resource efficiency.
- **Module Initialization**: Using `#/bin/bash -l` as the first line in job scripts can simplify module initialization.
- **Python Environment**: The Python module may lack certain development headers, which can be found in the Python-Spack module.
- **Cgroups**: Users see only the GPUs allocated to their job when using `nvidia-smi`.
- **MPS**: Information on MPS can be found in the cluster documentation to optimize GPU usage.

## Root Causes and Solutions
- **Low GPU Utilization**:
  - **Root Cause**: Jobs were using less than 20% of GPU resources.
  - **Solution**: Combine multiple runs in a single job to increase GPU utilization.

- **Module Initialization Issues**:
  - **Root Cause**: Manual module initialization was causing problems.
  - **Solution**: Use `#/bin/bash -l` at the beginning of the job script to simplify module loading.

- **Python Environment Issues**:
  - **Root Cause**: Missing development headers in the Python module.
  - **Solution**: Use the Python-Spack module which includes the necessary headers.

- **Cgroups Configuration**:
  - **Root Cause**: Confusion about GPU visibility in `nvidia-smi`.
  - **Solution**: Understand that `nvidia-smi` shows only the GPUs allocated to the job's Cgroup.

## Additional Notes
- **A100 GPUs**: Currently unavailable due to issues; only A40 GPUs can be used.
- **Partition Allocation**: Explicitly request the `a40-partition` as the `any-partition` is not functioning correctly.
- **Resource Allocation**: The user was granted access to more GPUs, increasing from 24 to 48 parallel GPUs.

This summary provides key insights and solutions for common issues encountered in the HPC support ticket conversation, which can be used to address similar problems in the future.
---

### 2025020342002481_Extending%20Validity%20of%20HPC%20project.md
# Ticket 2025020342002481

 # Extending Validity of HPC Project

## Keywords
- HPC account validity
- Project validity
- Management tab
- HPC portal
- PI (Principal Investigator)
- Error message

## Problem
- **Root Cause:** The user (PI) attempted to extend the validity of an HPC account but received an error stating that the account's validity cannot exceed the project's validity.
- **Error Message:** "The account’s validity cannot exceed the project's validity."

## Solution
- **Steps Taken:**
  1. The user was instructed to extend the account validity through the management tab of the HPC portal.
  2. Upon encountering the error, it was determined that the project's validity itself needed to be extended.
  3. The HPC admin confirmed that the project was valid until February 2026 and suggested that the accounts should be extendable until then.

## General Learnings
- PIs can extend account validity through the management tab of the HPC portal.
- Account validity cannot exceed the project's validity.
- If the project's validity needs to be extended, an HPC admin must perform the extension.

## References
- [HPC Portal Management Tab Documentation](https://doc.nhr.fau.de/hpc-portal/#the-management-tab-visible-only-for-pis-and-technical-contacts)

## Roles Involved
- **HPC Admins:** Provide support and manage project validity.
- **2nd Level Support Team:** Assist with initial troubleshooting and guidance.
- **PI (Principal Investigator):** Manages account validity within the project's constraints.
---

### 2025022142002553_Graphical%20applications%20on%20Fritz.md
# Ticket 2025022142002553

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: Graphical Applications on Fritz

## Keywords
- Vampir
- VNC
- SSH
- X forwarding
- Qt platform plugin
- GPU acceleration

## Problem Description
The user was unable to run the Vampir application on the Fritz node after connecting via VNC to fviz1 and using SSH with the `-x` option. The error messages received were:
- `Could not connect to display`
- `This application failed to start because no Qt platform plugin could be initialized`

## Root Cause
- The `ssh -x` command disables X forwarding, preventing the remote session from forwarding any X connection to the machine where Vampir runs.
- The new shell did not have a valid `$DISPLAY` set up for GUI applications.

## Solution
1. **Enable X Forwarding**: Use `ssh -X` or `ssh -Y` instead of `ssh -x` to enable X forwarding.
2. **Run GUI Applications Directly in VNC Session**: Wherever possible, run Vampir and other GUI applications directly within the VNC session on the node that has GPU acceleration (e.g., fviz1) to avoid performance degradation.

## General Learning
- Ensure X forwarding is enabled when using SSH for graphical applications.
- Utilize the VNC session on nodes with GPU acceleration for optimal performance of graphical applications.
- Avoid unnecessary SSH hops that can degrade performance and cause connectivity issues.
```
---

### 2021012542000584_Fehler%20bei%20Slurm-Job%20auf%20Meggie%20%28gwgi008h%29.md
# Ticket 2021012542000584

 ```markdown
### Fehler bei Slurm-Job auf Meggie (gwgi008h)

#### Problem
Die Nutzerin Franziska Temme arbeitet als Doktorandin am Institut für Geographie und nutzt das in Python geschriebene Programm COSIPY zur Simulation der Gletscheroberflächenbilanz. Das Programm funktionierte sowohl interaktiv am HPC als auch via Slurm-Job auf dem Meggie-Cluster. Nach einem Update des Programms läuft die neue Version interaktiv direkt am HPC ohne Probleme, aber nicht als Slurm-Job auf dem Meggie-Cluster.

#### Fehlermeldung
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "COSIPY.py", line 367, in <module>
    main()
  File "COSIPY.py", line 86, in main
    run_cosipy(cluster, IO, DATA, RESULT, RESTART, futures)
  File "COSIPY.py", line 273, in run_cosipy
    LAYER_IRREDUCIBLE_WATER,LAYER_REFREEZE,stake_names,stat,df_eval = future.result()
  File "/home/hpc/gwgk/gwgi008h/libraries/pip3.6/lib/python3.6/site-packages/distributed/client.py", line 221, in result
    six.reraise(*result)
  File "/apps/python/3.6-anaconda/lib/python3.6/site-packages/six.py", line 693, in reraise
    raise value
distributed.scheduler.KilledWorker: ('cosipy_core-693c8603a77db599ec39b1889ba12aa9', <Worker 'tcp://10.28.25.48:34674', memory: 0, processing: 9>)
```

#### Ursache
Die Ursache des Problems liegt in der Verwendung des Parameters `scheduler_port` in der neuen Version von COSIPY, der in der aktuellen Version von `dask-jobqueue` nicht mehr unterstützt wird.

#### Lösung
1. **Entfernen des Parameters `scheduler_port`:**
   - In der Datei `COSIPY.py` in Zeile 88 den Parameter `scheduler_port=port` entfernen.

2. **Anpassen der `slurm_config.py`:**
   - Ändern von `shebang = '/bin/bash -l'` zu `shebang = '#!/bin/bash -l'`.

3. **Verwenden einer älteren Version von `dask-jobqueue`:**
   - Falls die obigen Schritte nicht ausreichen, kann eine ältere Version von `dask-jobqueue` verwendet werden, die den Parameter `scheduler_port` noch unterstützt.

#### Zusätzliche Informationen
- Die Nutzerin hat zusätzliche Python-Module installiert, die in der neuen Version von COSIPY benötigt werden.
- Die alte Version von COSIPY läuft problemlos auf Meggie, was darauf hindeutet, dass das Shell-Script korrekt ist.
- Die Daten der Nutzerin liegen unter:
  - Installierte Python-Module: `/home/hpc/gwgk/gwgi008h/libraries/pip3.6/`
  - COSIPY (neue Version): `/home/titan/gwgk/gwgi008h/COSIPY/cosipy-1.4`
  - COSIPY (alte Version): `/home/titan/gwgk/gwgi008h/COSIPY/cosipy-1.3.1`

#### HPC-Kennung
- Die HPC-Kennung der Nutzerin ist `gwgi008h`.

#### Kontakt
- Die Nutzerin ist auch in Kontakt mit den COSIPY-Entwicklern, um das Problem zu lösen.
- Die Entwickler haben Schwierigkeiten, alles auf einem fremden Cluster zu verstehen, genauso wie es für die HPC Admins schwierig ist, alles bei einem fremden Programm zu verstehen.

#### Fazit
Das Problem konnte durch die Entfernung des Parameters `scheduler_port` und die Anpassung der `slurm_config.py` gelöst werden. Falls dies nicht ausreicht, kann eine ältere Version von `dask-jobqueue` verwendet werden.
```
---

### 2022053142000563_Probleme%20beim%20Job%20ausf%C3%83%C2%BChren%20TinyGPU.md
# Ticket 2022053142000563

 # HPC Support Ticket: Probleme beim Job ausführen TinyGPU

## Keywords
- TinyGPU
- Jobscript
- Python-Code
- PBS
- Slurm
- Anaconda
- FAU-Email-Adresse
- qsub
- GPU
- CPU
- Ubuntu 20.04
- Interaktiver Job

## Problem
- User konnte Python-Code auf TinyGPU nicht ausführen.
- Jobscript und Output wurden angefordert.
- User verwendete falsche Email-Adresse für Support-Anfragen.
- User führte Jobscript direkt auf dem Frontend-Knoten aus.
- Jobscript enthielt gemischte Optionen von PBS und Slurm.

## Lösung
- Verwenden der FAU-Email-Adresse für Support-Anfragen.
- Jobscript mit `qsub.tinygpu` einreichen.
- Anzahl der GPUs über die Anzahl der angeforderten CPUs bestimmen (4 CPUs pro GPU).
- Beispiele für Slurm-basierte Jobscripts auf TinyGPU verwenden.
- Anaconda-Modul im Jobscript laden.
- Anaconda-Environment neu bauen, da Slurm-Knoten Ubuntu-basiert sind.
- Interaktiven Job anfordern, um das Environment direkt auf dem Knoten zu bauen.

## Root Cause
- Falsche Email-Adresse für Support-Anfragen.
- Falsche Ausführung des Jobscripts auf dem Frontend-Knoten.
- Gemischte Optionen von PBS und Slurm im Jobscript.
- Unterschiedliche Betriebssysteme zwischen PBS- und Slurm-Knoten.

## Dokumentation
- [Slurm Batch Scripts auf TinyGPU](https://hpc.fau.de/systems-services/documentation-instructions/clusters/tinygpu-cluster/)
- [Interaktive Slurm Jobs](https://hpc.fau.de/systems-services/documentation-instructions/clusters/tinygpu-cluster/#interactive_slurm)

## Weitere Schritte
- User sollte die angehängten Dokumente überprüfen und gegebenenfalls weitere Anpassungen vornehmen.
- Bei weiteren Problemen sollte der User erneut Kontakt mit dem HPC-Support aufnehmen.
---

### 2018120342002751_CPU%20mit%20AVX-512.md
# Ticket 2018120342002751

 # HPC Support Ticket: CPU with AVX-512 Instruction Set

## Keywords
- AVX-512
- CPU
- Testcluster
- Xeon Gold 6148
- HPC Account
- LIKWID
- Performance Counter
- Proof of Concept

## Problem
- User requires access to a CPU with AVX-512 instruction set for a research project.
- No such CPU is currently available at the user's department.
- User needs to test the feasibility of the project before purchasing a CPU.

## Solution
- HPC Admin provided access to a test cluster with Xeon Gold 6148 CPUs that support AVX-512.
- Instructions for logging into the test cluster and submitting an interactive job were given.
- Recommendation to use LIKWID module version 4.3.2 for pinning and performance counter measurements.
- User was advised to obtain an HPC account for access.

## Steps to Access AVX-512 CPU
1. Obtain a valid HPC account.
2. Log into the test cluster:
   ```sh
   ssh <user>@testfront1.rrze.uni-erlangen.de
   ```
3. Load the PBS Pro module:
   ```sh
   module load pbspro
   ```
4. Submit an interactive job:
   ```sh
   qsub -l nodes=skylakesp2:ppn=80,walltime=01:00:00 -I
   ```
5. Optionally, use LIKWID for pinning and performance counter measurements.

## Notes
- The test machine's system parameters, including clock frequency, may be arbitrarily set.
- Contact HPC Support for further assistance.

## Conclusion
- The user was successfully guided to access a CPU with AVX-512 support for their proof of concept.
- The solution involved using the test cluster and obtaining an HPC account.
---

### 2024021642002752_GPUDirect%20RDMA.md
# Ticket 2024021642002752

 ```markdown
# HPC-Support Ticket: GPUDirect RDMA

## Keywords
- GPUDirect RDMA
- GROMACS
- cuFFTmp
- nvidia-peermem
- nv_peer_mem
- RHEL in-box OFED drivers
- MOFED stack

## Problem Description
- **User Issue:** User compiled GROMACS with cuFFTmp library on Alex but encountered an error: `NULL value mem registration failed`.
- **Root Cause:** According to Nvidia troubleshooting, the error is due to GPUDirect RDMA not being enabled, which requires the `nv_peer_mem` kernel module.

## Ticket Conversation Summary
- **User Request:** User requested the installation of `nv_peer_mem` to enable GPUDirect RDMA.
- **HPC Admin Response:**
  - Clarified that Alex uses RHEL in-box OFED drivers, not the MOFED stack, making `nvidia-peermem.ko` unloadable.
  - Suggested that the cluster in Karlsruhe might have the required option available.

## Solution
- **Immediate Action:** The user was advised to try accessing the cluster in Karlsruhe, which might support GPUDirect RDMA.
- **Long-term Consideration:** No immediate solution on Alex due to the use of RHEL in-box OFED drivers.

## Lessons Learned
- **Driver Compatibility:** Understanding the differences between RHEL in-box OFED drivers and the MOFED stack is crucial for enabling specific features like GPUDirect RDMA.
- **Alternative Resources:** When local resources cannot support a feature, exploring other clusters or resources can be a viable solution.

## References
- [Nvidia GPUDirect RDMA FAQ](https://docs.nvidia.com/nvshmem/api/faq.html)
```
---

### 2024040842001747_Activate%20my%20HPC%20account%20-%20compiling%20WaSiM%20-%20gz16100h.md
# Ticket 2024040842001747

 # HPC Support Ticket Conversation Summary

## Keywords
- Account activation
- SSH key
- MobaXTerm
- Woody cluster
- WinSCP
- VPN
- OpenMPI
- NetCDF
- Compilation errors

## General Learnings
- **Account Activation**: New users may face issues with account activation even after generating SSH keys.
- **Cluster Access**: Tier3 users need to access specific clusters like Woody for multi-core CPU tasks.
- **WinSCP Configuration**: Users may need guidance on setting up WinSCP, including hostnames and SSH key attachment.
- **Compilation Issues**: Common issues include missing modules, incorrect library paths, and deprecated features.

## Root Causes and Solutions

### Account Activation
- **Problem**: User's account not available despite successful SSH key generation.
- **Solution**: HPC Admins can activate the account and provide the correct cluster login information.

### Cluster Access
- **Problem**: User attempted to log in to the wrong cluster (GPU cluster Alex).
- **Solution**: Direct the user to the appropriate cluster (Woody) for multi-core CPU tasks.

### WinSCP Configuration
- **Problem**: Incorrect hostname and unsuccessful connection.
- **Solution**: Provide the correct hostname (`csnhr.nhr.fau.de`) and refer to the official WinSCP documentation.

### Compilation Issues
- **Problem**: Missing modules (`foss`), incorrect library paths, and deprecated OpenMPI C++ bindings.
- **Solution**:
  - Use available modules (`openmpi/4.1.3-gcc12.1.0`, `netcdf-c/4.8.1-ompi`).
  - Correct library paths (`NETCDF_C_ROOT` instead of `NETCDF_ROOT`).
  - Use compile wrappers (`mpic++`, `mpiCC`) for OpenMPI with C++ bindings.
  - Consider using IntelMPI if OpenMPI does not work.

## Documentation and Tutorials
- **WinSCP**: Official documentation available at [FAU NHR WinSCP Guide](https://doc.nhr.fau.de/data/copying/#winscp).
- **OpenMPI Compilation**: Use compile wrappers and correct library paths as per the provided solutions.

## Additional Notes
- **VPN**: Users may need to set up a VPN for external access, but an FAU email account is not necessarily required.
- **Software Developer Input**: Collaboration with software developers can help resolve specific compilation issues.

This summary provides a concise overview of the support ticket conversation, highlighting key issues and their resolutions for future reference.
---

### 42111692_NFS-Last%20durch%20pw.x%20Rechnungen.md
# Ticket 42111692

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket: NFS-Last durch pw.x Rechnungen

## Keywords
- NFS-Last
- IO-Last
- Simulationsdaten
- $HOME
- $WOODYHOME
- /home/vault
- $TMPDIR
- Quantum-Espresso
- PBS-Jobs
- Fileserver

## Problem
- User's compute jobs on TinyBlue caused excessive IO load, affecting all users on the HPC systems due to the NFS load.
- The user's job (68692) was put on hold to prevent further issues.

## Root Cause
- The user was storing simulation data in $HOME, which is not designed for such data due to frequent snapshots and backups.
- Quantum-Espresso, the software used, is known for high IO load.

## Solution
- The user was advised to move simulation data to $WOODYHOME or /home/vault for long-term storage.
- For jobs on TinyBlue, the user should copy necessary data to $TMPDIR at the beginning of the PBS job and move it back to /home/vault or /home/woody at the end.
- If data must be generated directly in /home/*, the IO intensity should be significantly reduced.

## General Lessons
- Avoid storing simulation data in $HOME due to frequent snapshots and backups.
- Use $TMPDIR for temporary data during jobs to reduce load on the fileserver.
- Be mindful of the IO load generated by software like Quantum-Espresso and adjust data handling practices accordingly.

## Status
- The issue was resolved by the user adjusting their job script to use $TMPDIR.
- The user apologized for the inconvenience caused to other users.
```
---

### 2024061042002916_Problem%20with%20Fritz.md

 # HPC Support Ticket: Problem with Fritz Cluster

## Keywords

- SSH connection
- SLURM commands
- squeue
- Denial of service attack

## Problem Description
- User reported slow SSH connection to Fritz cluster.
- SLURM commands (e.g., `squeue`) resulted in errors:
  ```
  squeue: error: Invalid user for SlurmUser slurm, ignored
  squeue: fatal: Unable to process configuration file
  ```
- User's account worked fine on another cluster (Alex).

## Root Cause
- Denial of service attack on Fritz cluster infrastructure.

## Solution
- HPC Admins stabilized the operation of the Fritz cluster.

## Lessons Learned
- Slow SSH connections and SLURM command errors can indicate broader infrastructure issues.
- Check for ongoing attacks or system-wide problems when users report such issues.
- Communicate with users about the resolution of infrastructure problems.

## Actions Taken
- HPC Admins acknowledged the issue and informed the user about the denial of service attack.
- The cluster operation was stabilized, resolving the user's issues.

## Follow-up
- Monitor the cluster for any recurring issues.
- Ensure users are informed about any future attacks or disruptions.
---

### 2023072042005743_Frage%20zu%20Betriebssystem%20und%20Partitionierung%20Tier3-HPC-Cluster.md
# Ticket 2023072042005743

 # HPC-Support Ticket Conversation: Operating System and Partitioning on Tier3-HPC-Cluster

## Keywords
- Operating System
- Partitioning
- HPC Nodes
- AlmaLinux
- Ubuntu
- SPACK
- Modules
- Lmod
- EasyBuild

## Summary
A user inquired about the operating system and partitioning scheme used on the HPC nodes at the Friedrich-Alexander-Universität Erlangen-Nürnberg (FAU). The HPC Admin provided detailed information on the operating systems used and the partitioning approach.

## Root Cause
The user needed information on the operating system and partitioning of HPC nodes to configure a new node in their data center.

## Solution
- **Operating Systems**:
  - **GPU-Cluster Alex and Parallelrechner Meggie + Fritz**: AlmaLinux 8.x
  - **TinyGPU and TinyFAT**: Ubuntu 20.04
- **Partitioning**:
  - Parallelrechner run disk/stateless directly from a network-booted RAMdisk, hence no partitioning.
- **Software Installation**:
  - Prefer using SPACK for scientific software.
  - Provide different versions via Modules.
  - Consider using Lmod for module management.
  - EasyBuild is an alternative to SPACK.

## General Learnings
- The choice of Linux distribution should be based on admin familiarity or ISV software support.
- Regular updates and distribution changes are common in HPC environments.
- Network-booted RAMdisks are used for disk/stateless operation.
- SPACK and EasyBuild are preferred tools for managing scientific software.
- Modules and Lmod are used for managing software versions.

## References
- [SPACK](https://github.com/spack/spack/)
- [Modules](https://modules.readthedocs.io/en/latest/)
- [Lmod](https://lmod.readthedocs.io/en/latest/)

## Conclusion
The HPC Admin provided comprehensive guidance on the operating systems, partitioning, and software management practices used at FAU, which can be applied to configure new HPC nodes.
---

### 2022033142003117_The%20system%20requirements%20for%20HPC.md
# Ticket 2022033142003117

 # HPC Support Ticket: System Requirements for HPC

## Keywords
- HPC system requirements
- Python simulations
- Fourier spectral method
- FFT (numpy)
- Memory requirements
- Parallel computing
- mpi4py
- TinyFAT cluster

## Problem Description
- User is a PhD student running 3D simulations for domain evolution in ferroelectric materials.
- Code is fully developed in Python using the Fourier spectral method and FFT from the numpy package.
- Current office computer has 8 CPUs and 32 GB memory, but only 2 cores are used with full memory.
- Execution time grows exponentially with an increase in grid points.
- User needs to run simulations with at least 5000 time steps.
- User is unsure about system requirements for HPC application form.

## Root Cause
- Insufficient memory and computational resources on the user's office computer.
- Inefficient use of computational resources (only 2 cores utilized).

## Solution
- **HPC Admin** suggests using the TinyFAT cluster for the job.
- Recommends looking into mpi4py for parallel computing, which includes a parallel FFT implementation.
- Informs the user that the system requirements in the application form are not binding and are meant to provide a rough plan.

## General Learnings
- Understanding the limitations of local computational resources.
- Importance of specifying rough plans for system requirements in HPC application forms.
- Exploring parallel computing options like mpi4py for improved performance.
- Identifying suitable HPC clusters (e.g., TinyFAT) for specific computational needs.

## References
- [TinyFAT Cluster Documentation](https://hpc.fau.de/systems-services/systems-documentation-instructions/clusters/tinyfat-cluster/)
- [mpi4py FFT Documentation](https://mpi4py-fft.readthedocs.io/en/latest/parallel.html)

### 2025031342002861_Jobs%20pending.md
# Ticket 2025031342002861

 # HPC Support Ticket: Jobs Pending

## Keywords
- Job pending
- Node limit
- GPU allocation
- Slurm limits
- Hierarchical limits
- Resource throttling

## Problem Description
- Users reported jobs pending due to node limit reasons.
- The user's group was occupying more nodes than allocated.

## Root Cause
- The user's group was using 75 GPUs (19 nodes) while the allocated limit was lower.
- Other groups from the same institution were also active, leading to resource contention.

## HPC Admin Response
- The HPC admin explained the current resource allocation and the need to adjust the throttling policy.
- Hierarchical limits were described, with specific limits for different groups and projects.

## Solution
- The HPC admin adjusted the throttling policy to ensure fair resource allocation.
- Specific limits were set for different groups to balance utilization and access.

## General Learnings
- Understanding hierarchical limits and resource allocation policies is crucial for managing HPC resources.
- Regular adjustments to limits may be necessary to ensure high utilization and fair access.
- Polite and professional communication is essential for effective support interactions.

## Action Items
- Review and adjust resource limits as needed.
- Ensure clear communication of resource allocation policies to users.
- Maintain a professional tone in support communications.
---

### 42286209_How%20to%20enable%20automatic%20offload%20to%20INTEL%20MIC%20cards.md
# Ticket 42286209

 # HPC Support Ticket: Automatic Offload to Intel MIC Cards

## Keywords
- Intel MKL libraries
- Automatic offloading
- MKL_MIC_ENABLE
- OFFLOAD_REPORT
- MIC utilization
- Job script
- Linpack benchmark

## Problem Description
- User unable to get automatic offloading feature of Intel MKL libraries working.
- Environment variables `MKL_MIC_ENABLE` and `OFFLOAD_REPORT` set as instructed, but no utilization observed on MIC cards.
- User's program is heavily dependent on MKL and appears to be calling MKL intensively.

## Troubleshooting Steps
- HPC Admin provided a working example using Linpack benchmark.
- Example command: `qsub -I -lnodes=1:ppn=40:phi2x,walltime=10:0:0`
- Module used: `mkl/11.0up05`
- Offloading observed starting from size 8192 in Linpack benchmark.

## Solution
- Verify the job script and ensure it follows the working example provided by HPC Admin.
- Ensure the correct module (e.g., `mkl/11.0up05`) is loaded.
- Run the Linpack benchmark to confirm offloading functionality.

## General Learnings
- Proper module loading and job script configuration are crucial for enabling automatic offloading.
- Linpack benchmark can be used to verify offloading functionality.
- Offloading may start at a specific problem size, as observed in the Linpack benchmark.
---

### 2024040442002619_HPC%20Group%20name%20iwfa%20%7C%20ID%20101703%20%7C%20Institute%20FAPS%20-%20Question%20regarding%20.md
# Ticket 2024040442002619

 # HPC Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

## Keywords
- AssocGrpGRES
- TinyGPU
- GPU
- HPC
- FAU
- NHR@FAU
- Slurm
- ClusterCockpit
- Coordinator-Rechte
- squeue
- sacctmgr
- acc_mem_used

## General Learnings
- **AssocGrpGRES**: Indicates that all GPUs assigned to the association or group are currently in use.
- **TinyGPU**: A cluster primarily used for student projects, funded by specific research groups.
- **Coordinator-Rechte**: Special permissions that allow canceling or pausing jobs from group members.
- **ClusterCockpit**: A tool for monitoring job status and resource usage.
- **squeue**: Command to display job queue information.
- **sacctmgr**: Command to manage Slurm accounting data.
- **acc_mem_used**: Metric for tracking GPU memory usage.

## Root Causes and Solutions

### Problem: Students unable to access GPUs due to AssocGrpGRES
- **Root Cause**: High demand and limited resources, exacerbated by some users submitting excessive jobs.
- **Solution**:
  - Implemented throttling for specific users.
  - Provided Coordinator-Rechte to the group manager to cancel or pause problematic jobs.
  - Encouraged better job scheduling and resource sharing among students.

### Problem: Inability to track individual job usage and group quota
- **Root Cause**: Lack of visibility into individual job submissions and group-specific resource usage.
- **Solution**:
  - Granted Coordinator-Rechte to the group manager for better job management.
  - Used `squeue -A iwfa` to view group-specific job queues.
  - Encouraged students to check overall cluster usage with `sinfo`.

### Problem: Incorrect GPU memory usage display in ClusterCockpit
- **Root Cause**: Incorrect maximum values set for GPU memory usage.
- **Solution**:
  - Identified the issue with GPU memory display.
  - Planned to update the maximum values to reflect actual GPU memory sizes.

## Additional Notes
- **Filesystem Issues**: Ongoing problems with the filesystem, causing intermittent disruptions.
- **Future Expansion**: Discussed potential future expansion of TinyGPU, considering cost and resource availability.
- **User Communication**: Established a Teams group for better communication and resource sharing among students.

## Conclusion
The conversation highlights the importance of effective resource management and communication in an HPC environment. By granting additional permissions and encouraging better job scheduling, the HPC support team was able to address immediate issues and plan for future improvements.

### 2022020942000744_error%20submitting%20job%20via%20torque%20on%20tinygpu%20%28iwal004%29.md
# Ticket 2022020942000744

 # HPC Support Ticket: Error Submitting Job via Torque on tinygpu

## Keywords
- Torque
- Conda
- Job Submission
- Shell Configuration
- Module Load
- Python
- CUDA

## Problem Description
- User encountered `CommandNotFoundError` when submitting a job script to the tinygpu cluster.
- The error message indicated that the shell was not properly configured to use `conda activate`.
- The script used to work but started failing after more than a year.

## Root Cause
- The `conda` command syntax or environment setup had changed, causing the old script to fail.

## Script Details
```bash
#!/bin/bash -l
# allocate 4 nodes (80 Cores / 160 SMT threads) for 6 hours
#PBS -l nodes=1:ppn=4,walltime=23:50:00
# job name
#PBS -N master
# first non-empty non-comment line ends PBS options
module load cuda/10.2
module load python/3.8-anaconda
conda activate torch
python train.py
```

## Attempted Solutions
- User tried adding `conda init bash` and `conda init tcsh` to the script, but it did not resolve the issue.
- The script worked fine when commands were executed manually in an interactive shell.

## Solution Provided by HPC Admin
- Replace `conda activate torch` with `source activate torch` in the job script.

## General Learnings
- Conda commands and environment setup can change over time, affecting old scripts.
- Always check for updates in command syntax and environment configuration when reusing old scripts.
- Using `source activate` instead of `conda activate` can resolve issues related to shell configuration.

## Additional Notes
- Ensure that the shell is properly configured to use Conda commands.
- Restarting the shell after running `conda init` may be necessary for changes to take effect.

## Relevant Commands
- `conda init <SHELL_NAME>`
- `source activate <ENV_NAME>`

## Support Team
- HPC Admins
- 2nd Level Support Team
- Software and Tools Developers
- Head of the Datacenter
- Training and Support Group Leader
- NHR Rechenzeit Support and Applications for Grants
---

### 2022071842003484_Fwd%3A%20Need%20some%20help%20understanding%20code%20performance%20%28Issue%20with%20linear%20solver.md
# Ticket 2022071842003484

 # HPC Support Ticket: Solver Performance Issue

## Keywords
- Linear Solvers
- MPI Communication
- Deadlock
- Trilinos
- Mumps
- Superlu-dist
- CG+AMG
- Performance Analysis
- MPI Trace Analysis

## Problem Description
The user encountered performance issues with linear solvers in a multi-node scenario. The code gets stuck during the solver switch from iterative to direct solvers (CG to Mumps or Superlu-dist) for large problem sizes.

## Root Cause
- Indefinite communication causing indefinite wait time in some inter-node communication.
- Possible memory accumulation with runtime that needs to be sent across several processes when switching to direct solver.

## Observations
- The issue was observed for large problem sizes (1.6 million degrees of freedom) with 8 nodes but not for smaller problem sizes (400k degrees of freedom) on 2 nodes.
- The code does not get stuck if the Trilinos error is thrown early in the time-step.
- No issue observed if the direct solver is used from the start.

## Suggested Solutions
- Perform an MPI trace analysis using tools like Intel ITAC to detect where the code runs into deadlock.
- Synchronize all processes before switching solvers to avoid deadlocks.
- Check MPI communications in the interface between iterative and direct solvers.

## Additional Information
- The user provided displacement matrices for a few iterations in Trilinos format.
- The user suggested testing the linear solver performance with different configurations:
  - CG+AMG
  - CG+AMG switched to Superlu-dist
  - CG+AMG switched to Mumps
  - Superlu-dist
  - Mumps

## Next Steps
- Analyze the provided displacement matrices to understand the solver performance.
- Perform MPI trace analysis to identify the root cause of the deadlock.
- Synchronize processes before switching solvers to mitigate the issue.

## Conclusion
The issue is likely related to indefinite communication and memory accumulation during the solver switch. Further analysis and synchronization of processes are recommended to resolve the deadlock.
---

### 2024010542000482_question%20about%20cluster%20-%20mfsi100h.md
# Ticket 2024010542000482

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket Conversation Summary

## Issue:
- User encountered memory allocation issues and missing dependencies while using RStudio on the HPC cluster.
- Specifically, the user had trouble with the `hdf5` dependency and memory allocation for their RStudio session.

## Key Points:
- The user initially had issues with memory allocation, where the allocated memory was much lower than expected.
- The user also faced issues with installing the `hdf5` package due to missing dependencies.
- The user attempted to use a custom RStudio container but faced issues with running it on the compute nodes.

## Solutions:
- **Memory Allocation:**
  - The user was advised to specify the number of cores and memory allocation correctly using the `salloc` command.
  - Example: `salloc --constraint=icx --nodes 1 --time 06:00:00 --mem=128G`

- **HDF5 Dependency:**
  - The user was advised to load the necessary modules for `hdf5`:
    ```bash
    module load 000-all-spack-pkgs/0.18.1
    module load hdf5/1.12.2-gcc8.5.0-impi-s2vbl5o
    ```
  - The user was also advised to ensure the correct path to the `h5cc` file and to install the `pkg-config` package if missing.

- **Custom RStudio Container:**
  - The user was advised to ensure the container was correctly configured and to delete any old session files that might be causing conflicts.
  - The user was also advised to check the installation path and ensure it points to the correct location for installing R packages.

## Conclusion:
- The user was able to resolve the memory allocation issue by correctly specifying the resources.
- The user was able to resolve the `hdf5` dependency issue by loading the correct modules and ensuring the correct path to the `h5cc` file.
- The user was able to run the custom RStudio container on the compute nodes by ensuring the container was correctly configured and deleting old session files.

## Additional Notes:
- The user was advised to use the native R module if the container approach continued to cause issues.
- The user was also advised to consider creating their own container if the provided one did not meet their needs.

This summary provides a concise overview of the issues faced by the user and the solutions provided by the HPC support team. It can be used as a reference for future support cases with similar issues.
```
---

### 2018072642000551_gmond%20port%20access.md
# Ticket 2018072642000551

 ```markdown
# HPC-Support Ticket: gmond port access

## Keywords
- gmond port
- pbs4rzacct
- Apache configuration
- Firewall settings
- Connection refused
- Perl scripts migration

## Summary
A user requested access to the gmond port and pbs4rzacct on new servers (eadm and ladm) while keeping the old server (fepa) active for parallel operation.

## Problem
- Initial access to gmond port was successful.
- `wget` requests to `pbs4rzacct` on new servers resulted in "Connection refused" errors.

## Root Cause
- The new IP was added to the Apache configuration but not to the firewall settings.

## Solution
- The HPC Admin updated the firewall settings to allow access from the new IP.

## Lessons Learned
- Ensure both Apache configuration and firewall settings are updated when adding new IPs.
- Verify all necessary configurations are in place to avoid partial access issues.
```
---

### 2022050642001592_Nachfrage.md
# Ticket 2022050642001592

 ```markdown
# HPC Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

## Subject: Nachfrage

### Keywords:
- HPC
- SLURM
- Memory Bandbreite
- NUMA Domänen
- MPI Prozesse
- Fritz
- Hyperthreading
- Speicherbandbreite
- Skalierung
- Antragssystem
- DFG-Antrag
- Container-Lösung

### General Learnings:
- **Memory Bandwidth**: Memory bandwidth can significantly affect the performance of jobs, especially when using a large number of cores per node.
- **NUMA Domains**: Proper distribution of processes across NUMA domains is crucial for optimal performance.
- **MPI Processes**: The communication between MPI processes can have a minimal impact on performance in certain scenarios.
- **SLURM Configuration**: Correct configuration of SLURM and MPI is essential for efficient resource utilization.
- **Scaling**: Reducing the number of cores per node can lead to better performance due to increased memory bandwidth per process.
- **Antragssystem**: There is a simplified application system for HPC resources if a DFG/BMBF project is already in place.

### Root Cause of the Problem:
- The user observed significant differences in computation times when varying the number of cores per node. This was attributed to memory bandwidth limitations.

### Solution:
- Reducing the number of cores per node can improve performance by increasing the available memory bandwidth per process.
- Ensure proper distribution of processes across NUMA domains to avoid performance bottlenecks.
- Consider applying for HPC resources through a simplified application process if a relevant project is already funded.

### Additional Notes:
- The user was advised to monitor the memory bandwidth usage and consider the impact of communication between MPI processes.
- The HPC Admins provided detailed explanations and suggestions for further testing and optimization.
- The user was encouraged to apply for additional HPC resources through a simplified application process.
```
---

### 2025012942001096_suggested%20improvement%20of%20ansys%20bash%20and%20journal%20script%20in%20your%20documentation.md
# Ticket 2025012942001096

 # HPC Support Ticket: Improvements for Ansys Fluent Scripts

## Keywords
- Ansys Fluent
- Script Improvements
- Idle Time Minimization
- Case File Handling
- Journal File Settings

## Problem
- User encountered idle time on the cluster due to typos in case files or journal files.
- Hardcoded case file paths in journal files caused issues when paths were incorrect.

## Suggested Improvements
1. **Specifying Case File in Bash Script**
   - Pass the case file as an argument to the bash script to avoid hardcoding paths.
   ```bash
   CASE_FILE=$(realpath "${2}")
   fluent 3ddp -g -env -case "$CASE_FILE"
   ```

2. **Setting `/file/set-error-on-invalid-command` to `no`**
   - Prevent Fluent from aborting the journal script on invalid commands or missing case files.
   ```text
   /file/set-error-on-invalid-command no
   ```

3. **Specifying Idle Timeout in Journal File**
   - Set an idle timeout to terminate the session if it remains idle for a specified duration.
   ```text
   /file/set-idle-timeout yes 3 yes
   ```

4. **Setting Fluent Batch Option `exit-on-error` to `yes`**
   - Ensure Fluent exits on errors to avoid staying in idle time.
   ```text
   /file/set-batch-options yes yes no
   ```

5. **Changing `exit y` to `(exit)` in Journal File**
   - Use `(exit)` to ensure the Fluent session exits from any submenu.
   ```text
   (exit)
   ```

## Solution
- HPC Admins reviewed and added the user's suggestions to the documentation to improve future simulations and minimize idle time.

## Documentation Update
- The improvements were added to the [Ansys Fluent documentation](https://doc.nhr.fau.de/apps/ansys-fluent/#notes).

## Conclusion
- The user's feedback was valuable in improving the Ansys Fluent scripts and documentation, leading to more efficient use of cluster resources.
---

### 2024051942000789_Requiring%20CPU%20hours%20for%20downloading%20dataset%20-%20Panda-10M%20-%20b207dd14_b211dd11.md
# Ticket 2024051942000789

 # HPC Support Ticket: Downloading Large Dataset from YouTube

## Keywords
- CPU hours
- Dataset download
- YouTube videos
- Proxy bottleneck
- IP blacklisting
- Post-processing
- Data transfer
- Webdataset

## Problem
- User requires significant CPU hours to download and process a large video dataset (Panda-10M) from YouTube.
- Dataset size is around 8TB, and the estimated CPU hours needed are around 224k.

## Root Cause
- Downloading large amounts of YouTube videos can cause proxy bottlenecks and risk IP blacklisting.
- The dataset involves a large number of files, which can be problematic for storage and processing.

## Solution
- **Download Restriction**: Downloading large amounts of YouTube videos is not allowed on the HPC system due to proxy bottlenecks and the risk of IP blacklisting.
- **Alternative Download Location**: Users are advised to download the data from another location (e.g., TUM or MCML systems at LRZ).
- **Data Transfer**: Data needs to be transferred to the HPC system using methods like FTP. Refer to the documentation for data copying: [Data Copying Documentation](https://doc.nhr.fau.de/data/copying/).
- **Post-Processing**: CPU resources can be offered for post-processing if the data is stored in a reasonable number of files (e.g., using webdataset).

## General Learnings
- Always consider the impact of large-scale downloads on shared resources.
- Use alternative locations for downloading large datasets to avoid affecting the HPC system.
- Ensure data is stored efficiently to facilitate processing and storage.
- Follow documentation for data transfer methods to move data to the HPC system.
---

# HPC-Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

- **Account Linking**: Accounts need to be associated with a relevant department or chair.
- **System Constraints**: Long-term computations are not supported; users should optimize their workflows.
- **Parallelization**: Encourage users to parallelize their code to reduce computation time.

### 2022013142001695_slurm%20on%20tiny.fat%20-%20modules%20will%20not%20load.md
# Ticket 2022013142001695

 # HPC-Support Ticket Conversation Summary

## Subject: slurm on tiny.fat - modules will not load

### User Issue:
- User Philipp Malz is having trouble converting a bash script from Torque to Slurm on tiny.fat.
- The script fails to load GAMMA and R modules.
- The user has tried setting `--export=NONE` and `unset SLURM_EXPORT_ENV`, but it did not help.

### HPC Admin Response:
- The `MODULESHOME` path has changed to `/apps/modules/4.6.1` on tiny.fat.
- The user should not explicitly set environment variables; they are automatically set in a login shell.
- The user should use `--export=<environment_variables>` to export specific variables.
- The user should use `sbatch -M tinyfat` to specify the cluster when submitting a job within a job.

### Additional Issues:
- The GAMMA module version 20191203 is not compatible with Ubuntu 20.04 on tiny.fat.
- The user requested the installation of a newer GAMMA version (20210701) on both tiny.fat and woody.

### Solutions:
- The HPC Admin installed the newer GAMMA version (20210701) on both tiny.fat and woody.
- The user should test the new GAMMA module and provide feedback if any dependencies are missing or if it does not function correctly.

### Key Learnings:
- Always check the module paths and versions when migrating scripts between different schedulers or clusters.
- Use `--export=<environment_variables>` to export specific variables in Slurm.
- Use `sbatch -M tinyfat` to specify the cluster when submitting a job within a job.
- Consider using Singularity containers for complex software dependencies to ensure compatibility across different clusters and operating systems.

### Documentation:
- [Slurm Documentation](https://slurm.schedmd.com/sbatch.html)
- [Singularity Containers Introduction](https://hpc.fau.de/files/2020/05/2020-05-12-singularity-containers.pdf)

### Next Steps:
- The user will test the new GAMMA module and provide feedback.
- The HPC Admin will assist with creating a Singularity container if needed.

### Closure:
- The ticket is closed for now. The user will reopen it if further issues arise.
---

### 2022121342000235_Long%20pending%20jobs%20on%20tinyfat.md
# Ticket 2022121342000235

 # Long Pending Jobs on tinyfat

## Keywords
- Pending jobs
- Priority
- Idle nodes
- University email
- Job submission date
- Partition priority
- Short running jobs
- Single cores
- Woody

## Problem
- User submitted jobs to `tinyfat` which remained pending with "Priority" reasoning.
- All nodes appeared idle, causing confusion.

## Root Cause
- Jobs were submitted on Sunday, not Saturday.
- All nodes in the work partition were paid for by specific groups, giving them priority.
- Idle nodes were reserved for short running jobs.
- User requested single cores, which are better suited for `Woody`.

## Solution
- Use University email for communication with HPC support.
- Move jobs requesting single cores to `Woody`.

## Lessons Learned
- Always use the official University email for support communications.
- Understand partition priorities and job types suitable for different clusters.
- Check job submission details carefully.
- Short running jobs have reserved idle nodes.
---

### 2025011742000421_Anfrage%20f%C3%83%C2%BCr%20nodes%20auf%20Fritz.md
# Ticket 2025011742000421

 # HPC Support Ticket: Request for Big Partition Access

## Keywords
- HPC
- Big Partition
- Finite-Elemente Rechnung
- HyTeG
- Slurm Account
- Node Limit

## Summary
A PhD student requested access to the big partition for a large finite-element calculation using the HyTeG framework. The request was initially sent to an individual HPC admin but should have been directed to the general support email.

## Root Cause
- User needed access to the big partition for a large computation.
- User contacted an individual HPC admin instead of the general support email.

## Solution
- HPC admins clarified that individual accounts cannot be granted access to the big partition; only entire groups (Slurm accounts) can be granted access.
- The user's account was temporarily granted access to the big partition.
- The user was instructed to send future requests to the general support email.

## General Learnings
- Always direct support requests to the general support email (support-hpc@fau.de) instead of individual admins.
- Access to the big partition is granted to entire groups, not individual accounts.
- The current node limit for the big partition is 512 nodes, which may need to be reviewed and adjusted.

## Actions Taken
- The user's account was temporarily granted access to the big partition.
- The user was informed about the correct procedure for requesting support.

## Follow-up
- Review and potentially adjust the node limit for the big partition.
- Ensure users are aware of the correct procedure for requesting support and access to resources.
---

### 2022110742003201_Re%3A%20Call%20for%20contributions%3A%20NHR%40FAU%20user%20forum%20on%20November%208.md
# Ticket 2022110742003201

 ```markdown
# HPC-Support Ticket Conversation Analysis

## Keywords
- User Forum
- Verbesserungsvorschlag
- Gruppenlimit
- AssocGrpNodeLimit
- Slurm
- squeue
- Gruppennutzung
- Knotenlimit
- NHR@FAU
- FAU
- HPC-Team
- Jobs
- Queue
- Studenten
- Zeitkritische Simulationen

## General Learnings
- **User Feedback**: Importance of user feedback for improving HPC services.
- **Group Limits**: Introduction of group limits (AssocGrpNodeLimit) on the new cluster.
- **User Requirements**: Users need a way to monitor group job usage to manage resources effectively.
- **Technical Limitations**: Slurm does not support displaying jobs by group, making it difficult to implement the user's request.
- **Communication**: Encouraging open communication between users and HPC staff to address issues and improve services.

## Root Cause of the Problem
- Users need a way to monitor the current status of group job usage to ensure fair resource allocation and prevent overuse by individual members.

## Solution
- **Technical Feasibility**: The requested feature is not technically feasible with the current Slurm setup.
- **Alternative Solutions**: Encourage users to transition to NHR projects to mitigate the impact of group limits.
- **Future Improvements**: Continue to gather user feedback and explore alternative solutions to address resource management challenges.
```

### 42088211_Turbomole%206.3%20version%20problem.md
# Ticket 42088211

 # HPC-Support Ticket: Turbomole 6.3 Version Problem

## Keywords
- Turbomole 6.3
- Executable not found
- Permissions issue
- Job submission error

## Problem Description
- User encountered an issue where the Turbomole 6.3 executable was not found when submitting a job to the queue.
- The error message indicated that the parallel program `/apps/Turbomole/TURBOMOLE6.3/TURBOMOLE/bin/em64t-unknown-linux-gnu_mpi/dscf_mpi` was not found.
- The user confirmed that the executable existed in the specified directory.

## Root Cause
- The issue was due to incorrect permissions on the executables.

## Solution
- HPC Admins fixed the permissions on the executables.
- The user was the first to use Turbomole 6.3, which likely exposed the permissions issue.

## Lessons Learned
- Always check permissions on newly installed software executables.
- Ensure that executables are accessible to all users who need to run them.
- Initial users of new software versions may encounter setup issues that need to be resolved by HPC Admins.

## Follow-up Actions
- HPC Admins should verify permissions on all executables when installing new software versions.
- Users should report any issues with new software versions promptly to allow for quick resolution.
---

### 2021110342002309_HPC%20Cluster%20Registrierung.md
# Ticket 2021110342002309

 # HPC-Support Ticket Conversation Summary

## Subject: HPC Cluster Registrierung

### Keywords:
- HPC Registration
- Jupyter Notebook
- GPU Access
- Batch Jobs
- TinyGPU
- Interactive Work vs Production Runs

### What Can Be Learned:

#### Registration Process:
- Users need to submit an application form for HPC resources.
- HPC Admin creates an HPC ID (e.g., `iwi5041h`) and provides instructions for setting up a password.
- Initial setup on HPC servers can take up to 2 days.

#### Jupyter Notebook and GPU Access:
- JupyterHub is used for interactive development work.
- For GPU access, users need to select `tinygpu` during login.
- For specialized hardware, users need to submit a job to the queue.

#### Batch Jobs:
- Example command for job submission: `qsub.tinygpu -l nodes=1:ppn=4:anygtx -I`.
- Documentation for job submission can be found [here](https://hpc.fau.de/systems-services/systems-documentation-instructions/clusters/tinygpu-cluster/).

#### Interactive Work vs Production Runs:
- **Interactive Work**: Limited resources, suitable for development and testing.
- **Production Runs**: Requires submitting a job to the queue, suitable for larger computations.

#### Additional Resources:
- HPC-Cafe: A bi-weekly event for HPC users to get support and learn about HPC resources.
- Documentation for Python and Jupyter: [Link](https://hpc.fau.de/systems-services/systems-documentation-instructions/special-applications-and-tips-tricks/python-and-jupyter/).

### Root Cause of the Problem:
- User was unable to access GPU in Jupyter Notebook.

### Solution:
- Use `tinygpu` during login to JupyterHub.
- For larger computations, submit a job to the queue using the provided command.

### Additional Notes:
- JupyterHub is limited to 6-hour jobs with one GPU.
- For larger resource needs, submit an individual job to the queue.

This summary provides a concise overview of the registration process, GPU access, batch job submission, and the difference between interactive work and production runs. It also includes links to relevant documentation and resources for further support.
---

 # HPC Support Ticket: Lammps Jobs Stalling on Alex

## Keywords
- Lammps
- Job stalling

- Race condition
- Debugger

## Problem Description
- **User Issue**: Lammps jobs on the Alex cluster are stalling and not performing any work on the GPU after some time.

- **Monitoring Link**: [Job Monitoring](https://monitoring.nhr.fau.de/monitoring/jobs/?cluster=alex&duration=900-360000&user=bccc007h&userMatch=eq)

## Troubleshooting Steps
- **User Actions**:
  - Attempted to run the script in both interactive sessions and through job submission without issues.
  - Unable to identify the cause of the stalling.
- **HPC Admin Suggestions**:
  - Possible race condition in Lammps.
  - Suggested using a debugger to attach to processes when the problem occurs.

## Root Cause
- Intermittent issue, possibly a race condition in Lammps.

## Solution
- No definitive solution found. Further investigation using a debugger is recommended.

## Next Steps
- Attach a debugger to the processes when the stalling occurs to gather more information.
- Continue monitoring the jobs for any patterns or additional clues.

## References
- [FAU HPC Support](mailto:support-hpc@fau.de)
- [FAU HPC Website](https://hpc.fau.de/)
- [Computer Chemistry Center (CCC)](https://chemistry.nat.fau.eu/ccc/)
